IE28.4.summary: Toronto is moving forward with a strategy to significantly boost the use of renewable energy and electric technologies like rooftop solar and battery storage across the city. This plan aims to accelerate climate action, reduce carbon emissions, and help residents access programs and funding for cleaner, more efficient energy.

IE28.1.summary: City Council is updating how it manages road disruptions, which affect traffic across Toronto. This involves clarifying rules and fees for street work, making it easier for community street events, and exempting eligible affordable housing projects from certain road disruption fees to help their development. The goal is to reduce congestion and support the creation of more affordable homes.

EY30.1.summary: A proposal to build a 27-storey apartment building with 420 new homes at 2775 Jane Street was recently considered by a local community council. The council did not recommend approving the necessary zoning changes for this development, which means City Council will now make the final decision on whether this significant project proceeds.

CR9.4.summary: City Council has adopted recommendations to address the rising number of evictions from Toronto Community Housing. This includes requesting TCHC to consider a temporary halt on evictions for rent arrears until a comprehensive plan is developed, and to create new pathways to rehouse and support tenants who have been evicted, with a focus on Black, Indigenous, and racialized residents. The City also declared an escalating housing crisis, recognizing the critical role of social housing for many residents.

GG27.5.summary: City officials have approved a five-year contract with ClaimsPro LP to provide insurance adjusting services for the City of Toronto. This agreement ensures the city can effectively manage its own insurance claims, helping to assess liabilities and protect public funds.

EY29.2.summary: City Council has approved planning rule changes for 2256 Lake Shore Boulevard West, allowing for a new development in the area. This approval requires the developer to provide a new public park on the site, measuring at least 556 square metres.

EY29.6.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to add the property at 2125 Lawrence Avenue West to the city's Heritage Register. This means the building is now officially recognized for its historical or architectural value, providing a layer of protection against demolition or major alterations and helping preserve local heritage.

MPB38.1.summary: Toronto City Council has formally adopted the city's 2026 Operating and Capital Budget. This decision sets out how the city will fund all its services and investments for the upcoming year, including minor adjustments to support local economic development, cultural events, and community initiatives.

CC38.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the property tax rates and overall budget implementation for 2026, setting new tax ratios for different property types. Homeowners will see a 0.7% increase in their property tax bills, while a new special levy will collect nearly $75 million dedicated to fund priority transit and housing projects across the city.

IA36.1.summary: City Council received an inquiry and a response about the issue of litter found in blue recycling bins across the city. After reviewing the information, Council decided not to take any further action on the matter at this time, meaning specific initiatives to address blue bin litter are not moving forward.

PH27.8.summary: Toronto City Council has directed city staff to review and study various parking requirements for new developments across the city. This includes examining visitor parking and accessible parking needs, especially in areas near major transit, along with loading zones and the feasibility of expanding city-owned parking lots to support growing neighborhoods.

PH27.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved an updated long-term plan for the Scarborough Centre area, known as Official Plan Amendment 871. This plan will guide how the area grows and develops in the coming years, shaping future decisions on housing, businesses, and public spaces to create a vibrant urban hub.

CC36.10.summary: City Council has approved alterations to the historic buildings at 353-355 Sherbourne Street and 157 Carlton Street. This decision allows for the construction of a new 48-storey residential tower and a 4-storey building with shops and homes, while also ensuring the heritage features of the existing property are protected through a legal agreement.

TE28.12.summary: City Council has approved zoning and planning changes for properties at 353-355 Sherbourne Street and 157 Carlton Street. This decision permits a new development on these sites, shaping what can be built and how it impacts the surrounding neighbourhood, from housing to local density.

PH27.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the planning rules for properties on Queen Street East and Eastern Avenue. This decision will allow for the development of new housing as part of the Toronto Builds program, and will also explore integrating community spaces and adjusting local permit parking.

PH27.20.summary: To protect Toronto's economy and job market, City Council reaffirmed its request to the provincial government to approve Official Plan Amendment 804, a key plan for preserving vital industrial and commercial areas. City staff will also work with businesses, labour groups, and other partners to develop a shared strategy across governments to protect these lands, boost productivity, and grow manufacturing jobs. This decision aims to secure dedicated spaces for businesses and ensure local employment opportunities.

SC28.1.summary: City Council has approved zoning changes for the properties at 1212-1238 Kingston Road, allowing for a new development in the area. The decision also requests that funds collected from the developer be used for local park improvements in Ward 20. Furthermore, this new development will not be eligible for on-street permit parking if it's introduced on nearby streets in the future.

TE28.27.summary: Toronto City Council has directed its transit office to press Metrolinx for greater transparency and mitigation efforts related to Ontario Line construction. Council is requesting Metrolinx to immediately publish its community benefits report, re-evaluate an acoustic shelter for Queen/Spadina Station, and work with the City to reduce construction impacts near Exhibition Station during the 2026 FIFA World Cup. These actions aim to ensure Metrolinx better addresses noise, local business support, and community engagement.

BU12.1.summary: Toronto's Budget Committee has made recommendations to the Mayor for the upcoming 2026 City Budgets, focusing on strategic investments and future financial planning. This includes exploring opportunities for increased community safety, advocating for youth violence prevention, and supporting a plan to boost property tax discounts for small businesses from 15% to 20%, funded by an adjustment to other commercial property taxes without impacting overall city revenue.

NY29.1.summary: After an appeal, North York Community Council allowed a homeowner at 10 Glencairn Avenue to remove a privately owned tree, despite city forestry staff initially deeming the tree healthy. To maintain Toronto's tree canopy, the resident must provide five replacement trees, either by planting them or by paying a fee in place of planting.

EX28.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved increasing the land transfer tax for luxury homes, with new, higher rates for properties valued over $3 million. This change aims to generate more revenue for the city, potentially helping to fund services like transit and housing, and reduce the city's financial shortfall, with the revised rates taking effect on April 1, 2026.

MM35.11.summary: Toronto City Council has formally expressed its support for the new Defence, Security and Resilience Bank to establish its headquarters in the city. This move is intended to strengthen Toronto's economy and create thousands of good jobs for residents. The Council will now communicate this position to federal and provincial government leaders.

MM35.12.summary: City Council sent a proposal about developer contributions for the 199 College Street development and related streetscape improvements to a local community council for further review. This means the plan to collect funds for local services and enhance the College Street area will be discussed locally before potentially returning to City Council.

MM35.17.summary: Toronto City Council has asked the TTC board to study the possibility of implementing a money-back guarantee for transit riders. If approved, customers could receive refunds for TTC delays of 15 minutes or more, which aims to improve accountability and rider experience.

IE26.3.summary: Toronto has adopted its TransformTO Net Zero Strategy Action Plan for 2026-2030, setting out how the city will work towards its ambitious climate goals. This plan directs staff to develop a detailed financial analysis and policy recommendations by 2028, outlining the estimated funding needed to reach net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2040 and shaping future city investments.

EC25.12.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new initiative to make events across the city more accessible for people with disabilities. City staff will develop an "Accessibility and Inclusion Handbook" to guide event organizers, ensuring events held on city property and elsewhere are welcoming to all residents.

EC25.13.summary: Toronto City Council recently approved a plan to explore creating a part-time fellowship program for youth living with disabilities. This initiative aims to provide valuable work experience within City Hall offices, and city staff will now prepare a report outlining how best to implement the program.

IE26.12.summary: City Council has approved accepting an in-kind donation of up to $15 million from the Chinese Cultural Centre of Greater Toronto to build a new Asian Garden at Cynthia Lai Park. This decision allows for the creation and long-term management of a significant cultural green space, enhancing local park amenities for residents.

PH26.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to planning rules for major roads (called "Avenues") in Wards 9 and 11, especially near transit stations. These updates to the city's Official Plan and zoning bylaws aim to encourage more diverse housing development and growth along these important corridors.

PH26.10.summary: City Council has approved the permanent closure of a portion of the public laneway located at 66-66Y Third Street in the Etobicoke-Lakeshore area. This decision means that part of the laneway will no longer be accessible to the public, potentially affecting local property access or pedestrian routes for nearby residents.

DM35.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a settlement for the development proposal at 847-855 Kingston Road, directing City staff to support the revised plans at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This decision also requires staff to consult with local Indigenous communities regarding the project and the nearby Glen Stewart ravine.

PH26.17.summary: City Council has approved an agreement with WoodGreen Community Housing to support 160 affordable homes for seniors at 444 Logan Avenue. This agreement includes a 40-year property tax exemption for the project, helping ensure quality housing for seniors in the community.

CC35.7.summary: Toronto City Council is preparing for a provincial law called Bill 60, the "Fighting Delays, Building Faster Act, 2025." Council decided to let its legal team join any public lawsuits challenging this bill to protect the city's interests, especially regarding housing security and preventing homelessness.

MM35.10.summary: City Council has directed city staff to study new design options for the 'Transform Yonge' project. They want to explore ways to improve public spaces and add bike lanes on Yonge Street without removing any existing car lanes, and also look into extending the plan further north to Steeles Avenue. This decision aims to find a compromise that benefits pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers, with a report expected by early 2027.

MM35.1.summary: City Council has asked city staff to explore ways to enforce the rules prohibiting e-bikes and e-scooters from being ridden on sidewalks. This action aims to improve safety for pedestrians and will also include recommendations on how to better inform the public about existing rules for these devices.

MM35.26.summary: Toronto councillors have approved a plan to advance a new West Scarborough Rail Trail, initiating a study for its design, property needs, and preliminary cost estimates. This future multi-use path would improve walking and cycling connections for residents and may be included in the city's cycling network plan for 2028-2030, pending future funding.

EC25.1.summary: City Council has approved a plan to support the North York Harvest Food Bank, recognizing a portion of its Chesswood Drive location as a key community facility. This decision exempts the food bank from paying property taxes, helping it focus more resources on providing vital social and health services to residents. The City will also explore providing a loan guarantee to help finance upgrades to the food bank's distribution facility.

IA35.1.summary: Toronto City Council has asked its Executive Committee to look into how traffic will be managed during the upcoming FIFA World Cup. This item, originally an inquiry from a Councillor, addresses a crucial issue that will affect how residents travel and get around the city during the major event.

IA35.3.summary: Toronto City Council has sent a request for more information about the city's application for funding from the Community Sport and Recreation Infrastructure Fund to its Infrastructure and Environment Committee. This means the committee will now review how the city is seeking grants to improve local sports and recreation facilities.

MM35.15.summary: To make public transit faster and more reliable, City Council has approved measures to speed up light rail transit and streetcars across Toronto. This includes immediately implementing more aggressive traffic signal priority for the Eglinton and Finch West LRTs, and developing detailed plans by early 2026 to improve streetcar speeds through signal adjustments, traffic agents, and potentially parking or turn restrictions.

RM35.7.summary: City Council formally approved the list of items they would discuss during their December 16 and 17, 2025 meetings. This process of reviewing and adopting the "Order Paper" establishes the agenda and priorities for Council's upcoming decisions.

EX28.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new rules requiring apartment buildings to provide a common cooled space, kept at or below 26 degrees Celsius, from June 1 to September 30 each year. This applies to buildings with existing amenity spaces where individual units lack air conditioning, helping tenants stay safe during heatwaves. City staff will also develop a proposal for maximum temperature limits within rental units themselves.

EX28.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan to update and improve the city's approach to extreme heat. This includes finalizing the 2026 Heat Relief Strategy, making cooling centres more accessible and better promoted, and improving the application process for hardship funds to help vulnerable residents. These changes aim to better protect Toronto residents during hot weather.

DM35.2.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to take a firm stance against lawsuits that aim to halt new housing and shelter developments. To support this, the city will offer reimbursements of up to $50,000 to consultants involved in these cases to cover their legal fees not paid by insurance, helping advance critical projects.

TTC11.9.summary: The TTC has approved exploring new strategies to speed up streetcar service across Toronto. This initiative will look at options like removing on-street parking, restricting left turns during peak hours, and reviewing stop spacing and internal speed policies to make commutes faster and more reliable for riders.

TTC11.8.summary: Toronto's transit agency has directed its CEO to quickly improve the speed and reliability of the new Line 6 (Finch West LRT) and the upcoming Line 5 (Eglinton Crosstown LRT). This aims to make these crucial new light rail lines more appealing than private vehicles, reducing traffic congestion and better serving the thousands of residents who depend on them.

IE26.14.summary: The Infrastructure and Environment Committee has referred a proposal to an executive director for consideration regarding Toronto's electricity supply. The proposal argues that the provincial electricity plan for Toronto does not align with the city's climate goals, which include phasing out gas-fired power and increasing local renewable energy to achieve net-zero emissions by 2040.

PH26.8.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a two-year extension for the lease agreements of Beech Hall Housing Co-operative and Alexandra Park Housing Co-operative. This decision allows these vital affordable housing providers to continue operating under their current terms while new, long-term agreements are finalized with the City.

EC25.4.summary: Toronto City Council adopted the Mount Dennis Community for All Action Plan, a strategy to guide development and support residents in the Mount Dennis area over the next 10 years. This plan focuses on community well-being, addressing potential displacement, and includes initiatives like a grant program for anti-displacement education.

ZB19.7.summary: The Toronto Zoo Board of Management has adopted confidential instructions for its upcoming collective bargaining negotiations with the union representing Zoo employees. These negotiations will determine future wages, benefits, and working conditions for staff, impacting the Zoo's operational costs and services.

CR8.5.summary: A city committee has recommended that the Mayor consider allocating dedicated funding in the 2026 budget for Social Development Plans across Toronto's priority neighbourhoods. These plans aim to boost safety, well-being, and community resilience, particularly benefiting Black, Indigenous, and racialized residents through local initiatives.

GG25.15.summary: To complete an important gap in the Humber River Trail, City Council has authorized continued negotiations to acquire portions of two properties on Humberview Crescent and St. Phillips Road. If negotiations are unsuccessful, the City will begin the process to acquire this private land for public use, allowing for the construction of a new multi-use path, bridges, and a boardwalk.

MM34.14.summary: Toronto City Council has formally opposed a new provincial bill (Bill 60) that could limit the city's authority over local road design. Council is asking the Province to confirm that Toronto retains the power to make decisions on road layouts, such as bike lanes and traffic flow, to best serve residents' interests and improve safety.

CC34.4.summary: City Council has approved a settlement regarding a development project at 2 Tippett Road, directing staff to support this agreement at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This decision sets the conditions for the development to proceed, requiring the owner to finalize design plans and address various engineering and environmental concerns before construction can begin.

MM34.4.summary: To enhance safety in public spaces, City Council updated its rules for managing homeless encampments. The new policy prioritizes removing encampments within 200 metres of schools, daycares, or playgrounds, aiming for removal within 24 hours of being reported. It also limits shelter offers to three before proceeding with removal in these specific areas, intending to ensure these spaces are safe for children and families.

MM34.11.summary: City Council has approved a request to the province for a special zoning order that would limit building heights at 1875 Steeles Avenue West. This decision aims to protect the operations of Sanofi Pasteur's biomanufacturing facilities and headquarters nearby, ensuring a major local employer can continue to operate effectively.

MM34.13.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to challenge a development approval for 712 Eglinton Avenue East. The City's legal team will attend the Toronto Local Appeal Body to oppose the proposed development, seeking to prevent or change it. This ensures the city's position on local development is heard and considered.

PH25.3.summary: Toronto City Council has updated zoning rules to allow more local retail shops and services on major streets within residential neighbourhoods. This change aims to make daily errands more convenient for residents and create more complete communities.

EX27.3.summary: To facilitate the construction of the Metrolinx Ontario Line's Cosburn Station, City Council approved multi-year temporary closures of a local laneway, parts of Pape Avenue's roadway and sidewalk, and changes to parking rules in the area. These significant disruptions will affect traffic, pedestrians, and parking along Pape Avenue from early 2026 until the end of 2030.

IE25.8.summary: Toronto City Council has asked the Province of Ontario to provide funding for critical road safety initiatives. This includes $35.1 million to maintain crossing guards and traffic safety police, and $210 million for road safety infrastructure projects like traffic calming and lights, ensuring these vital programs continue.

MM34.22.summary: City Council has requested updates from the Province of Ontario and Metrolinx on plans to extend the Finch West Light Rail Transit line further west to Woodbine and Pearson International Airport. This decision aims to ensure that the extension aligns with City Council's vision and that necessary planning is underway, including ensuring emergency access for police services along the route.

MM34.17.summary: To find efficiencies and reduce budget pressures, City Council has restructured the Toronto Parking Authority's Board to be made up entirely of senior City officials. The City Manager will now conduct a full review of the Authority's operations, finances, and staffing to explore potentially integrating it more directly into the City or another agency. This aims to optimize how parking services are run and contribute to the 2026 city budget.

EX27.13.summary: City Council approved a five-year agreement making Coca-Cola Canada the exclusive soft drink provider for Exhibition Place, including the Enercare Centre and Automotive Building. This means visitors to these major Toronto venues will primarily find Coca-Cola products. Council also requested Exhibition Place to continue pursuing enhanced sustainability practices across all its food and beverage operations.

EY26.21.summary: City Council has approved naming the north west baseball diamond at Martingrove Gardens Park the “Jim Horton Baseball Diamond”. This decision honors Jim Horton, a dedicated long-time volunteer coach recognized for his significant contributions to Martingrove Baseball since 1987.

SC26.13.summary: To improve safety and traffic flow, Toronto has approved installing new traffic lights at the intersection of Clonmore Drive and Queensbury Avenue/Private Access. This decision will also remove the existing pedestrian crossing nearby, integrating pedestrian safety into the new signalized intersection for residents in Scarborough Southwest.

SC26.14.summary: Toronto City Council has approved installing new traffic lights at Birchmount Road and Rolark Drive, also banning right turns on a red light at this intersection. These changes aim to improve safety and traffic flow for drivers and pedestrians in the area, alongside removing a specific southbound right-turn lane on Birchmount Road.

CC34.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new labour agreement with CUPE Local 2998, a union representing some city employees. This decision means changes to employee pay and benefits, which will affect the city's operating budget for 2025 and beyond.

EX27.1.summary: Toronto City Council has formally opposed recent provincial legislation (Bill 60) that aims to weaken protections for renters, including security of tenure and rent control. The city is requesting Ontario reinstate rent control for newer buildings and provide $596 million to help build more rental housing, while also exploring legal challenges to the new provincial rules.

HL28.5.summary: Toronto City Council has formally expressed its opposition to the provincial Bill 60, also known as the Fighting Delays, Building Faster Act. The City believes that proposed changes to tenant security and rent control within the bill could undermine stable housing, which is crucial for residents' overall health and well-being.

EC24.10.summary: Toronto City Council is concerned about how the provincial Bill 60 might impact housing stability and homelessness. Council has asked the Association of Municipalities of Ontario to organize a province-wide research effort to forecast the potential increase in demand for shelter and housing services due to this legislation. This action aims to help the city better understand and prepare for future challenges related to housing insecurity.

EX27.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to its winter maintenance program, which include significantly increasing fines for parking on or blocking streetcar tracks during snowstorms. The city will also work on improving public access to real-time snow clearing updates and modernizing its PlowTO tracking map, while exploring requirements for corporate property owners to clear their sidewalks.

RM34.1.summary: City Council formally opened its November 2025 meeting, marking the beginning of its deliberations. During the opening, members observed moments of silence for several individuals and delivered a land acknowledgement.

EX27.6.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a long-term lease agreement for the Weston Lions Arena with the Youth Centre for Sports Development (MLSE LaunchPad), allowing the organization to redevelop and operate the facility. This decision aims to revitalize the arena with new design and programming, particularly for youth sports, while ensuring a smooth transition for current users like the Weston Lions Club and exploring options for additional ice facilities in the area.

EX27.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan for the new St. Clair-Old Weston Station to be served by the Union-Pearson Express (UPX) line, potentially offering faster transit for nearby residents. This approval is conditional on the Province fully covering all capital costs for this change and expanding the One Fare program to include UPX service, ensuring more affordable and integrated fares.

EY26.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new development rules for the property at 1138 Islington Avenue, paving the way for a new project. The developer is required to ensure and pay for any necessary upgrades to local city services, such as water and drainage, that are needed for the development.

EY26.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the city's long-term development plan and zoning rules for the land at 3418 Lake Shore Boulevard West and 0 Skeens Lane. This decision paves the way for new development or significant alterations to how this property can be used, impacting the surrounding neighbourhood.

EY26.5.summary: City Council approved a zoning change for 5280 Dundas Street West, allowing for new development at this location. This decision also sets the fees developers will pay if they choose not to provide the required bicycle parking spaces on-site, with these funds potentially directed towards other cycling infrastructure.

TE26.14.summary: Following the implementation of transit priority lanes (RapidTO) on Dufferin and Bathurst Streets, City Council is directing city staff to address community concerns. This includes developing solutions for traffic calming on local roads, making changes to on-street permit parking, and working with religious institutions to accommodate funeral processions. These actions aim to mitigate the impact of the transit changes on local residents and businesses.

AU10.1.summary: Toronto's City Council approved the Auditor General's 2026 work plan, outlining planned investigations into city operations. Council also asked the Auditor General to consider adding reviews of the SmartTrack project and TTC Subway Maintenance to the 2026 schedule. This ensures independent oversight of major city services, helping to improve efficiency and accountability for residents.

EC24.8.summary: Toronto City Council has approved plans for the new long-term care home at 4610 Finch Avenue East to prioritize specialized care for Black, Caribbean, and African diaspora seniors. This initiative aims to improve access and outcomes for these communities, and staff will also explore how the project can benefit the local Brimley Forest neighbourhood.

PH25.4.summary: Toronto has requested significant funding from federal and provincial governments to advance its housing plans. These funds are earmarked to create thousands of new affordable and supportive homes, and to protect existing rental units for residents across the city.

keyAmounts.label: Federal funding for new rental homes

EX27.5.summary: City Council received an informational report detailing the ongoing progress of the Toronto Ferry Service. The update covers the continued fabrication of new electric ferries, improvements to operational maintenance and safety, and plans to enhance the customer experience at the Jack Layton Ferry Terminal. These efforts ensure a reliable and modern ferry service for residents and visitors traveling to the Toronto Islands.

IE25.1.summary: City Council has adopted a 10-year 'Circular Economy Road Map' to help Toronto reduce waste, reuse resources, and become more sustainable. This plan allows city staff to partner with businesses, non-profits, and other governments to implement initiatives and secure funding to achieve these environmental goals.

GG25.8.summary: Toronto City Council has extended and significantly increased a contract with OpenText Corporation for enterprise document and records management services. This ensures the city can continue to efficiently store, organize, and access its vast amount of electronic information, which is crucial for city operations and public transparency.

GG25.9.summary: A city committee has approved adding over $1.6 million to an existing contract with AECOM CANADA ULC for engineering services on the Dufferin Staff Facility Project. This extra funding is needed to revise designs, meet updated city guidelines, and prepare for construction, bringing the total cost for these services to over $3.6 million.

TE26.15.summary: The Toronto and East York Community Council denied a resident's request to remove a large, healthy red oak tree from their private property at 171 Neville Park Boulevard. This decision upholds the city's Tree By-laws, which aim to protect mature and maintainable trees, reflecting the council's commitment to preserving Toronto's urban forest.

EY26.25.summary: Etobicoke York Community Council recently decided not to pause the installation of speed humps on Strathdee Drive. This means the city will proceed with adding traffic calming measures to the street, despite a new petition from some residents asking for a delay due to withdrawn support for the project.

PA16.6.summary: The Toronto Parking Authority received an update on its ongoing legal disputes, which involve issues like supplier disagreements, personal injury claims, and property tenancy matters related to its parking operations, tenanted properties, and Bike Share Toronto. The Board decided to keep the details of these specific disputes confidential, as they involve sensitive legal advice and potential litigation. This regular update helps the Authority manage legal risks in its operations.

CC33.25.summary: City Council has approved new colour-coded signs for apartment buildings under the RentSafeTO program. Starting June 15, 2026, building owners must display these signs, which will reflect how well the building meets City bylaws and maintenance standards, providing clearer information for current and future tenants. Owners will also be required to inform tenants about their building's rating and include contact information for reporting issues.

CC33.1.summary: To enhance safety and support riders, City Council approved expanding the Toronto Community Crisis Response Service to the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) for a pilot program lasting through 2026. This initiative will provide non-police mental health and crisis support on transit, while also requesting reports on a police deployment strategy for TTC stations.

MM33.4.summary: City Council has approved a plan to reduce Toronto's reliance on external consultants. This initiative aims to improve city services by having departments prioritize using internal staff for consulting work, potentially leading to lower costs and increased accountability. The city will also look into bringing contracted services in-house and building up its own expertise.

MM33.22.summary: City Council has asked the Board of Health to plan for a pilot program that offers mobile eye testing to school children in high-needs areas by early 2026. This initiative aims to ensure all students have access to vision care, helping them to perform better in school.

MM33.25.summary: Toronto City Council has directed its legal team to participate in an appeal hearing regarding a proposed development at 180 Queens Drive. The City's lawyers will advocate against the project, supporting a prior decision that refused the development and aiming to preserve the neighbourhood's existing character.

MM33.31.summary: City Council has directed its legal team to appeal a previously approved development at 226 Olive Avenue, aiming to stop or modify the project. This decision means the city will officially oppose the proposed development before the Toronto Local Appeal Body, signaling support for residents' concerns in Willowdale.

IE24.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved several measures to enhance micromobility safety and public awareness throughout the city. This includes ongoing public education campaigns and enforcement blitzes with police, exploring ways to require vendors to inform customers about device rules, and considering a licensing system for motorized micromobility devices. These actions aim to make city streets and parks safer for all residents by improving compliance with current rules.

IE24.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved an expansion of the free-floating car-share program. This means car-share vehicles will now be allowed to operate and park in more city areas, specifically those that do not have resident permit parking. This change aims to make car-sharing more widely available and convenient for residents across Toronto.

IE24.10.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to gradually increase the amount of tree maintenance work performed by city staff, aiming for an even split between in-house work and services provided by outside contractors. This phased approach will change how the city's trees are cared for and managed.

TE25.41.summary: To improve the speed and reliability of the 7 Bathurst bus, City Council has approved new traffic rules along Bathurst Street. These changes include creating no-stopping zones on the east side during peak hours and restricting left turns at Davenport Road and Dupont Street. City staff will monitor these measures to ensure they deliver better transit service for residents.

CC33.22.summary: City Council has rejected a proposal to build a new structure adjacent to 2 Whitney Avenue, located within the North Rosedale Heritage Conservation District. This decision upholds the historic character of the neighbourhood, aligning with protections under the Ontario Heritage Act.

MM33.26.summary: Toronto City Council has asked its Parks and Recreation department to research how other cities manage noise from pickleball courts. This review will explore best practices for reducing noise, including court placement, sound barriers, and different court surfaces, to address community concerns about temporary and seasonal facilities.

MM33.28.summary: Toronto is launching a citywide public education campaign in 2026 and 2027 to address intimate partner and gender-based violence. This initiative will highlight support services for survivors and promote prevention efforts, helping residents access crucial resources and learn how to combat violence in the community.

MM33.34.summary: City Council has approved plans to develop a new "Legacy of Learning" program, which aims to provide free weekday field trips to the Toronto Zoo for students in Toronto schools. This initiative will specifically focus on ensuring equitable access for all students, especially those in underserved communities, and will honour the legacies of renowned scientists Dr. Jane Goodall and Dr. Anne Innis Dagg.

SC25.9.summary: Toronto City Council has approved installing new traffic lights at Milner Avenue and Executive Court/Mid-Dominion Acres in Scarborough North. This decision aims to improve pedestrian safety by providing a controlled crossing, and will also restrict crossings in nearby sections of Milner Avenue to guide people to the new lights.

PH24.5.summary: Toronto City Council recently refused an application to allow a large, third-party electronic roof sign at 2 College Street. This decision means the proposed digital billboard will not be installed, helping to preserve the current visual character of the neighbourhood.

IE24.13.summary: City Council adopted new measures to make Toronto's speed camera program fairer and more effective at stopping speeding. This includes giving drivers a 7-day grace period between tickets from the same camera, installing larger warning signs, and piloting high-visibility wraps on cameras to make them more noticeable. The city will also inform residents and provincial politicians about local speed camera data and road safety to highlight the program's importance.

MM33.2.summary: City Council approved a review of Toronto's speed camera program to improve road safety and fairness for drivers. City staff will report on the program's impact on reducing collisions, ticketing practices, and ways to ensure clearer signage and a reasonable speed threshold before tickets are issued. This decision aims to make speed camera enforcement more transparent and effective in protecting our communities.

EX26.12.summary: City Council has reviewed the Toronto Parking Authority's 2024 report on its environmental, social, and governance performance. This report provides residents with an overview of how the city-owned parking authority is addressing sustainability, social responsibility, and ethical practices in its operations.

TTC9.4.summary: The Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) has decided to publicly release a report from an external review of its subway and streetcar maintenance programs and assets. This report, which will be redacted to remove sensitive business information, aims to improve how the TTC manages its critical transit infrastructure and provides transparency to residents.

TTC9.9.summary: The TTC Board has approved an additional payment for legal fees incurred by its Chair, Jamaal Myers. This covers expenses from an Integrity Commissioner investigation into a confidential information leak, where Chair Myers was found to have made a minor technical breach of conduct but faced no penalty.

GG24.13.summary: A City committee has indefinitely postponed a decision on a proposed agreement that would allow FoodShare Toronto to continue and expand a community garden in the East Flemingdon Park Hydro Corridor. This deferral means the urban farm, which serves the Flemingdon Park neighbourhood, cannot move forward with its expansion plans for the time being.

NY26.10.summary: North York Community Council has approved a homeowner's request to remove one privately owned tree at 258 Glengrove Avenue West, despite a previous denial by City forestry staff. This decision requires the homeowner to replace the tree by either planting five new trees on their property or by paying a fee to the City.

EY25.24.summary: To help build more housing, Toronto City Council approved waiving certain development fees for properties at 87 Ethel Avenue and 2595 St. Clair Avenue West. This decision aims to expedite the creation of nearly 2,000 new homes, including 141 affordable units, in Ward 5.

CA29.1.summary: City Council has appointed three new public members – Jehad Aliweiwi, Elizabeth McCallion, and Jean Olemou – to the Toronto Atmospheric Fund Board. These individuals will serve until October 2029, helping to guide the fund's efforts to reduce carbon emissions and improve air quality across Toronto.

PH23.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the city's land use rules for six properties across Toronto. This decision allows the city to move forward with its strategy to build or expand facilities that provide services for people experiencing homelessness at these locations.

MM32.6.summary: To improve transit speed and reliability in Ward 11, the City has asked two departments to propose changes. The Toronto Parking Authority will look at adjusting parking rates on busy transit routes, while Transportation Services will suggest traffic changes like parking, loading, and turn restrictions, particularly for the afternoon northbound Bathurst bus.

MM32.17.summary: A proposal to review how residents appeal tickets issued by automated speed cameras was sent to the Infrastructure and Environment Committee for future discussion. This means City Council did not make a final decision on changing the appeals process at this meeting, and it will be debated by the committee first.

MM32.23.summary: To boost transparency for renters, Toronto City Council approved a new colour-coded rating system for apartment buildings. This system will rate buildings based on their maintenance and compliance, requiring owners to display a visible sign near the entrance and on the RentSafeTO online map, helping residents make informed housing choices.

MM32.42.summary: City Council decided not to send the City's lawyer to represent its interests at an appeal regarding a planning application at 42 Donalda Crescent. This means the City will not officially argue its position at the Toronto Local Appeal Body, which reviews local planning decisions that affect neighbourhoods.

MM32.46.summary: City Council has approved a plan to further develop the new Indigenous Women and Children's Shelter at 68 Sheppard Avenue West. This includes exploring acquiring adjacent land to expand the site and assessing the optimal size and operations to ensure long-term sustainability and effective support for residents.

EX25.1.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted a new "Toronto Builds" framework aimed at significantly speeding up housing construction across the city. This plan involves creating detailed workplans, streamlining development processes, and prioritizing building permits to make it faster and easier to deliver new housing projects. The goal is to boost housing supply by improving coordination among city agencies and fast-tracking approvals for housing developments.

PH23.5.summary: To address the housing shortage, City Council has adopted a strategy to create more homes across Toronto, specifically by authorizing capital funding and incentives for new affordable rental developments. While certain financial details remain confidential to protect ongoing negotiations with federal and provincial partners, this decision aims to increase housing options for residents.

PH23.6.summary: To boost housing options, City Council approved a plan to help residents build multiplexes and mid-rise homes more easily across Toronto. The City will develop and make publicly available pre-approved designs for four- and six-unit homes by mid-2026, simplifying the construction process for property owners.

MM32.5.summary: To encourage more housing options, City Council removed certain fees for building additional homes on single lots. This means that the second to sixth residential units (and some laneway/garden suites) will no longer pay development charges or parkland fees, making it cheaper to create duplexes, triplexes, and other multi-unit homes in Toronto neighbourhoods.

PH23.1.summary: City Council has approved changes to Toronto's planning and zoning rules to expand options for building garden suites in residential neighbourhoods. These updates specify the required distances between a main house and a garden suite on the same property, aiming to clarify how residents can add these additional living spaces.

CC32.11.summary: Toronto City Council adopted its legal strategy for an upcoming hearing at the Ontario Land Tribunal regarding rules for new housing on major streets. The city will oppose certain proposed changes to these planning rules, aiming to ensure that new housing development across Toronto's neighbourhoods aligns with the city's vision.

SC24.1.summary: City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for the property at 4610 Finch Avenue East. This decision allows for a specific development project to move forward, which could change the types of buildings or how the land is used in this Scarborough neighbourhood.

EX25.5.summary: To improve winter maintenance services, City Council approved changes that will allow residents to report snow removal issues immediately during a snowfall. The City will also develop a detailed plan to handle major snow events, including determining equipment needs and responding to specific neighbourhood deficiencies.

EX25.14.summary: Toronto City Council is urging the federal government to enhance and expand programs that help low-income families afford home internet, and to update eligibility thresholds for these subsidies. Additionally, the city will explore setting up public kiosks that offer free Wi-Fi, 911 calling, and access to city services, aiming to improve digital access for all residents.

EC22.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved its Early Years and Child Care Service Plan for 2025-2030, outlining how the city will manage and grow its childcare system over the next five years. This plan prioritizes equitable access to services, including a commitment to co-develop an Indigenous-led plan, and urges provincial and federal governments for increased funding, expanded age coverage, and better wages for childcare workers.

EC22.4.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted an updated plan for managing coyotes in the city. This strategy aims to improve public education on how to coexist with coyotes, include wildlife considerations in new development projects, and seeks financial support from the Ontario government for urban wildlife management.

CC32.20.summary: Council has decided to keep legal advice confidential regarding an ongoing land dispute at the Ontario Land Tribunal for properties on Lake Promenade and Park Boulevard. This protects the City's strategy as it addresses a potential development and a settlement offer for the Etobicoke-Lakeshore neighbourhood.

EX25.6.summary: To help local businesses counter US tariffs and protect jobs, Toronto City Council adopted a plan to boost the city's manufacturing and industrial sectors. This plan directs staff to develop a job growth strategy and to explore increasing financial incentives, potentially offering grants covering up to 100% of new property tax growth for businesses that create jobs, for a limited time until 2027.

GG23.2.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to allow IPAC Paving Inc. and Pave-1 Construction Limited to bid on city contracts again, effective immediately. These two paving companies were previously suspended and can now compete for city work on roads and other infrastructure projects.

IE23.9.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new measures to combat "greenwashing" – misleading claims about environmental benefits – in advertising displayed on city property. This means advertisers will need to sign a declaration, and the city will provide details on its website about how residents can report potentially false environmental claims to federal regulators. The goal is to ensure ads are truthful and promote transparency on climate action.

MM32.21.summary: Toronto City Council has approved measures to better protect the city's valuable tree canopy. This involves exploring new tools to safeguard trees, clarifying how city tree rules interact with provincial building permits, and seeking to make tree by-law violation outcomes publicly available. These actions aim to preserve more of Toronto's green spaces and enhance environmental health for residents.

MM32.44.summary: City Council decided not to send the city's lawyers to a provincial hearing about a minor zoning change for the property at 250 Wincott Drive. This means the City of Toronto will not officially present its position on the requested building alterations at this hearing, which could affect local development.

MM32.45.summary: Toronto City Council approved accepting a $150,000 donation from the Scarborough Cricket Association to build new cricket facilities at Milliken Park. This investment will create a cricket pitch and/or batting cages, enhancing recreational options for residents in Scarborough North at no direct cost to taxpayers, pending community consultation results.

NY25.6.summary: City Council has approved changes to planning rules for properties along Yonge Street and Alexandra Boulevard, paving the way for a new development. This approval requires the developer to provide 205 square metres of public parkland on-site to expand Snider Parkette, and also pay funds for additional parkland not provided directly on the property.

EC22.6.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted new licensing regulations for animal-related businesses like pet shops, kennels, and groomers. These changes require separate licenses for each establishment and specific endorsements for services like breeding or boarding, aiming to improve animal welfare and safety standards across the city.

RM32.7.summary: Toronto City Council officially approved its agenda, known as the 'Order Paper,' for its meetings on July 23 and 24, 2025. This action finalized the specific items and key matters that councillors debated and made decisions on during these sessions.

PH23.14.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to review its Foundation Drainage Policy, which impacts how new buildings manage water drainage. City staff will consult with housing developers and other experts to understand the policy's costs and risks, aiming to balance the need for new homes with the policy's environmental objectives. A report with potential amendments and criteria for exemptions is expected in early 2026, alongside a look into revising manganese limits for storm sewer discharge.

EX25.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new dedicated transit lanes on major sections of Dufferin Street and Bathurst Street, with some lanes also open to bicycles. This decision aims to improve the speed and reliability of TTC services, while also altering traffic flow for drivers on these busy corridors.

AU9.9.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted a series of recommendations to improve how the city bids for, plans, and governs future large-scale events like the FIFA World Cup. These changes aim to ensure better oversight, transparency, and risk management, making sure councillors are well-informed about project changes and potential financial impacts.

RM32.1.summary: Toronto City Council formally convened its meeting, with the Speaker calling members to order. The session began with O Canada, an acknowledgement of Indigenous lands, and moments of silence in memory of several individuals. This procedural item marked the official start of the Council's deliberations.

CA28.1.summary: Toronto City Council has appointed Ben Liu as a new public member to the Toronto Public Library Board. He brings extensive experience in higher education administration and community board leadership, which will help guide the city's library system until November 2026.

TA11.7.summary: The Toronto Atmospheric Fund approved over $260,000 in new grants for projects that will advance renewable energy and electric vehicle adoption in the city. This funding will support research into new energy models and advocacy for policies that make it easier for Toronto residents and businesses to embrace cleaner energy, helping the city meet its climate goals.

TTC7.7.summary: After an investigation into the unauthorized release of confidential information from a 2023 meeting, the TTC Board decided not to issue a penalty to Chair Myers. Instead, the Board is asking staff to develop new guidelines on when and with whom confidential information can be shared, aiming to prevent future leaks and ensure appropriate conduct.

TTC7.10.summary: The TTC Board has asked its staff to explore new ways to honour and remember Canada's veterans, especially those who have worked for the transit system. This initiative aims to highlight their historical contributions in TTC vehicles and stations, ensuring the public is more aware of their service to Canada and Toronto.

TTC7.11.summary: Toronto's transit commission has decided to honour Frederick Langdon Hubbard, the first Black Canadian to chair the TTC Board. Staff will identify TTC properties to be named after him by June 2026, marking the 100th anniversary of his historic 1930 appointment, and an annual leadership award will also be created in his name.

PA15.11.summary: The Toronto Parking Authority Board has approved moving forward with selecting a company to negotiate a contract for new parking access and revenue control systems. This decision will modernize the hardware and software used in city parking lots, affecting how residents pay for and access these facilities.

GG23.24.summary: The city is exploring the possibility of leasing a former fire hall at 641 Eglinton Avenue West to Toronto Fire Fighters Charities Inc. for a 'Home Away Program' assisting burn victims and their families. This update reports that the project is feasible but requires substantial renovation and funding, with some urgent repairs already planned.

IE23.6.summary: City Council approved several updates to Toronto's cycling network, including changing bikeway types on streets like Blue Jays Way, Peter Street, and Davenport Road. These adjustments, along with the installation of a new traffic signal on Finch Avenue East, aim to improve safety and traffic flow for cyclists and drivers on these routes.

IE23.3.summary: Toronto's Infrastructure and Environment Committee has asked senior city staff to conduct a thorough review of future waste collection services for District 2. Staff will explore the practicality of having city employees take over waste collection services after the current private contract ends in 2031, as well as alternative options for facilities and operations. This work aims to ensure future services maintain high standards for residents, improve waste diversion, and control costs.

SC24.4.summary: Scarborough Community Council approved the renaming of Rosebank Park to Cynthia Lai Park. This decision honours the late Councillor Cynthia Lai, who represented Scarborough North, recognizing her public service and lasting impact on the community.

HL26.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the continued operation of the Downtown Community Outreach Response and Engagement (CORE) program. This initiative allows the Medical Officer of Health to work with the Toronto Police Service and other agencies to provide coordinated outreach and support services in the downtown area.

NY25.8.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to oppose the current development proposal for 1410 Eglinton Avenue West at an upcoming Ontario Land Tribunal hearing. This action allows the city to negotiate with the developer to resolve outstanding issues and ensure any approved project includes necessary infrastructure upgrades to protect local services.

IE22.6.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the temporary closure of the eastbound Gardiner Expressway and northbound Don Valley Parkway on Sunday, July 26, 2026, from 2:00 a.m. to 12:00 p.m., for the 2026 Toronto Triathlon Festival. To manage expected traffic, the city will deploy extra traffic agents and prohibit other event-related road closures in a wide area that day.

IE22.7.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the full closure of the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway on Sunday, May 31, 2026, from 2:00 a.m. to 4:00 p.m. for the annual Bike for Brain Health event. This decision means residents should expect significant traffic impacts, but to help alleviate congestion, other major road closures will be prohibited that day, and the TTC is requested to avoid subway disruptions.

EX24.3.summary: City Council has approved funding to expand Toronto's student nutrition programs, creating a "Universal Morning Meal Program" to provide more students with nutritious food. This initiative aims to accelerate the rollout of meals to students in schools. The city also requested increased and inflation-adjusted funding from provincial and federal governments to support this vital program.

MM31.1.summary: The city has decided to authorize its City Solicitor to attend the Toronto Local Appeal Body to defend previous planning decisions regarding the property at 36 Romeo Street. This means the City will uphold the Committee of Adjustment's rulings for this address, and the solicitor has the discretion to negotiate a settlement or hire outside experts if needed.

MM31.9.summary: City Council has approved waiving over $115,000 in Toronto Parking Authority fees associated with removing three paid parking spots on Bond Street. This decision supports the St. Michael's Hospital Redevelopment project by reducing costs for this critical infrastructure work.

MM31.10.summary: Toronto City Council opted to send a proposal about public consultations for a new Etobicoke Centre shelter to the Economic and Community Development Committee for more review. This means the community's opportunity to provide input on the potential shelter will be delayed while the committee considers the matter.

MM31.14.summary: City Council has officially congratulated the Cabbagetown Group Softball League on its 50th anniversary. This decision celebrates a significant milestone for a local sports league that has fostered community spirit and recreation for half a century in Toronto.

DM31.1.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to improve how it consults with residents about new homeless shelters. The city will now hold individual public meetings for each planned shelter location, working closely with local councillors to determine the best format and timing. This also means restarting the community engagement process for a proposed shelter at 1220 Wilson Avenue, ensuring local input and language needs are met before any construction begins.

EX24.4.summary: City officials have approved a new strategy to build more housing by combining new homes with existing or planned Parks and Recreation facilities. This initiative aims to make better use of city-owned land to help address Toronto's housing needs and will be integrated into the five-year plan for parks and recreation spaces.

IE22.1.summary: City Council has decided to oversee a pilot program testing automated vehicles for delivering goods in Toronto, with a report on the findings and lessons learned due by late 2026. Council is also urgently seeking advice from federal and provincial privacy commissioners regarding a company's practice of recording people's faces on public streets without consent and sending those images to the United States. This action aims to address privacy concerns and ensure residents' rights are protected as new vehicle technologies are explored in the city.

EX24.2.summary: Toronto City Council is requesting the provincial government to compensate the city for an estimated $1.9 billion cash flow reduction over the next decade, caused by Bill 17's changes to development charge payments. The city also decided to temporarily stop increasing local development charges in 2025 and 2026, as it seeks more control over funding new infrastructure and setting affordable housing requirements.

IE22.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new city-wide plan for managing parking. A key decision prevents the city from introducing new fees for parking at currently free city-owned lots, such as those at parks, community centres, and libraries. The plan also aims to improve parking signage, make visitor permits more convenient, and add more accessible parking spaces.

SC23.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for properties located at 2823, 2825, 2827, and 2829 Eglinton Avenue East. This decision allows for new development at these specific addresses, impacting the type and scale of buildings that can be constructed in this Scarborough Southwest neighbourhood.

PH22.5.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new zoning rules aimed at increasing housing options across the city. This decision makes it easier to build small apartment buildings in residential neighbourhoods, previously limited to single-family homes, helping to address the housing shortage.

PH22.8.summary: Toronto City Council adopted the next phase of its plan to review policies for building more housing along the city's "avenues" – major streets designated for growth. This work plan includes public consultations and outlines how the city will study potential changes to encourage more housing options, including adding Ward 2 (Etobicoke Centre) to a later stage of the review.

EY23.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the planning rules for the property located at 3406-3434 Weston Road. This decision allows for new development on the site, potentially altering the neighbourhood's character by permitting different types of buildings or uses.

MM31.21.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a series of measures to improve the city's response to extreme heat. This involves allocating funds for water bottle distribution, ensuring outdoor pools are properly staffed, planning for more 24/7 cooling centres and water trucks, and developing a program with the Red Cross to check on vulnerable residents during heat waves. These steps aim to better protect residents, especially those most vulnerable, during extreme weather events.

DM31.2.summary: To boost Toronto's thriving film and television industry, City Council has asked staff to explore finding a closed Toronto District School Board school. This location would be dedicated to film and TV production, with staff reporting back by late 2025 with options for securing such a site.

EY23.23.summary: To allow for a construction staging area at 3755 Bloor Street West, Toronto City Council has approved temporary closures of a sidewalk, a right-turn lane, and a bike lane on Kipling Avenue. These closures, which will last from June 30 to December 31, 2025, will impact pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers, but the City is requiring the developer to provide a safe, accessible pedestrian walkway and ensure traffic safety measures are in place.

EY23.16.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the installation of new traffic lights at the intersection of Torbarrie Road and Judy Sgro Avenue to improve safety and traffic flow. This decision means residents in the Humber River - Black Creek area will get traffic signals, but not a separate pedestrian crossover.

MM31.18.summary: To alleviate downtown traffic congestion, Toronto City Council approved temporarily reopening King Street between Spadina Avenue and Church Street to vehicular traffic while streetcars are not in service. Council also requested a report by April 2026 outlining better ways to engage the public and mitigate traffic impacts before future major construction projects begin, aiming to reduce disruptions for residents.

MM31.17.summary: To help manage traffic during ongoing streetcar track construction at King and Church Streets, City Council approved temporary changes to parking and traffic rules on nearby Adelaide, King, and York Streets. An additional $350,000 will also be spent to hire more traffic officers, aiming to keep traffic moving and speed up streetcar service in the area.

PH22.3.summary: To increase housing options across Toronto, City Council has approved changes to zoning and city rules that make it easier to build multiplexes like duplexes and triplexes in more neighbourhoods. The city will also closely monitor the impact and effectiveness of these new rules until the end of 2027.

PH22.4.summary: City Council has approved changes to city planning rules to allow for low-rise buildings with up to six units, known as sixplexes, in residential neighbourhoods. This decision aims to increase the supply of housing options, initially focusing on the Toronto and East York district and a specific area in Scarborough. The city will also ensure infrastructure and services are in place to support these new housing types.

RM31.1.summary: Toronto City Council officially began its meeting on June 25 and 26, 2025. Council observed a moment of silence for several community members and acknowledged the traditional territory of Indigenous Peoples before commencing their work on city matters.

TTC6.3.summary: To boost subway safety and prevent service disruptions from track intrusions, the Toronto Transit Commission has approved plans to integrate platform safety enhancements. This means future new stations will require Platform Edge Doors, and a pilot installation for an existing station is being considered for the 2026 budget, alongside ongoing evaluation of other protective technologies.

SC23.6.summary: Scarborough Community Council approved a resident's request to remove a private black walnut tree at 49 Bellamy Road South, even though the city's forestry division found it to be healthy. This decision requires the resident to replace it with five new trees, either by planting them or making a payment to the city.

SC23.4.summary: City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for 62 Orchard Park Drive, which will allow for a new development on the property. To address community concerns, the city will also work with local residents to improve parking and traffic safety on nearby streets, exploring options like speed humps, stop signs, and traffic signals.

SC23.8.summary: To improve road safety and slow traffic, the Scarborough Community Council has authorized installing eight new speed humps on Amberjack Boulevard, specifically between Bellamy Road North and Brimorton Drive. This action will make the street safer for local residents and pedestrians.

TTC5.1.summary: The TTC Board has approved the appointment of a new Chief Executive Officer to lead Toronto's public transit system. This decision ensures ongoing leadership for the TTC, impacting the future direction and management of transit services for residents.

TE23.55.summary: Toronto's East York Community Council approved a request to allow a lemonade stand on the public sidewalk next to 224 Hallam Street. The property owner will enter an agreement with the city, taking responsibility for the stand's maintenance and removal if needed, and will pay a fee for the use of public space. This decision sets conditions for small businesses to operate on public property while ensuring public safety and access.

TE23.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved amendments to the city's Official Plan and Zoning By-law for properties located at 604-610 Bay Street and 130 Elizabeth Street. These changes will permit new development in the area, which could transform the urban landscape and land use at these downtown sites.

NY24.1.summary: The North York Community Council has postponed its decision on a homeowner's request at 499 Briar Hill Avenue for an exemption from city fence rules. The property owner seeks to keep a gate and enclosure that do not meet city safety standards for fences and decks, having previously received a violation notice.

EY23.19.summary: The Etobicoke York Community Council has approved installing new speed humps on several streets within the Sunnylea and The Kingsway neighbourhoods. This decision aims to slow down traffic and enhance safety for residents and pedestrians on Ashton Manor, Edgemore Drive, Elsfield Road, Fieldway Road, Grenview Boulevard South, Humbervale Boulevard, and Meadowvale Drive.

HL25.3.summary: The Board of Health approved over $2.3 million in grants through the Toronto Urban Health Fund to support community-based health projects for 2025. This funding will go towards 22 initiatives focusing on critical public health areas like HIV prevention, harm reduction, and child and youth resiliency across Toronto.

CC30.5.summary: City Council has updated rules about activities near "social infrastructure" like schools and childcare centres. The changes expand protected zones around these facilities from 20 to 50 metres, while explicitly ensuring that peaceful protests, demonstrations, and labour picketing are still allowed.

MM30.15.summary: City Council has sent a motion proposing increased transparency and accountability within the Mayor's office to its Executive Committee for further review. This means the committee will discuss the proposed changes, which aim to make the Mayor's operations more open and responsible to residents, before potentially bringing them back to the full Council for a decision.

MM30.19.summary: Toronto City Council postponed its decision on a motion proposing to find a dedicated school location to support the city's film industry. This means the discussion about potentially creating new educational space for the film sector will continue at a later meeting in June.

MM30.20.summary: City Council has approved renaming the Sumach-Shuter Parkette to Louis March Park, recognizing a significant individual in the community. This decision, made despite standard property naming policies, means the local park will now carry a new name and honour.

MM30.33.summary: In a recent decision, City Council has instructed the city's legal team to attend an appeal hearing for the property at 521 Davisville Avenue. The legal team will actively oppose an appeal made against a local planning decision, which may include hiring outside consultants, to defend the city's approach to development in the area.

PH21.3.summary: Toronto's City Council has updated the fees developers pay for submitting new building applications, with the changes taking effect on July 1, 2025. This includes a substantial decrease in the fee for a specific type of by-law amendment application, aimed at refining development costs and processes. The city will also continue reviewing other development-related fees on a regular cycle.

MM30.39.summary: Toronto City Council has directed its legal team to challenge a proposed development at 14 Restever Gate before the Toronto Local Appeal Body. This move aims to stop a project in Etobicoke North that the city believes is inappropriate, giving local residents a say in protecting their neighbourhood's character.

MM30.40.summary: Toronto City Council has directed the City Solicitor to defend a local planning committee's decisions about property changes at 11 Oswald Crescent before an independent appeal body. This action means the city will use legal resources, and potentially hire outside consultants, to uphold rulings that affect local development and how planning rules are applied in the neighbourhood.

CC30.1.summary: City Council has declared the Ward 25 (Scarborough-Rouge Park) Councillor position vacant and decided to fill it by holding a by-election. This means residents in Ward 25 will soon vote for their new representative, with the election costing a significant amount from a city reserve fund.

RM30.1.summary: City Council officially opened its May 21 and 22 meeting, observing a moment of silence for recent tragedies and reaffirming its commitment to Indigenous Peoples with a land acknowledgment. This procedural step marked the beginning of their important work on city matters.

GG21.9.summary: City Council has approved a five-year contract for Carla Construction and Maintenance Ltd. to operate a new passenger shuttle service in High Park, starting December 2025. This service will help residents and visitors get around the park, with an aim to offer family fare discounts and potentially expand to other parks. Council also requested a review of expanding the TTC's High Park service and community input on stop locations.

IE21.10.summary: City Council has approved a new pilot program to help low-income seniors living in apartments get access to air conditioning. This initiative aims to protect vulnerable residents from extreme heat, with the program expected to launch before summer 2025.

PH21.4.summary: City Council has adopted a new strategy called 'Toronto Builds' to create more affordable rental homes and mixed-income communities on city-owned land. This framework will standardize how the City and its housing agencies develop these properties, aiming to increase housing options for residents across Toronto.

MM30.6.summary: City Council postponed its decision on whether to begin public consultations for communities affected by new shelter locations. This means that residents will have to wait longer for the opportunity to provide input on new shelters in their neighbourhoods.

IE21.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan to transition its own gas-powered outdoor equipment, like leaf blowers and lawnmowers, to zero-emission alternatives. The city will also promote these cleaner, quieter options to residents and businesses, aiming to reduce noise and air pollution across Toronto.

CC30.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved recommendations from the Ombudsman's report, which investigated how the city handled a vital services outage in a shared home. These changes will improve how city staff respond to urgent tenant complaints, communicate with residents, and ensure housing standards enforcement considers the right to adequate housing.

EX23.5.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted an update to SafeTO, the city's 10-year plan for community safety and well-being. The plan will continue to advance its goals, including creating a toolkit for resident-led Neighbourhood Watch programs and expanding access to crisis services on public transit. The City also asked the federal government for ongoing funding to support SafeTO's violence prevention programs.

EX23.1.summary: City Council has decided to create a task force to explore ways to improve transportation and access to Toronto Island Park, including potential long-term options like a fixed link. Additionally, residents will not see an increase in Toronto Island ferry fees for 2025, as current prices will be maintained while new fees are reviewed for the 2026 budget.

TTC4.10.summary: Toronto's transit commission has approved renaming Dundas Station to TMU Station, reflecting the area's strong connection to Toronto Metropolitan University. This change is part of a new partnership with the university and will be implemented with minimal cost and disruption by aligning it with other upcoming transit updates.

GG21.7.summary: City officials have approved a contract for professional consulting services to help manage Toronto's Bridge Rehabilitation Program. This funding will ensure the design and supervision of necessary repairs for 28 bridges and culverts across the city, helping to keep vital infrastructure safe and in good condition for residents.

GG21.4.summary: Toronto approved increasing a contract with Avron Foods Limited to provide snack foods for its Afterschool Recreation Care programs and CampTO summer camps. This additional funding helps feed more children participating in expanded afterschool care and a new snack program for 56,000 kids at 90 CampTO locations, ensuring they receive nutritious food.

GG21.22.summary: Toronto City Council has approved an agreement allowing the Malvern Family Resource Centre to use 2.6 acres of land in the Finch Hydro Corridor. This decision enables the centre to operate a community garden, providing local residents with green space for growing food and fostering community connections.

PH21.1.summary: Toronto City Council updated the city's Official Plan to align with provincial policy changes affecting designated 'Employment Areas.' These changes refine the types of businesses and developments, like offices and retail, that are permitted or encouraged in these job-focused zones. This decision helps shape future economic development and the character of these areas across the city.

HL24.4.summary: Toronto's Board of Health has adopted an updated Noise Action Plan to address noise pollution and its health impacts on residents. City departments will collaborate on assessing noise by-law changes and create guidelines to reduce noise from waste collection. The Board is also pressing provincial and federal governments, along with airport authorities, to fund health studies on airplane noise and engage with affected communities.

SC22.17.summary: Scarborough Community Council formally adopted new local by-laws, which include updates to fire route regulations for specific properties and changes to traffic and parking rules on Brockley Drive. These decisions help manage local safety and traffic flow in these Scarborough neighbourhoods.

NY23.4.summary: North York Community Council has approved a homeowner's request to remove a private tree at 390 Glencairn Avenue, despite initial recommendations to deny the permit. To offset the loss, the homeowner must provide five replacement trees, either by planting them on their property or by paying the city a cash equivalent. This decision reflects how the city balances individual property owner requests with maintaining Toronto's urban tree canopy.

NY22.27.summary: Toronto City Council has approved naming a new park in the Lawrence Park Ravine as "Jaye Robinson Park." This decision honours Jaye Robinson, a former city councillor, and provides a designated green space for the community, even though it deviates from standard naming policy.

EX22.10.summary: To boost Toronto's rental housing supply, the City Council has directed staff to review projects that could have their development fees deferred indefinitely. This incentive, contingent on provincial funding, aims to encourage the construction of more purpose-built rental homes, potentially increasing housing options for residents.

MM29.16.summary: City Council has decided to freeze development charges for new housing developments starting May 1, 2025, using special agreements. This move aims to incentivize new housing construction by keeping costs predictable for builders while a comprehensive review of these charges is underway.
