The Council also reiterated its requests to the Ontario government for more flexibility in managing development charge revenue and compensation for lost funds due to previous provincial legislation.

EX22.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the education property tax rates for 2025, which will fund local schools through a total levy of over $2.1 billion. Council also decided on 'clawback' percentages that reduce tax decreases for commercial, industrial, and multi-residential properties, helping the city recover lost revenue from property tax caps.

TE20.40.summary: Toronto City Council has approved temporary changes to traffic and transit routes to help streetcars move efficiently during upcoming construction at King Street East and Church Street. These measures include a new transit-only lane on Queen Street East, adjusted parking and traffic rules, and restrictions on some street-side patios along diversion routes. This aims to keep transit flowing and manage traffic for residents during the intersection closure.

GG20.10.summary: City Council approved changes to how the city awards contracts by eliminating the Bid Award Panel. This decision delegates authority to the Chief Procurement Officer and City Manager to approve contracts up to $30 million, aiming to streamline the purchasing process for large projects. New policies will also require mandatory performance evaluations for contracts over $500,000 and regular public reporting to maintain transparency.

TE21.4.summary: City Council has approved changes to the Official Plan and Zoning By-laws for the properties at 158 and 190 Sterling Road in the Davenport area. This decision allows for new development or different land use on these sites, with the property owner required to ensure necessary infrastructure upgrades are planned and secured.

MM29.10.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to challenge a planning decision made by the Committee of Adjustment for a property at 164 Homewood Avenue. This means the City's legal team will appeal the initial approval to ensure local development aligns with community planning guidelines. The City Solicitor is authorized to hire outside experts and negotiate a settlement to resolve the issue.

MM29.11.summary: City Council has requested a comprehensive report on the provincial Therme spa project planned for Ontario Place. This report will investigate how 16 acres of City-owned land were transferred to the Province, assess the project's alignment with City planning principles for public benefit and waterfront access, and detail any remaining required approvals. This decision aims to protect Toronto's public waterfront and address local concerns like parking and construction impacts.

CC29.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new labour agreement with the Carpenters' District Council of Ontario. This decision updates the city's fair wage rules for carpenters working on municipal construction projects, ensuring fair compensation and consistent standards across the city.

EX22.9.summary: Toronto City Council has approved continued work on the Mayor's Economic Action Plan, which aims to protect local businesses and jobs from the economic impacts of United States tariffs. City staff will keep advancing these efforts and report back on progress later this year.

IE20.1.summary: Toronto City Council is improving how it manages and delivers construction projects across the city. This initiative aims to make projects faster and more efficient by enhancing project management, strengthening contractor oversight, and improving communication with residents.

IE20.8.summary: Toronto City Council is fast-tracking the installation of permanent safety barriers on the Leaside Bridge and implementing immediate safety measures. This decision aims to improve public safety on the bridge, and the city will also seek provincial funding to support these suicide prevention efforts.

PH20.7.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new strategy to improve housing options for post-secondary students. This plan aims to create more affordable student housing, including a pilot project on public land, and will involve expanding financial incentives to help students secure housing. The goal is to address the significant housing challenges faced by students across the city.

SC21.10.summary: To improve bus service and traffic flow, City Council approved a series of road changes on Eglinton Avenue East in Scarborough. These changes include installing new traffic signals, creating dedicated bus lanes, and adjusting turn restrictions to better support the Scarborough Busway.

MM29.18.summary: City Council has postponed a decision on an environmental assessment agreement for the Scarborough Bluffs West Project until a later meeting in May. This agreement is crucial for the project to move forward and for the required Terms of Reference to be submitted to the provincial government, meaning the project's progress will be delayed.

BL29.1.summary: City Council formally approved a series of by-laws, confirming all decisions made during its recent meeting. These by-laws also authorize special charges on specific properties, allowing the city to bill owners for local improvements they benefit from.

PH20.2.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted new guidelines to better protect tenants when old rental buildings are demolished for redevelopment. The new Rental Replacement Handbook will guide how the city handles these development applications, and Council also requested further reports on additional support for tenants facing displacement, including improving access to legal help.

PH20.4.summary: City Council has updated the sign by-law for the area around 1 Dundas Street East, officially changing all references from "Dundas Square Special Sign District" to "Sankofa Square Special Sign District." This also means signs in the square will now follow the city's standard permitting rules, ensuring consistency with other areas.

NY22.1.summary: City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for 1 Broadlands Boulevard. This decision allows for new development or different types of buildings on the property, which will impact the surrounding Don Valley East neighbourhood.

TE21.9.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a zoning change for 111 Peter Street, allowing a new development to proceed. This decision includes a requirement for the developer to provide 24 affordable rental housing units within the building, offering more housing options for residents.

RM29.7.summary: City Council formally adopted its agenda for April 23rd and 24th, setting the list of items they would debate and decide on those days. This procedural step determines the order of business and highlights key priorities, including the Mayor's focus on congestion and building more rental homes.

EX22.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved moving forward with the design and construction of the first phase of the George Street Revitalization project. This initiative aims to improve social services, housing, and public spaces for residents in the downtown area.

IE20.3.summary: Toronto City Council is updating its plan to reduce traffic congestion, including funding Smart Commute programs with a $400,000 contract. The city also plans to introduce new escalating fees for companies that disrupt roads, aiming to better manage construction-related traffic and its impact on residents.

EX22.5.summary: Toronto's City Council decided to lower the minimum voting age from 18 to 16 for many local community polls, which could include decisions on neighbourhood issues or facility usage. This change, taking effect November 1, 2025, will give younger residents a greater voice in local matters that directly affect them.

PH20.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a zoning change for 7-9 Wardlaw Crescent, allowing for the development of new homes in the area. To support the community, the city will work with local groups to establish a liaison committee, conduct a community safety assessment, and explore improvements to parking and road safety.

EC19.3.summary: The City's Economic and Community Development Committee has postponed its decision on making Toronto's public outdoor ovens more accessible. They are looking at ways to encourage more community use, potentially by removing booking fees and simplifying administrative processes for residents who want to use these amenities.

NY22.2.summary: Toronto City Council approved changes to the city's zoning rules for properties at 150 The Donway West and 4 Overland Drive, which will allow for a new development. As part of this approval, the developer must provide 770 square metres of land for a new public park and make a payment to the city for the remaining parkland requirement.

HL23.1.summary: The Board of Health received an update on the Indigenous Wellness Committee, which helps shape Toronto's health strategies in partnership with Indigenous communities. This committee ensures that city health initiatives, especially for mental health and addictions, address the specific needs of urban Indigenous residents and align with Toronto's Reconciliation Action Plan.

CC28.2.summary: City Council has approved new measures to ensure that any future changes to who qualifies for Toronto's emergency shelters are made fairly and uphold human rights. This means creating clear procedures, consulting with human rights experts, training staff, and making sure policies align with the city's commitment to housing as a human right.

MM28.37.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted a plan to encourage the financial industry to reduce its carbon footprint. This includes exploring a "Sustainable Finance Hub," studying best practices from other cities, and urging local financial institutions to invest more in clean energy and less in fossil fuels. The aim is to build a greener economy and help future-proof against climate risks.

MM28.44.summary: New compensation limits have been approved for public members on the Toronto Community Housing Corporation (TCHC) Board of Directors. This change sets a maximum annual payment of $70,000 for the Board Chair and $20,000 for other public members, affecting the city's spending on governance for its largest social housing provider.

MM28.49.summary: Toronto City Council has instructed its legal team to challenge an appeal concerning a property at 55 Lonborough Avenue before the Toronto Local Appeal Body. This action aims to defend a previous planning decision about the site, which could affect local development and the character of the neighborhood for residents.

MM28.51.summary: Toronto City Council has approved allocating $140,000 in developer contributions to improve security for residents at a Toronto Community Housing Corporation building. These funds will be used to design and install a new secure exterior fence at 175 Cummer Avenue.

PH19.10.summary: Toronto City Council is exploring new rules to protect tenants displaced when buildings are demolished. City staff will study changes to planning policies that could require developers to replace affordable dwelling rooms, like those in rooming houses, if they demolish six or more such rooms, or if the combined total of units and rooms is ten or more. This initiative aims to prevent the loss of affordable housing and support vulnerable tenants in the city.

EX21.13.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted a new plan to speed up the construction of new condominium developments, especially those that include affordable housing units. This initiative allows developers of qualifying projects to defer significant fees to the city, waive interest on these deferrals, and benefit from potentially lower fee rates, aiming to make more housing available sooner. The city will prioritize projects with a higher percentage of affordable homes, supporting up to 3,000 units.

CC28.4.summary: City Council has approved an increase in funding for the infrastructure needed to support Toronto's new electric ferry fleet at the Jack Layton Ferry Terminal. This will cover charging and docking facilities, necessary dock wall repairs, and interim upgrades to the ferry terminal itself, aiming to improve service for those travelling to the Toronto Islands.

EX21.3.summary: Toronto City Council has completed an operational review of its ferry services. The Council is now awaiting an update from city staff by late 2025 on the progress of implementing any changes recommended by this review, aiming to improve how ferries are run.

EX21.5.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new rules allowing taxis, limousines, and ride-share vehicles to operate for a longer period. This change permits most licensed vehicles to be up to ten years old, providing financial relief for drivers and potentially impacting the cost and availability of these services for residents.

MM28.36.summary: To address the problem of empty storefronts across Toronto, City Council has asked staff to investigate several solutions. This includes looking into ways to make landlords rent out vacant spaces, improving property standards for these sites, and making empty storefronts more lively with temporary art or pop-up shops. The city will also explore the possibility of giving tax breaks to property owners who participate in these beautification efforts.

EX21.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a comprehensive review of the city's winter maintenance operations and contracts, including a forensic audit of recent snow removal agreements. This initiative aims to improve how snow is cleared from streets and sidewalks, ensure contractors are held accountable, and better prepare for future winter storms, directly impacting residents' daily commutes and safety.

EY20.15.summary: City Council has approved a change to traffic rules on Evans Avenue between Islington Avenue and Royal York Road. Heavy trucks will now be banned from this section of the road 24 hours a day, instead of just overnight. This aims to improve quality of life for residents by reducing noise and traffic from large vehicles at all times.

CC28.6.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a salary increase for all Ward Councillors, setting their new annual pay at $170,588.60. The city will transfer $957,000 from its corporate budget to cover these increases, impacting city spending for services. The decision also established a process for future salary reviews at the start of each council term.

EX21.12.summary: To support the creation of a new community hub, City Council approved waiving planning application fees for the Tamil Community Centre project. This decision helps the Canadian Tamil community move forward with building a valuable centre for residents in northeast Scarborough.

NY21.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved updates to the planning and zoning rules for 104-114 Finch Avenue East, paving the way for a new development. As part of this approval, the developer must provide parkland, either by dedicating land at another location or by paying a sum to the city for future parkland acquisitions.

TE20.31.summary: Toronto City Council has approved closing the southerly eastbound lane on King Street East, between George Street East and 38 metres west, to regular car traffic. This change will alter traffic flow in the area, potentially benefiting transit or creating more space for other uses on this busy street.

TE20.54.summary: City Council decided to name a public laneway in the Toronto-Danforth area 'Luella Price Lane.' To do this, they bent the rules slightly by accepting signatures gathered before the pandemic, ensuring this local space gets a permanent identity.

TE20.29.summary: Toronto City Council has approved permits for many community and cultural organizations to host events in Nathan Phillips Square from May to September 2025. This decision allows for activities like beer gardens and donation drives, ensuring residents have access to a variety of public events downtown.

RM28.7.summary: Toronto City Council approved its meeting agendas for March 26 and 27, 2025. This decision finalized the order in which councillors would discuss various important items, including collective bargaining negotiations and pressures in the refugee and emergency shelter system.

EX21.4.summary: City Council has approved new interim priority bus lanes on sections of Queens Quay East, Parliament Street, and Front Street West. These changes aim to make transit service more efficient and reduce travel times for riders in the downtown core. The plan also includes specific intersection changes and turn restrictions to support the new bus lanes.

EX21.2.summary: City Council has adopted the Mayor’s Economic Action Plan to help Toronto respond to ongoing trade disputes and tariffs from the United States. This plan outlines strategies to protect the city’s economy and jobs, including support for businesses and improving the movement of goods and services. The City Manager will regularly update the plan to ensure it remains effective in addressing economic uncertainties.

CC28.3.summary: City Council has approved new collective agreements with CUPE Local 79, which represents many Toronto city workers. These agreements set new terms for pay, benefits, and working conditions, requiring adjustments to the city's operating budgets.

CC28.5.summary: Toronto City Council has appointed Kate Bassil and Denise Andrea Campbell to new Deputy City Manager roles. These senior leaders will oversee crucial city departments, including Community and Emergency Services, and Community Development and Social Services, shaping how the city delivers vital services to residents.

EX21.8.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a $5 million expansion for the city's school food program in 2025, aiming to provide more students with access to healthy meals. This increase will be covered by existing savings from last year, meaning no new net costs for taxpayers. Council is also requesting the federal and provincial governments contribute more funding to help build a universal student food program.

EC18.4.summary: City Council has launched a pilot program allowing non-motorized food vendors, such as food trailers, to operate in designated zones within the University-Rosedale area. This trial runs from April 1 to December 31, 2025, offering residents more diverse food options on city streets during this period.

GG19.17.summary: To ensure responsible contracting and protect public funds, City Council has banned Duron Ontario Ltd. and its affiliates from bidding on any City of Toronto contracts for five years. This decision also includes a directive to review potential impacts on unionized construction workers involved in city projects.

TTC2.13.summary: Transit officials have approved an urgent request to City Council to prioritize improving bus and streetcar travel times and reliability across Toronto. This decision aims to make public transit faster and more dependable by influencing urban planning, roadway design, and re-evaluating street parking, ensuring recent investments in increased service are truly effective. This matters to residents by encouraging transit use and helping reduce overall traffic congestion.

TTC2.6.summary: The Toronto Transit Commission is exploring options to provide public Wi-Fi on buses, streetcars, and in subway tunnels, working to close the city's digital divide. They will also survey customers to understand the actual demand, given that many riders now prefer using widely available cellular services. This decision will shape future internet access for commuters across the transit network.

SC20.6.summary: Scarborough Community Council rejected an application to remove a privately owned Colorado blue spruce tree at 513 Huntingwood Drive. While the homeowner wanted the tree removed due to concerns about its condition, the City's Urban Forestry staff determined the tree is healthy and maintainable. This decision means the tree will remain on the property, upholding the City's tree protection by-laws.

SC20.1.summary: City Council approved changes to planning rules for properties on Kingston Road and St Quentin Avenue in Scarborough, allowing for a new development. These changes require a minimum number of larger family-sized units, specifically 2- and 3-bedroom apartments, to be included in the project. The owner must also provide a comprehensive relocation and financial assistance plan for existing tenants displaced by the redevelopment.

TE20.55.summary: Toronto's City Council recently addressed safety concerns for the Ontario Line construction, asking Metrolinx to prioritize safety when using city roads for their work. They also requested Metrolinx to clearly post all heavy truck routes on their website, ensuring residents have access to crucial information about construction impacts in their neighbourhoods.

TE20.32.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the return of the Ontario Honda Dealers Indy Toronto Race for 2025, allowing a new agreement with the event organizer. This means residents in the downtown area, especially around Liberty Village, should expect temporary street closures and traffic disruptions for the annual event.

NY21.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to planning and zoning rules for a new development at 2810-2816 Bayview Avenue. This decision allows for a new building project, which now must include at least 16 visitor parking spaces and either provide land for a park or contribute funds towards creating park space in the city.

EY20.16.summary: The Etobicoke York Community Council has decided to reduce the speed limit on a section of Evans Avenue from 50 km/h to 40 km/h. This change, applying to the stretch between Islington Avenue and Royal York Road, aims to improve safety for residents in the area.

MPB27.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the city's 2025 annual spending plan, which outlines how funds will be allocated for various services and programs. Council made several adjustments, reallocating money to support initiatives such as the Little Jamaica Cultural District, expand pickleball and seniors programs, and enhance tree care and ravine stewardship.

CC27.1.summary: Toronto City Council has set the property tax rates for 2025, which includes a 5.4% increase for residential properties. These changes will help fund essential city services and specific capital projects for transit and housing.

MM26.1.summary: City Council rejected a request to re-open and amend a previous decision regarding the homelessness services capital infrastructure strategy, specifically for a site at 1220 Wilson Avenue. This means the original plans for that shelter location remain in effect, continuing the city's approach to housing and support for people experiencing homelessness.

MM26.11.summary: City Council is advocating for a "New Deal for Cities" to establish a more sustainable financial framework for Toronto. This means the city will collaborate with other municipal organizations to highlight the need for better funding from provincial and federal governments, helping Toronto fund services without relying heavily on property taxes.

MM26.7.summary: Toronto City Council recently approved a 'Buy Local, Buy Canadian' campaign to protect local jobs and businesses. In response to potential tariffs from the US, the city will encourage residents, businesses, and its own departments to prioritize Canadian-made goods and services. They also directed staff to enhance local procurement practices and called on the federal government to create a clear 'Canadian content' label for products.

MM26.13.summary: Toronto City Council has formally declared its opposition to any proposal for Canada to join the United States. This decision serves as a statement of national identity from the country's largest city and will be communicated to all Toronto Members of Parliament.

PH18.5.summary: To help speed up housing construction, City Council removed some review requirements for development along "Avenues" (major streets identified for growth) as part of its Housing Accelerator Fund commitment. However, for new areas being considered as Avenues, the city will undertake detailed community consultations and studies on local impacts like traffic, parking, and infrastructure before any changes are made.

PH18.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to planning and zoning rules to allow for more housing options, specifically fiveplex and sixplex buildings, in Ward 23 (Scarborough North). These updates aim to increase housing choices in established neighbourhoods. The city will also protect by-law protected trees during new construction and monitor the impact of these changes over the next few years.

EX20.5.summary: Toronto City Council has updated the governance of Moss Park Arena, changing how its managing board is appointed. The Board is now tasked with developing a multi-year plan to boost the arena's finances through new revenue and fundraising, explore potential full reconstruction, and enhance community access with free skating opportunities and improved facilities. This decision aims to revitalize the arena and better serve local residents.

EX20.11.summary: The City Council has adopted the final plans for new and reconfigured streets in the Golden Mile area of Scarborough, moving forward with a major infrastructure project. This decision approves specific street designs and additional road improvements, ensuring that transportation in the area is ready for future growth and development while addressing safety and utility needs.

EX20.13.summary: City Council has decided to establish a new advisory group focused on enhancing Toronto's independence and improving how the city is governed. This group will explore ways for the city to gain more control over local decisions, with representatives from all four city districts to ensure broad input.

PH18.6.summary: Toronto City Council has approved applying for and receiving federal funding from a rapid housing program. This funding will be used to build more affordable and supportive homes across the city, often in partnership with non-profit groups and the University Health Network, to help address the urgent need for housing.

PH18.16.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to officially designate the property at 3100 Weston Road as a heritage site, protecting it under the Ontario Heritage Act. This decision ensures the preservation of its historical and cultural significance, which includes Indigenous heritage and its past use by a Ukrainian Greek Catholic monastic order.

HL21.5.summary: Toronto's health board recently approved recommendations to enhance support for residents experiencing homelessness. This means public health officials will assess city services for better accessibility and collaborate with other city divisions and health partners to ensure comprehensive care. The city also urged provincial and federal governments to provide more funding and policy support for critical health, mental health, and harm reduction services.

NY20.1.summary: Local city councillors denied a homeowner's request to remove a healthy red oak tree on their private property. This decision upholds city bylaws designed to protect maintainable trees, ensuring the preservation of local green spaces and the urban forest canopy.

CC25.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new labour agreement with the Toronto Civic Employees Union Local 416, which represents many city workers. This settlement sets the terms for their pay and benefits, directly impacting the city's 2025 operating budget and how public funds are spent on delivering city services.

EX19.3.summary: City Council has decided to launch a public campaign advocating for fair wages for gig workers in Toronto, including ride-share and taxi drivers. The city will also conduct a data-driven study to analyze taxi driver wages and explore how to improve accessible vehicle-for-hire services for residents.

PH17.5.summary: Toronto City Council has adjusted the dates when landlords must provide heating and air conditioning in rental properties. The new rules, effective April 30, 2025, will require air conditioning systems (if provided by the owner) to operate from June 1 to September 30, while the heating season will run from October 1 to May 15. This aims to improve tenant comfort and safety, with City staff also tasked to explore establishing a maximum indoor temperature standard of 26°C for rental units in the future.

PH17.7.summary: Toronto City Council approved the relaunch and updated terms of its Home Ownership Assistance Program, aiming to make homeownership more accessible for residents. The program offers financial incentives like waived development fees and charge exemptions to non-profit and private developers building affordable ownership housing, with $5 million allocated to support these projects. A pilot of up to 50 units will also test a new 'turn-key' approach.

CC24.11.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a settlement for a development application concerning properties on Dupont, Dufferin, and Emerson Streets. This decision means the City will support the project at the Ontario Land Tribunal, conditional on the developer addressing requirements for infrastructure upgrades and municipal servicing.

CC24.1.summary: City Council acknowledged and reviewed a report from Ombudsman Toronto regarding the city's decision to stop allowing refugee claimants into its main shelter system. This report investigated how the city handled this significant policy change, which impacts vulnerable newcomers seeking shelter.

MM24.2.summary: City Council decided to send a proposal for a new Toronto Youth Employment Program to a committee for further review. This means the idea for a program aimed at helping young people find jobs will be studied in more detail before Council makes a final decision.

MM24.9.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a request to review the city's nuisance lighting rules. This means city staff will look into strengthening the bylaw to address issues caused by excessive or poorly directed outdoor lighting, including modern LED and soffit lights. This decision aims to help residents dealing with light pollution and improve neighbourhood comfort by potentially updating current regulations.

MM24.15.summary: Toronto City Council has forwarded a motion to the Executive Committee for consideration, which proposes giving the city more power and control over its local governance. This initiative could significantly impact how the city delivers services and manages its finances, affecting all residents in the long run.

MM24.16.summary: Toronto City Council is exploring ways to support a "Home Away Program" for burn victims and their families. They have asked for a report on the possibility of leasing a City-owned property at 641 Eglinton Avenue West at a very low cost to the Toronto Fire Fighters Charities, to provide a temporary home for those receiving treatment at Sunnybrook or SickKids. The City will also collaborate with the provincial government to support this vital program.

MM24.21.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to challenge a recent planning decision for the property at 187 Oakridge Drive. This means the city's legal team will appeal the ruling by a city planning committee at an independent appeals board, aiming to ensure local development aligns with city guidelines and community interests.

MM24.27.summary: Toronto City Council has officially expressed its strong objection to proposed new tariffs on Canadian goods entering the United States. Council believes these tariffs would severely harm both Canadian and American economies, impacting local businesses and residents. They will forward their objection to the U.S. Consul General in Toronto.

MM24.28.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to have its legal team officially oppose an appeal at a local tribunal regarding a property at 30 Charles Street East. The appeal challenges the city's initial refusal of a property owner's request for minor changes, and the city's lawyers will defend this decision, potentially hiring outside experts. This move ensures the city defends its planning decisions, which can impact local development.

MM24.29.summary: Addressing concerns about cross-border firearm smuggling, City Council has urged the Canadian government to implement retaliatory tariffs on specific U.S. imports, such as delivery trucks and auto parts. This action is proposed as a response to potential U.S. tariffs on Canadian goods, aiming to pressure the U.S. to halt the flow of illegal guns into Canada and enhance public safety.

MM24.32.summary: Toronto has finalized an agreement with Scarborough Health Network to allow the relocation and alteration of city water and sewer pipes on hospital property. This supports the urgent expansion of the Centenary Hospital Emergency Department, with the hospital network fully responsible for all related costs.

EC17.4.summary: Toronto City Council adopted a new action plan to improve safety for both staff and clients within the city's shelter system and will implement tools to address anti-Black racism. The city also approved reimbursing community organizations up to $3.4 million for providing temporary shelter to refugee claimants and committed to expanding specialized housing options for refugees.

MM24.43.summary: Toronto City Council has directed its legal team to participate in an appeal concerning a property at 321 Laird Drive. The City Solicitor will attend the local appeals board to oppose a challenge to a previous planning decision, aiming to uphold the city's planning rules for this specific development. This action shows the city's commitment to defending its position on local planning matters.

RM24.1.summary: Toronto City Council convened its meeting, beginning with a formal land acknowledgement recognizing Indigenous peoples and territories. Council members also observed moments of silence to reflect on significant events and to remember individuals who have passed away.

CC24.3.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to prioritize the construction of three new SmartTrack transit stations: East Harbour, Bloor-Lansdowne, and St. Clair-Old Weston, as part of a multi-billion dollar program. Council is also pressing the Province of Ontario to secure funding for the two remaining stations, Finch-Kennedy and King-Liberty, ensuring no additional financial burden on city taxpayers. This decision shapes future transit development and local community access across Toronto.

CC24.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new policy framework outlining how the city will respond to public demonstrations, aiming to balance public safety with protecting residents' rights. The city will also develop a by-law to protect places of worship, schools, and cultural institutions from hate-motivated protests, and is establishing a grant program to help community groups fund security improvements.

PH17.2.summary: Toronto City Council has delayed a decision on proposals to allow more housing and small local businesses in residential neighbourhoods. Instead of approving new rules, they sent the plan back for further public consultation and a campaign to explain the changes, ensuring residents can provide feedback before any updates to zoning bylaws are considered.

CC24.6.summary: City Council has approved a settlement with a developer regarding new zoning rules for properties at 4001 Steeles Avenue West, 5000 Jane Street, and 360 Hullmar Drive. This decision means the city avoids a long dispute over development plans at a provincial tribunal, allowing for progress on the project once the developer meets specific conditions for things like traffic and utilities.

CC24.12.summary: The city has agreed to a settlement with a developer regarding proposed building projects on Walsh Avenue and Weston Road, which were previously under review by a provincial planning appeals board. This decision allows for development to proceed, provided the developer meets specific conditions related to zoning, infrastructure, and traffic management to ensure the project fits within community standards and local services.

CC24.13.summary: City Council has agreed to settle a development application for a property at 417-419 Burnhamthorpe Road with the Ontario Land Tribunal. This decision allows for a new development to proceed, provided the builder meets specific conditions, including requirements for residential and visitor parking, addressing drainage issues, and ensuring proper tree preservation.

MM24.42.summary: Toronto City Council has declared food insecurity an emergency, recognizing the urgent need to address the issue across the city. This decision directs the City Manager to incorporate the emergency declaration into ongoing strategies like the Poverty Reduction Strategy and Food Charter. Council also called on the provincial government to immediately boost funding for school food programs and enable their expansion.

EX19.10.summary: Beginning January 1, 2025, Toronto residents will see new interim rates for solid waste management services, as approved by City Council. The city will also launch a campaign to help residents, especially seniors, find ways to save on their garbage bills, for example by switching to smaller bins.

PH17.6.summary: Toronto City Council has updated its development rules to remove parkland dedication requirements for certain types of new housing. This change exempts homeowners creating additional residential units, like secondary suites or laneway homes (up to five units per property), from having to set aside land for parks or pay a fee. The goal is to encourage more diverse housing options, including multi-tenant homes, across the city.

SC18.3.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to oppose a proposed development at 123 Bellamy Road North at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This means the city is advocating for stricter conditions and revisions to the project to ensure it meets city standards for issues like traffic, servicing, and green spaces. If the development is eventually approved, the developer will be responsible for paying for any necessary infrastructure upgrades.

IE18.1.summary: Aiming to enhance road safety, City Council approved new rules requiring vehicles to stop further away from pedestrian crossovers, specifically 15 metres on the approach side and 10 metres beyond. These changes, part of the Vision Zero plan, also include designating new Community Safety Zones across the city and establishing updated penalties for violations, effective February 10, 2025.

IE18.5.summary: Toronto City Council has taken a step to improve road safety for pedestrians and cyclists by approving a plan to install side guards on its suitable commercial trucks. This measure, intended to prevent serious collisions, is estimated to cost $5.68 million and will be funded through the 2025 budget process. The City is also asking the federal government to implement national safety regulations for commercial vehicle side guards.

BL24.1.summary: City Council passed a series of by-laws, including one that appoints Dr. Na-Koshie Lamptey as the new Acting Medical Officer of Health for Toronto. This change in leadership at the Toronto Public Health unit ensures continued oversight of public health initiatives vital to resident well-being.

CC24.16.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new financial incentives to encourage the construction of more rental housing, including affordable units. This decision allows developers to defer fees for building over 6,100 new rental homes and grants property tax exemptions for the affordable units within these projects. These measures aim to boost the supply of much-needed rental housing across the city.

EX19.1.summary: City Council has approved a new approach to speed up housing construction across Toronto. This will involve Toronto Community Housing Corporation and CreateTO working as "public developers" to build more homes on City-owned land, aiming to deliver housing faster for residents.

EX19.21.summary: City Council has approved a plan to lease a portion of 1785 Finch Avenue West to the Community Music Schools of Toronto. This decision allows the non-profit music school to establish or expand its facilities on city property, providing valuable music education and arts programs to residents at a very low cost.

AU7.1.summary: Toronto City Council recently reviewed the Auditor General's office 2025 work plan, which outlines upcoming audit projects for the city. This plan guides how the Auditor General will investigate city operations to find efficiencies and ensure public funds are managed responsibly, benefiting all residents.

EC17.1.summary: A report from Ombudsman Toronto regarding its Housing Unit has been approved by City Council. This decision ensures regular updates on how the city supports housing rights and will prompt a review of a potential "Housing Commissioner" role, aiming to improve how Toronto addresses systemic housing challenges and advocates for residents.

EX19.26.summary: City officials reviewed a proposal recommending that revenue from the Guild Inn Estates sublease be reinvested into Guild Park and Gardens. This suggestion aims to fund future repairs, improvements like public washrooms and trail upgrades, and the restoration of public art, ensuring the park's long-term vitality for residents.

EX19.6.summary: City Council has decided to remove the old Scarborough RT infrastructure that is no longer needed for transit. This decision paves the way for public consultations in 2026 to explore new uses for the corridor, such as parks, trails, and other community spaces, with future capital costs to be determined.

SC18.12.summary: To allow for continued construction of the Scarborough Subway Extension, City Council has approved several temporary road and sidewalk closures in Scarborough. Drivers and pedestrians in areas like McCowan Road and Eglinton Avenue East will experience disruptions and detours for periods lasting up to six years.

SC18.20.summary: Scarborough Community Council is reviewing a proposal to rename Rosebank Park after the late Councillor Cynthia Lai, who represented Ward 23 - Scarborough North. This change would honor her significant contributions as a dedicated public servant and community builder, particularly as the first immigrant woman of Asian descent elected to City Council in Scarborough. City staff will report back in 2025 on the renaming and the inclusion of a biographical plaque.

SC18.9.summary: Scarborough Community Council rejected a homeowner's request to remove a large silver maple tree at 20 Shirley Crescent. The Council decided to uphold the city's tree bylaws, which deemed the 153-centimeter diameter tree healthy and maintainable. This means the tree will remain, contributing to the local environment and urban tree canopy.

SC18.14.summary: To make streets safer, Scarborough Community Council has approved the installation of 13 speed humps on Euclid Avenue between Morrish Road and Meadowvale Road. They also reduced the speed limit on that stretch of road from 40 km/h to 30 km/h, aiming to slow down traffic and improve neighborhood safety for residents.

SC18.15.summary: Scarborough Community Council has approved measures to make Megan Avenue safer for residents. Speed humps will be installed along the street, and the speed limit will be reduced from 40 or 50 km/h to 30 km/h in different sections. These changes aim to calm traffic and reduce speeding.

SC18.16.summary: Scarborough Community Council has approved the installation of 13 speed humps on Rouge Hills Drive, between Island Road and Lawrence Avenue East. This decision aims to improve road safety by reducing speeding, and also includes lowering the speed limit in this area from 40 km/h to 30 km/h.

SC18.17.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the installation of new traffic lights at Lawrence Avenue East and Valia Road/Private Driveway. This decision aims to improve safety for drivers, pedestrians, and transit riders in the area.

PH17.11.summary: Toronto's Planning and Housing Committee is moving forward with a plan to explore allowing more multi-unit homes, specifically buildings with up to six units and four storeys, across the city. This initial report outlines a workplan for staff to study the idea, learn from other projects, and gather community input throughout 2025. The goal is to increase housing options in residential areas, a initiative supported by the federal Housing Accelerator Fund, with final recommendations expected by late next year.

NY19.2.summary: North York Community Council has rejected a property owner's appeal to remove a 63-centimetre honey locust tree at 124 Sandringham Drive, which is located in a protected ravine area. This means the permit to remove the tree, initially denied by Urban Forestry, will not be issued, upholding the city's bylaws that protect trees in ravine and natural feature areas.

EY18.7.summary: Toronto City Council will oppose a developer's request at a provincial tribunal to change planning rules for a proposed development near Lake Promenade and Park Boulevard. If the development is approved, the City will require the developer to cover costs for any necessary upgrades to local services like water, sewers, and roads, ensuring existing infrastructure can handle the new buildings.

EY18.6.summary: Toronto City Council has refused a developer's application to change the building and land use rules for the property at 4875 Dundas Street West. This decision means the proposed project cannot move forward as requested, though the applicant has the option to appeal the refusal to the Ontario Land Tribunal.

GG18.28.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a long-term lease agreement with Unity Health Toronto for the property at 50 Richmond Street East. This decision enables Unity Health Toronto to use the space, with the City contributing up to $10.025 million to cover significant renovation and accessibility upgrade costs. This investment supports healthcare or community services provided by Unity Health Toronto in the downtown core.

CC23.19.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a settlement for a new development at 3374 Keele Street, agreeing to a specific zoning plan rather than continuing a dispute at the planning appeals tribunal. This means the developer must meet conditions, including ensuring local infrastructure like stormwater and transportation can handle the new buildings, before the project can fully proceed.

PH16.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new by-law requiring landlords to get a special license before renovating rental units. This new rule aims to protect tenants from "renovictions," where landlords evict residents under the guise of renovations. The by-law comes into effect on July 31, 2025.

PH16.13.summary: Toronto City Council has directed staff to clarify rules for building multiple homes in semi-detached properties, which could lead to more diverse housing options in residential areas. They also asked for a review of development fees for multiplexes with more than four units, potentially impacting the cost and feasibility of building larger multi-unit homes.

PH16.3.summary: City Council has updated the planning rules and zoning for 72 Amroth Avenue, a key step in the Beaches-East York Pilot Project. This decision aims to create more diverse housing options within the neighbourhood, potentially leading to more types of homes being built in existing residential areas.

IE17.4.summary: City Council has approved a road safety project in principle for Parkside Drive in the Parkdale-High Park area. This plan includes adding a new bikeway, upgrading TTC bus stops, and improving major intersections at Lake Shore Boulevard West and Bloor Street West with designated turning lanes, aiming to make the street safer for all users.

MM23.1.summary: Toronto City Council has formally declared its opposition to private companies that pay people for blood and plasma donations within the city. This decision means the city will advocate to other levels of government and health organizations for a system of voluntary donations only, aiming to uphold ethical blood collection practices.

MM23.10.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to review how councillors are paid and how their office staff's salaries and job roles are structured. The City Manager will report by March 2025 with recommendations to adjust councillor pay to be competitive, while the City Clerk will suggest modernizing staff compensation by late 2025. This process aims to ensure fair and appropriate pay for those serving the public.

MM23.12.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to send the city's legal team to an independent appeals tribunal to defend a local planning committee's refusal of a development application at 16 Martindale Road. This means the city will actively oppose an appeal by the property owner, impacting the future development of the site and the surrounding neighbourhood.

MM23.13.summary: Toronto City Council has asked for a study on the possibility of adding a pedestrian scramble at the busy Yonge and Eglinton intersection. This aims to improve safety and convenience for people walking in the area once the Eglinton Crosstown LRT is fully operational.

MM23.14.summary: Councillors Dianne Saxe and Amber Morley proposed a motion concerning provincial interference in Toronto's infrastructure funding and local decision-making. However, City Council did not consider the motion as it was deemed redundant, meaning a similar discussion was already taking place under a different agenda item.

MM23.16.summary: Toronto City Council has directed its legal team to challenge a proposed development at 171 Pendrith Street. City lawyers will oppose the project before the independent appeals body, with the option to hire outside experts or negotiate a settlement. This decision means the city is actively working to prevent a development in the University-Rosedale neighbourhood that may not align with local planning expectations.

MM23.18.summary: To protect tenants from potential rent price-fixing, Toronto City Council has asked the City Manager to investigate software used by landlords for this purpose. The report, due by mid-2025, will explore how the city can restrict such software and also includes a request to the provincial government to ban it outright.

MM23.20.summary: City Council has approved a 10-year low-cost lease renewal for the Toronto Azzurri Soccer Club, allowing them to continue operating their field house and soccer fields at 4995 Keele Street until 2035. This decision ensures ongoing community access to recreational sports facilities in the York Centre area.

MM23.32.summary: To protect tenants at 80 Guestville Avenue from eviction, City Council asked the Chief Building Official to investigate the building permits issued for that address. This inquiry will determine if construction has not begun within six months, potentially leading to the revocation of permits to stop current residents from losing their homes.

CC23.1.summary: Toronto City Council has formally opposed a provincial bill (Bill 212) that would change the Highway Traffic Act, arguing it interferes with the city's ability to manage its own roads and address traffic congestion. Council believes this provincial action undermines local democracy and has stated it will not pay for any costs resulting from these proposed changes to city infrastructure.

MM23.26.summary: City Council rejected a proposal related to the environmental report for converting the Scarborough Rapid Transit line into a busway, specifically concerning a bus stop near Tara Avenue and Mooregate Avenue. This decision means that the city will not proceed with the associated recommendation, which could have allowed funds to be reallocated to other priority Toronto Transit Commission capital projects.

MM23.34.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to appoint a new Fire Chief and General Manager for Toronto Fire Services, following reference checks and contract negotiations. This individual will lead the city's fire safety and emergency response efforts, with their official start date set for January 2, 2025, or when their employment contract is finalized.

EC16.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved "Culture Connects," a new 10-year action plan to support and grow the city's arts and culture sector from 2025 to 2035. This plan aims to improve funding, stability, and affordability for cultural organizations and artists, with dedicated staff and resources to be requested in upcoming city budgets. The City will also seek provincial support to help achieve these goals.

EC16.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new long-term economic plan called 'Sidewalks to Skylines,' which will guide the city's growth and development from 2025 to 2035. This strategy aims to boost the city's economy by outlining future initiatives, including developing a major event hosting strategy and enhancing support for local business districts. City departments are now tasked with putting this plan into action and will include necessary resources in upcoming city budget discussions.

CC23.16.summary: Toronto City Council has reached an agreement with a developer regarding a project at 49-59 Lawrence Avenue East, avoiding a full hearing at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This decision means a revised development plan for the site will be approved, but only after the developer meets specific conditions related to planning, engineering, and wind studies.

IE17.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the temporary closure of the eastbound Gardiner Expressway and northbound Don Valley Parkway for the 2025 Toronto Triathlon Festival. These major routes will be shut down on Sunday, July 27, 2025, from 2 AM to 12 PM, impacting residents' travel; to mitigate congestion, the city will prohibit other major road closures on that day and require the event organizer to cover related expenses.

PH16.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to make it easier to build mid-rise apartment buildings along major streets (Avenues). These updates simplify zoning rules and adjust how new buildings fit with existing neighbourhoods, aiming to boost housing supply and offer more housing options across the city.

CC23.14.summary: Toronto City Council has agreed to settle a development appeal for a property at 710 The West Mall with the developer. This means the city will support a specific development plan at the provincial Ontario Land Tribunal, provided the developer meets various conditions related to traffic management, local infrastructure, and stormwater plans.

MM23.6.summary: City Council has formally declared December as Christian Heritage Month across Toronto. This decision aims to recognize and celebrate the cultural contributions and heritage of the Christian community within the city.

TE17.53.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new traffic lights at the intersection of Kingston Road and Columbine Avenue. This decision aims to improve pedestrian safety and make crossings safer and more accessible for residents following community concerns about the busy street.

EX18.3.summary: City Council has officially named the new island in the Port Lands "Ookwemin Minising" and the new park "Biidaasige Park." These names, chosen through engagement with Indigenous communities, establish the identity of significant new public spaces created as part of the Port Lands flood protection project.

PH16.2.summary: City Council has approved changes to the city's zoning by-law for low-rise residential areas in the Toronto and East York district. This decision updates the rules for what can be built on these lands, aiming to increase housing options and supply as part of the city's Housing Action Plan.

RM23.7.summary: City Council approved its official agenda for the November 13 and 14 meetings. This decision sets the schedule for debating important city issues, including key matters such as housing incentives and a rental renovation by-law, guiding what topics councillors will prioritize for Toronto residents.

EX18.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new program aimed at creating 20,000 new rental homes across the city, including at least 4,000 affordable units. The city will provide financial incentives like property tax reductions to developers, while also requesting substantial grant funding from the federal and provincial governments to help achieve this housing goal.

DM23.1.summary: City Council has decided to explore allowing 16 and 17-year-olds to vote in local neighbourhood polls. They have asked the City Clerk to develop a plan by early 2025 on how to reduce the minimum voting age for these specific local elections. This move aims to increase youth engagement and participation in important community decisions.

EX18.17.summary: To help fund the 2026 FIFA World Cup, City Council approved a temporary increase in the Municipal Accommodation Tax, also known as the hotel tax. This tax will rise from 6% to 8.5% for stays between June 2025 and July 2026, meaning visitors staying in Toronto hotels during that period will pay more to help cover hosting costs.

AU6.1.summary: To improve the cleanliness and safety of Toronto's parks, City Council has adopted recommendations from an audit of the Parks Branch. The Council is directing staff to use GPS tracking and better oversight of work records to ensure park maintenance crews are more efficient and accountable for their time. This aims to improve how parks are maintained across the city.

AU6.2.summary: City Council has adopted a series of recommendations to improve how Toronto handles unsolicited proposals from companies, sparked by an audit of a previous 'PayIt' proposal. This decision ensures greater fairness, openness, and transparency in city procurement processes. It aims to protect public interests by updating policies, providing staff training, and strengthening project planning for large city-wide initiatives.

EX18.7.summary: Toronto City Council has updated the regulations for sidewalk cafés, including the popular CaféTO program, for 2025. These changes amend city bylaws to clarify who must be notified when a new sidewalk café applies for a permit, particularly when it is located next to residential properties. This impacts how residents and local businesses are informed about proposed cafés in their communities.

FWC2.1.summary: City Council is considering increasing the tax on hotel stays from 6% to 8.5% between June 2025 and July 2026, a move recommended by a city subcommittee. This proposed change aims to raise more money to cover Toronto's hosting costs for the 2026 FIFA World Cup and includes updates to how the city plans to procure goods and services for the event.

SC17.6.summary: The Scarborough Community Council has decided to name a new park located at 320 Markham Road as "Grant Faulkner Park." This action establishes a permanent memorial in Scarborough to honour the memory and lasting impact of Grant Faulkner on the community.

NY18.12.summary: New development is coming to 230 and 240 The Donway West after Toronto City Council approved changes to the area's Official Plan and zoning. This decision requires the developer to create a new public park spanning over 513 square metres, adding green space for local residents.

NY18.4.summary: North York Community Council has denied a resident's request for a permit to remove a private black walnut tree at 85 Helendale Avenue. This decision means the healthy tree, protected by City by-laws, will remain on the property, supporting the city's urban forest canopy.

NY18.27.summary: North York Community Council has decided to deny a resident's request to remove a City-owned red oak tree at 110 Caines Avenue. This decision means the healthy tree, which the resident wanted to remove for a driveway construction, will be protected and remain part of the city's urban forest.

TE17.8.summary: Toronto City Council approved zoning changes for properties on Woodbine and Danforth Avenues. This decision allows for a new development to proceed, which will include 12 affordable rental housing units as part of the project.

PA11.3.summary: The Toronto Parking Authority Board received its regular update on ongoing legal issues, which include disputes with suppliers, personal injury claims, and trademark protection. Details about these specific cases remain confidential to protect solicitor-client privilege. This ensures the TPA continues to manage potential liabilities and safeguard its operations, such as parking facilities and Bike Share Toronto.

PA11.10.summary: Toronto Parking Authority's Board received an update on a lawsuit they filed to claim $7 million from a developer. This lawsuit is about the Authority's option to buy a retail unit at 935 Eglinton Avenue West, and its outcome could affect how this property serves residents.

GG17.16.summary: Toronto's General Government Committee is exploring new ways to fund improvements for Nathan Phillips Square. They've asked staff to review current event booking fees and consider dedicating that revenue to a special fund for capital upgrades. This aims to ensure the iconic public space remains well-maintained for residents and visitors.

EX17.1.summary: City Council is moving forward with plans for a universal student food program in Toronto schools. They've requested a report by November 2024 detailing funding, including contributions from other governments, and operational needs to start programs by January 2025 for eligible schools previously denied due to budget limits. Additionally, a comprehensive strategy will be developed by mid-2025 to achieve universal mid-morning meals by the 2026/2027 school year and universal lunch programs by 2030, ensuring all students have access to nutritious food.

PH15.3.summary: City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for 35 Bellevue Avenue, which will allow a new development to proceed at this location. This decision also includes requests for a new city-serviced garbage collection system that won't delay construction and a lighting plan for the building's south side.

MM22.3.summary: Toronto City Council has supported reserving a portion of the city land at 770 Don Mills Road, not designated for new housing, exclusively for recreation and education. This decision ensures that this specific area in Don Mills will provide dedicated space for community activities and learning.

MM22.5.summary: Toronto City Council has adjusted the boundaries for a 40 km/h speed limit zone on Bloor Street West. This updated rule sets the slower speed limit for a slightly different stretch of the road, aiming to improve safety for drivers and pedestrians, especially near St. Clement Catholic School.

MM22.6.summary: City Council has decided to postpone its discussion on a proposal that would allow 16 and 17-year-olds to vote in Toronto's municipal elections. This means the important question of extending voting opportunities to younger residents will be revisited at an upcoming meeting in November.

MM22.8.summary: Toronto City Council has asked the Province of Ontario to consider alternatives to a potentially disruptive and costly tunnel under Highway 401. The Council suggests exploring a high-speed rail system connecting cities like Hamilton and Montreal, and buying back the 407 Express Toll Route, aiming to reduce congestion and improve travel options for residents.

MM22.20.summary: Toronto City Council is demanding more transparency and accountability from Metrolinx regarding the severely delayed Eglinton Crosstown LRT project. Council has formally asked the Province of Ontario to launch a public inquiry into the project's extensive delays and rising costs. They also want top Metrolinx and provincial transportation officials to come before a city committee to explain the situation, including the expected completion date and additional costs, and outline support for affected residents and businesses.

MM22.21.summary: Toronto City Council has called on the federal and provincial governments for increased support and funding for bail reform initiatives, including resources for the Toronto Police Service to improve monitoring of individuals on bail and fund programs addressing the root causes of crime. This action aims to enhance public safety by ensuring bail decisions are effective and supported by adequate services and data collection.

MM22.26.summary: City Council has decided to send its lawyers to an appeal hearing to defend a local planning committee's decision regarding a property at 186 Thirtieth Street. This means the City is upholding the refusal of a development application, which could impact the future of the neighbourhood.

MM22.30.summary: To help speed up the creation of affordable housing, City Council has decided that a new multi-tenant home at 268 Seaton Street does not need to provide land for a park or pay money in its place. This exemption aims to reduce costs and expedite the development of much-needed housing options.

MM22.31.summary: Toronto's legal team has been directed to attend an appeal hearing to oppose a development application for 228 Guildwood Parkway. This decision helps uphold local planning rules and protect the character of the Guildwood neighbourhood, with the city solicitor able to negotiate a resolution if possible.

MM22.34.summary: Toronto City Council has temporarily paused the expansion of the MyToronto Pay platform, which residents use to pay for city services online. No new services or payment options will be added until Council reviews a report from the Auditor General that investigated how the platform was acquired, aiming to ensure transparency and public confidence in city contracts.

IE16.2.summary: City Council has approved the first phase of a new Strategic Capital Coordination Office to improve how the city manages major infrastructure projects. This office aims to make large construction projects, like roads and water systems, more efficient and accountable, which could reduce delays and disruptions for residents.

IE16.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved measures to reduce traffic congestion and travel times across the city. This plan includes exploring how new developments can minimize lane closures during construction and establishing a new fee for temporary street occupation permits.

EX17.4.summary: Toronto City Council approved several adjustments to the Vacant Home Tax program, making it easier for residents to understand and comply with the rules. These changes include extending the declaration and payment deadlines, adding new exemptions for medical reasons, and clarifying definitions for self-contained units and employment-related occupancy. The goal is to improve fairness and efficiency in a program designed to encourage housing occupancy in the city.

IE16.5.summary: Toronto City Council adopted a plan to expand public electric vehicle (EV) charging across the city until 2030. This initiative will coordinate city efforts to install more charging stations on public properties, develop funding plans, and ensure consistent operations for these new facilities.

IE16.8.summary: City Council has directed staff to develop a new policy by mid-2025 that will restrict fossil fuel advocacy advertising on city-owned property, like transit shelters and community centres. This decision aims to align city assets with Toronto's climate action plan, TransformTO, by only allowing ads that meet specific environmental goals and have independently verified claims.

PH15.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new rules that will change how infrastructure projects, such as transit lines or utilities, can be built within the city's parks and green spaces. This decision affects the future use and protection of Toronto's public parkland by establishing new policies for these developments.

MM22.9.summary: Toronto City Council has urged the provincial government to continue funding all supervised consumption and treatment services beyond March 31, 2025. Council also requested that the province include these vital services in new homelessness and addiction recovery programs, emphasizing safety and improved access to treatment for community health and well-being.

MM22.14.summary: Toronto City Council has formally endorsed the Ontario Big City Mayors' "Solve the Crisis" campaign, urging the provincial government to take immediate action on the growing mental health, addictions, and homelessness crisis. Council is asking the province to provide sustainable funding to municipalities, appoint a dedicated Minister, and invest in 24/7 community hubs and crisis centres. This move aims to push the province for a coordinated, funded strategy to address these critical issues affecting Toronto communities.

PH15.5.summary: City Council adopted a progress report on Toronto's housing plans, formally requesting billions in funding and partnerships from the provincial and federal governments. This effort aims to build 65,000 new rent-controlled homes by 2030 and provide stable housing and support for residents experiencing homelessness.

TE16.75.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to thoroughly review the rules for garden suites on properties along Parkmount Road that back onto Craven Road in the Danforth area. This detailed study will examine existing regulations, potential changes, and the impact of garden suites on the community, ensuring any future zoning amendments are well-considered before a final decision.

EX17.5.summary: City Council approved extending its agreement with PortsToronto for Billy Bishop Airport, allowing for the construction of new runway safety areas that are critical for aviation safety. This project involves extending land into the lake and requires PortsToronto to meet environmental, community impact, and financing conditions. The agreement term can be extended until 2045 to secure the necessary funding, and the City will discuss updating the airport's master plan to align with broader waterfront goals.

GG16.12.summary: Toronto City Council has postponed making a decision on a contract to GFL Environmental Inc. for curbside waste collection services in District 2. This means that residents across several central and western wards will await a final choice on who will handle their garbage and recycling pickup, with the item sent back to a committee for further review.

EX17.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a work plan to upgrade the shoreside infrastructure at the Jack Layton Ferry Terminal, preparing it for the future electric ferry fleet. This involves an agreement with the Toronto Port Lands Company to manage the design and construction of these essential improvements, aiming to enhance the ferry service and facilities for residents.

TE16.5.summary: City Council has approved zoning and official plan changes for the properties at 214-230 Sherbourne Street. This decision permits a new development project in the area, and Council also requested efforts to include affordable housing and allocate community funds from the project towards local neighbourhood revitalization.

NY17.25.summary: North York Community Council has approved new parking rules for Denrock Drive. The previous blanket 'no parking' restriction has been replaced with 'no standing' zones on most of the street, alongside a specific 17-meter section on the east side where vehicles can park for up to one hour at any time. These changes aim to improve traffic flow and manage vehicle stopping in the area.

HL16.2.summary: The Board of Health has adopted its proposed 2025 operating budget for Toronto Public Health, outlining how the city will fund essential public health services. This budget includes increased funding from the provincial government for health programs and the Healthy Babies Healthy Children Program, and calls for more federal and provincial investment in school food programs.

CC21.3.summary: This item concerns a development proposal at 70-86 Lynn Williams Street. City Council approved a settlement with the developer regarding their zoning application, which was being heard at the Ontario Land Tribunal. As part of this agreement, the developer will provide a minimum 325 square metre park space on the property for public use.

CC21.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a settlement for a development at 4195 Dundas Street West and nearby properties, resolving a dispute at the Ontario Land Tribunal. As part of this agreement, the developer will create and provide a new public park of at least 1,134 square metres to the city at no cost, offering more green space for local residents.

CC20.10.summary: City Council recently received a detailed report outlining strategies to transform how housing is delivered across Toronto. By formally acknowledging this report for information, the city is signaling its ongoing commitment to improve housing construction and availability, potentially influencing future development policies.

EX16.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a foundational agreement with the Province of Ontario to guide the ongoing subway program. This agreement sets the terms for how the city and province will work together on future subway expansion projects, ensuring continued progress on Toronto's transit network.

RM20.1.summary: City Council opened its July 24 and 25 meeting with traditional ceremonies. Members observed moments of silence for the discovery of unmarked graves near a former residential school and to remember recently deceased individuals, followed by a land acknowledgement.

PH14.7.summary: Toronto City Council is asking provincial and national authorities to consider allowing single exit stairs in residential buildings up to six storeys to make mid-rise housing easier to build. The city will also create public guidelines for developers, aiming to increase housing options while ensuring safety for residents and first responders.

MM20.3.summary: To celebrate Taylor Swift's upcoming concerts in November, City Council has approved temporarily renaming streets near the Rogers Centre as "Taylor Swift Way" and lighting the Toronto sign red. The signs will be donated to the Daily Bread Food Bank for auction after the event, with the costs covered by event organizers to avoid using taxpayer funds.

MM20.20.summary: City Council has approved a request to research how major cities worldwide are funded by their provincial or national governments. This study will help Toronto explore new ways to manage its finances and inform discussions about how the city gets funding, particularly as it prepares for the 2025 budget.

MM20.32.summary: Toronto City Council has officially declared April 11 to 17, 2025, as Black Maternal Health Week. This declaration aims to raise awareness about the health and well-being of Black mothers and birthing people, allowing organizers to proceed with planning community events and initiatives.

MM20.33.summary: City Council has approved funding to upgrade the public space around Manor Road Square, located west of Yonge Street. This investment of $318,600 will pay for streetscape improvements, making the area more enjoyable for residents. The funds come from community benefits collected from a nearby development, ensuring growth helps improve local neighbourhoods.

GG14.8.summary: Toronto City Council has approved moving forward with a contract to build two new fully electric ferries for service to the Toronto Islands. This decision also starts the planning process for necessary upgrades to the Jack Layton Ferry Terminal and the islands to support these new eco-friendly vessels, aiming for more reliable and sustainable island transportation.

MM20.24.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan to explore new ways to reduce flooding and better manage heavy rainfall across the city. This involves studying current and potential programs for homeowners to reduce runoff from their properties, increasing natural solutions like rain gardens in public spaces, and assessing how to reduce stormwater from commercial properties. The goal is to protect residents from flood damage and improve water quality.

MM20.30.summary: City Council recently approved changes to Toronto's Multi-Tenant Housing Framework. These updates will exempt certain types of multi-tenant housing (dwelling rooms) from needing to dedicate parkland and will clarify how the framework applies to temporary housing for refugees. This aims to remove hurdles for housing providers and help create more diverse and accessible housing options across the city, including better support for refugee housing initiatives.

PH14.14.summary: City Council decided not to support a request for a provincial order that would fast-track a proposed medical facility and affordable seniors' housing project at 4646 Dufferin Street. This means the development will not bypass standard city planning processes and will need to follow the usual approval procedures.

EX16.20.summary: Toronto City Council has shifted the authority for reviewing and approving development applications, including new subdivisions, condominiums, and site plans. These responsibilities were moved from the Chief Planner to the Executive Director of Development Review to help streamline how new construction projects are processed across the city.

EC14.12.summary: Toronto City Council is advocating for the long-term stability and expansion of childcare services across the city. Council is urging the provincial government to reverse the cancellation of 48 previously approved childcare projects in Toronto schools, which would have created 3,083 new spaces, and to implement a sustainable funding formula for the Canada-wide Early Learning and Child Care system.

IE15.2.summary: City Council has approved a new Master Plan for Toronto Island Park, setting the vision for its improvements and development over the next 25 years. This plan will guide future capital and operating budgets to enhance visitor experience, infrastructure, and ensure continued engagement with Indigenous communities.

IE15.9.summary: To enhance public safety, City Council has asked city staff to study the possibility of installing a permanent barrier on the Leaside Bridge. This initiative aims to help prevent suicide attempts from the bridge, with a report on its feasibility expected by early 2025.

PH14.6.summary: Toronto City Council has approved plans to advance the construction of new affordable rental homes at 777 Victoria Park Avenue. To address concerns about lost commuter parking at the site, the City is exploring options for new paid parking nearby and at the Dentonia Park Golf Course.

DM20.1.summary: City Council approved a settlement for a new building proposal at 5051-5061 Yonge Street, directing staff to support it at the Ontario Land Tribunal. A key part of this settlement removes the requirement for 333.71 square metres of affordable housing from the project.

PH14.13.summary: Toronto City Council is looking into expanding the types of housing allowed in laneway and garden suites, like group homes and multi-tenant homes, by requesting reviews of current zoning rules. This move could create more diverse housing options in residential areas across the city.
