Additionally, the Council decided to remove the option for new garden suites on properties along a specific section of Craven Road.

GG15.1.summary: Toronto's General Government Committee recently adopted By-law 885-2024 to officially confirm all the decisions it made at its July 2, 2024 meeting. This by-law is a procedural step that ensures previously approved actions and policies are legally binding.

IA20.1.summary: Toronto City Council has reviewed an inquiry and response concerning the proposed modular housing project at 39 Dundalk Drive. This means Council considered information about the project without making any new decisions on its approval or funding.

CC20.19.summary: City Council has approved a settlement offer for a revised development proposal at 1053 Don Mills Road and surrounding properties. This decision allows a new plan for the area to move forward, subject to final approvals on zoning details and engineering requirements, which will impact local density and design.

RM20.7.summary: City Council formally approved the agenda for its July 24th and 25th meetings, setting the order for debating important issues like the Gardiner Expressway and accelerating housing development. This procedural decision determines which topics councillors will discuss and vote on, impacting various city services and projects.

EX16.1.summary: City Council has approved a plan to accelerate rehabilitation work on the Gardiner Expressway, specifically the section between Dufferin Street and Strachan Avenue. This decision aims to complete the major repairs faster, but will involve specific traffic changes and temporary closures of the eastbound Spadina off-ramp and an on-ramp from Lake Shore Boulevard West to manage congestion during construction.

EX16.2.summary: Toronto City Council approved a plan to reallocate funds that became available when the Province took over responsibility for the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway. This means the money previously spent on the expressways will now be prioritized for investment in other city infrastructure projects starting with the 2025 budget.

NY15.3.summary: City Council has approved changes to the building rules for 155 Antibes Drive, allowing for a new development. As part of this approval, the developer will provide a 1,050 square meter strip of land along the northern boundary for a new multi-use pathway for the community, adding public space and improving local connectivity.

CC20.8.summary: City Council adopted findings that Councillor Brad Bradford violated the Code of Conduct regarding his use of constituent contact information. As a result, Council formally reprimanded him, reinforcing the importance of ethical conduct for elected officials and maintaining public trust.

CC20.7.summary: City Council agreed that Councillor Frances Nunziata violated the City's Code of Conduct regarding the use of constituent contact information. As a result, Council formally reprimanded the Councillor, upholding accountability for elected officials.

EX16.21.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan for the city to accept funds from corporations and donors to help cover the costs of hosting the 2026 FIFA World Cup. Additionally, the city will accelerate the planning and design for new dedicated transit priority lanes on Dufferin Street and Bathurst Street to improve public transportation.

EX16.5.summary: Toronto City Council has urged the Province of Ontario to reverse its decision to close the Ontario Science Centre and to make necessary urgent repairs to its building. Council also called for the Province to accept donations for immediate fixes and to establish a working group to review the original lease agreement and provincial maintenance obligations. This action aims to keep the Science Centre open and preserve a vital cultural and educational institution for residents.

EX16.7.summary: Toronto City Council granted a complaint concerning development charges for 100 Thorncliffe Park Drive, instructing city staff to refund $400,147.13. This decision means the city will return funds to a developer that were initially collected to help pay for infrastructure and services supporting new growth in the city.

EX16.4.summary: The Executive Committee has deferred a decision on leasing city land at Buttonwood Park for a new Somali Centre for Culture and Recreation. Instead, city staff will continue negotiating the land lease, explore other potential sites, and conduct public consultations with residents before bringing the proposal back for further consideration. This means residents will have an opportunity to provide feedback on the future use of Buttonwood Park for a community centre.

SC15.1.summary: City Council has approved the demolition of eight rental homes at 3291 Kingston Road, but with strict conditions. The property owner must replace these units with eight new rental homes on site, maintaining them as rentals for 20 years. At least these new units must be offered at affordable rents for ten years, and current tenants must receive a relocation and assistance plan including the right to return.

TE15.10.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the necessary planning changes to allow for a new long-term care facility at 400 Broadview Avenue. This decision paves the way for a new care home in the area and includes requests for the developer to work closely with local residents on construction management, like setting up a liaison committee and maintaining a clean site, to minimize neighborhood disruption.

NY15.30.summary: City Council has decided to create a detailed plan for how community spaces are used at the North York Civic Centre. This plan will focus on addressing local needs, ensuring affordability, and will involve input from residents and local school boards. This means the Civic Centre's community spaces could become more accessible and better meet the needs of North York residents.

NY15.4.summary: City Council refused a development application at 1875 Steeles Avenue West that proposed changing zoning rules and demolishing or converting existing rental housing. This decision means the proposed project will not move forward, maintaining the current status of the property.

IE15.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved updated plans for how the city manages winter snow, including new service levels for clearing cycling paths. These changes also allow the General Manager of Transportation Services to temporarily close roads for up to 24 hours during major snow events to quickly clear them, aiming for more efficient snow removal across the city.

PH13.9.summary: City Council adopted a strategy to modernize and grow Toronto's community housing sector. This includes new funding and service agreements to help providers with expiring mortgages repair and revitalize their homes, alongside a new policy for leasing City-owned land to non-profit and Indigenous housing groups. These changes aim to support long-term affordable housing for residents.

EC10.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a 10-year development plan for the Jane Finch community. This plan aims to guide long-term improvements in the area by fostering collaboration between City divisions, residents, and local businesses. It includes establishing a Community Partnership Circle and a Resident Leadership Circle to ensure ongoing local engagement and secure funding for various initiatives.

EY14.1.summary: City Council has adopted the new Jane Finch Secondary Plan and Urban Design Guidelines. This plan will guide future development in the Jane Finch area, outlining policies for new housing, public spaces, community facilities, and building heights to shape the neighbourhood's growth.

IE14.9.summary: Toronto City Council has asked the province for permission to charge gas utilities for their use of public property, a practice already common in other major Canadian cities. The goal is to collect fair payments that could help fund city projects to make infrastructure more resilient against climate change. The City also asked the Ontario Auditor General to investigate Bill 165 and its impact on Toronto residents.

PH13.2.summary: Toronto City Council approved a comprehensive set of plans and rules for developing Villiers Island, a major revitalization project in the Port Lands. This includes updating zoning and land-use guidelines, adopting a detailed development plan, and establishing a master plan for public art, all designed to transform the island into a vibrant new community.

IE14.15.summary: To help prevent basement flooding in the Winston Park Neighbourhood, City Council has decided to advance the upgrade of the underground storage tank at De Havilland 'Mossie' Park. Council also asked Toronto Water for an update on the detailed design and construction timeline for a related project that addresses local flooding.

MM19.2.summary: The City has decided to defend its refusal of a development application for a property at 14 Lacewood Crescent. Council authorized its legal team to represent the City at an appeal hearing and to negotiate a resolution, aiming to uphold local planning rules and the character of the neighbourhood.

MM19.6.summary: City Council approved a plan to honour the late Councillor Jaye Robinson by asking staff to identify suitable parks, streets, or other landmarks in Don Valley West to name after her. This decision recognizes her extensive public service and contributions to the city, with a report on potential locations expected by the end of 2024.

MM19.9.summary: Toronto City Council has urged Ontario's electricity system operator to align its energy planning for the city with Toronto's goal of achieving net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2040. This means phasing out gas-fired electricity generation at the Portlands Energy Centre by 2035 and boosting local renewable energy sources to ensure a reliable and affordable clean power supply for residents.

MM19.19.summary: A proposal calling for mandatory lead disclosure and mitigation in multi-residential housing was sent to the Planning and Housing Committee for further review. This initiative aims to protect residents from lead exposure, particularly in older apartment buildings, by requiring landlords to address potential health risks.

MM19.21.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted measures to combat hate and foster a more inclusive city. This includes re-launching a public education campaign, reviewing urban design for safety, improving rapid response to hate graffiti, and exploring future funding for community gathering spaces like gardens and benches.

MM19.25.summary: Toronto City Council is exploring options to keep the Ontario Science Centre at its current location and support nearby communities. They've asked city staff to assess if the city could operate the Science Centre and to connect with partners offering to fund urgent roof repairs. This move aims to preserve a valuable public institution and ensure community input on its future.

MM19.26.summary: Toronto City Council has requested a detailed plan to enhance the beauty and design quality of public spaces across the city. This initiative aims to create consistent design standards for streets, parks, and new private developments, and will explore establishing a City Designer's office to oversee these improvements, impacting how residents experience their neighbourhoods.

MM19.32.summary: City Council recently discussed a proposal to establish a Toronto International Pizza Festival. Rather than approving it directly, they sent the idea to the Economic and Community Development Committee for further review, meaning residents will need to wait for the committee's decision before knowing if the city will support a new festival.

MM19.34.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to challenge a proposed development at 222 Rosedale Heights Drive. They are directing the City Solicitor to appeal a previous decision and represent the City's opposition at the Toronto Local Appeal Body. This action ensures the city's legal team will work to prevent a project that may not align with community interests.

EX15.4.summary: City Council formally adopted the name change of Yonge-Dundas Square to Sankofa Square. This decision also directs the new Sankofa Square Board to create a multi-year plan for its revitalization, focusing on enhancing programming, safety, and exploring new funding to make the space more vibrant and accessible for residents.

EC13.8.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted an updated protocol for managing encampments in city parks and public spaces. This strategy aims to prioritize broad public access to parks while directing staff to connect people experiencing homelessness to housing and support services. It also urges provincial and federal governments to provide more funding and create a coordinated regional approach to homelessness and asylum seeker arrivals, specifically requesting a $54 million housing benefit allocation.

RM19.7.summary: City Council formalized the agenda for its June 26 and 27 meetings, approving the list of items that would be debated. This decision determined the key issues, such as housing policy, that councillors would discuss and vote on during these sessions.

MM19.8.summary: Toronto will apply to join the Strong Cities Network, an international group focused on making communities safer and more resilient against extremism. City staff will also study the possibility of Toronto hosting a global summit for this network in October 2025, and City Council is asking the provincial and federal governments for financial help to do so.

MM19.11.summary: Toronto City Council has approved steps to strengthen its city-wide approach to preventing youth violence. The plan involves requesting updates on existing community programs, improving youth outreach methods, and engaging community organizations to share best practices for gun violence reduction efforts.

MM19.7.summary: City Council has approved a request to explore naming a community garden after the late Estella Wheeler. City staff will now review options for renaming an existing garden or dedicating a new one in her honour, and report back with recommendations by the end of 2024.

PH13.5.summary: Toronto officials have authorized amendments to contracts with NRB Inc. for the construction of supportive housing at 175 Cummer Avenue and 39 Dundalk Drive. This decision helps advance the city's Modular Housing Initiative, which provides essential homes and supports for people experiencing homelessness.

IE14.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved an update to the city's Cycling Network Plan for 2025-2027. This decision will guide the design and implementation of new bikeway projects across the city, including studies for major city-wide routes, to improve cycling infrastructure for residents.

IE14.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the latest plans to expand the city's cycling network and install missing sidewalks in several areas. This decision will create more dedicated bike lanes and improve pedestrian safety, making it easier and safer for residents to get around actively.

TE14.16.summary: A local heritage property at 135 Roxborough Drive cannot be altered as proposed, specifically regarding painting its masonry. City Council rejected the changes to protect the house's heritage features, and the city's legal team will defend this decision if the property owner appeals.

CC19.1.summary: City Council has declared the Councillor position for Ward 15 - Don Valley West vacant and decided to hold a by-election to fill it. This means residents in Ward 15 will soon get to vote for their new representative. The by-election is expected to cost $550,000, which will be covered by a city reserve fund.

PH13.7.summary: Toronto City Council has taken a step towards protecting tenants from "renovictions" by directing staff to develop a new by-law. This proposed policy would require landlords to get a renovation licence and provide a tenant accommodation and compensation plan before evicting residents for major renovations. The goal is to prevent landlords from unfairly removing tenants and then re-renting at much higher rates.

PH13.3.summary: City Council approved a zoning change for the property at 2444 Eglinton Avenue East, allowing for new housing development. They also requested an analysis to find ways to replace the commuter parking lot that will be lost due to this project.

IE14.5.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a permanent program for "On-Street Logistics Mini-Hubs," allowing courier companies to apply for designated street parking spaces to sort packages and make deliveries. This aims to reduce traffic congestion and improve delivery efficiency across the city, with companies paying an annual fee for each parking spot utilized. The first permanent hub will be on St. George Street, operated by Purolator.

CC19.12.summary: City Council accepted a settlement proposal for significant development projects in Scarborough Centre, specifically for properties like 300 Borough Drive. This agreement determines the city's stance on future growth in these areas, and will be presented to the Ontario Land Tribunal for final approval.

PH13.8.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new program designed to increase the supply of affordable rental homes across the city. This initiative will offer financial incentives, like waiving certain development fees, to eligible rental housing projects and provide funding to community groups to help plan new housing developments, ultimately creating more rent-controlled and affordable options for residents.

EX15.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved an initial $89.5 million to start a new strategy for homelessness services infrastructure, focusing on acquiring properties for shelters and support. The city is also asking the federal government for an additional $674.5 million over ten years to help fund this long-term plan, which aims to improve and expand services for people experiencing homelessness.

EX15.5.summary: City Council has decided to take over the management of Moss Park Arena, transitioning it from an independent Board of Management to being directly operated by the City. This change, expected by the end of 2025, aims to improve community access to arena programs and services, with city staff tasked to develop a detailed transition plan that includes financial impacts.

DI7.2.summary: The Toronto Accessibility Advisory Committee recently received a presentation from Spinal Cord Injury Ontario. This presentation highlighted the challenges people with disabilities face using accessible taxi and vehicle-for-hire services, and provided recommendations to improve these services city-wide. The committee received this information to consider, but no specific decisions or actions were taken.

PH13.4.summary: Toronto's Planning and Housing Committee is moving ahead with plans to make it easier to build mid-rise buildings along major streets, known as Avenues. This involves drafting new zoning rules to allow for taller and denser buildings, as well as updating urban design standards, all aimed at creating more housing across the city. The committee also asked for a review of specific planning rules for garden suites on Craven Road.

SC14.18.summary: City Council has approved a new traffic rule prohibiting eastbound right turns on a red light at the intersection of Brimley Road and McNicoll Avenue. This change aims to improve safety for all road users, including pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers, especially given that TTC transit operates through this busy Scarborough intersection.

SC14.5.summary: City Council approved a zoning change for properties on Kingston Road and Galloway Road, allowing for new development. This decision ensures that 80 existing rental units will be preserved for at least 20 years and requires the owner to make significant improvements to these buildings at no cost to tenants. A plan to reduce construction impacts on current residents is also mandated.

EY14.2.summary: Toronto City Council approved changes to the Official Plan and zoning rules for the Jane Finch Mall site at 1911 Finch Avenue West. This decision paves the way for a major redevelopment of the property, which will include new housing and aims to bring affordable rental housing and community services to the Jane Finch neighbourhood.

GG13.2.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to remove nearly $9 million in uncollectible funds from its financial records. This includes outstanding property taxes from 182 accounts and over $6 million in Provincial Offences Act fines. This action helps the city clean up its books by acknowledging amounts it is no longer able to collect.

PA9.10.summary: The Toronto Parking Authority's Board has decided to keep confidential the legal advice regarding its ongoing lawsuit. The lawsuit seeks $7 million in damages from a developer for a broken contract concerning TPA's option to buy a retail unit at 935 Eglinton Avenue West.

PA9.9.summary: The Toronto Parking Authority Board approved confidential instructions to staff regarding a legal dispute. This dispute concerns unpaid rent for units at 50 Cumberland Street, with the details being kept private due to ongoing or potential litigation affecting the authority's property leases.

PA9.8.summary: Toronto's Parking Authority Board recently received an update on various ongoing legal disputes related to its operations, including commercial parking, property management, and Bike Share Toronto. These matters involve issues like supplier disagreements and personal injuries, which the Authority manages with insurance and legal counsel. The specific details of these legal cases remain confidential to protect the city's interests.

IE14.14.summary: Toronto's Infrastructure Committee approved an update on plans for a new sanitary sewer on Ridgewood Road, directing staff to minimize impacts on properties and trees. Residents will be actively involved throughout 2025 with mailed notices, walkabouts, and public meetings to discuss the design and timelines for the sewer, which will be paid for by property owners through a special charge.

HL13.3.summary: The Board of Health approved grants totaling over $2.5 million from the Toronto Urban Health Fund to 24 community projects focusing on HIV prevention, substance use, and harm reduction. This funding supports essential health services for residents, especially those facing linguistic, cultural, or geographic barriers. The city will also work with Indigenous groups to redesign how health funds are allocated to Indigenous-led initiatives.

NY13.3.summary: New zoning was approved for properties at 824 Sheppard Avenue West and Cocksfield Avenue to permit a new development. To support housing, the developer must provide fifteen replacement rental units on the site, all of which must be maintained at affordable rents for at least ten years.

IE13.1.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to allow low-speed vehicles (LSVs) on city streets as part of a provincial pilot project, but will not permit them to be used as taxis or ride-share vehicles. This means residents might see new types of small vehicles like golf carts used for local trips or deliveries, while electric kick-scooters remain prohibited in the city.

IE13.9.summary: Toronto City Council is looking into creating 1,000 new murals across the city and exploring new graffiti removal blitzes. They asked city staff to report on expanding the StreetARToronto program with community partners and to update on city-wide efforts to prevent vandalism and beautify public spaces.

MM18.17.summary: City Council has sent a proposal about the future of visitor parking to the Planning and Housing Committee for further review. This means the committee will study potential changes to how visitor parking operates across the city, which could affect residents and their guests.

MM18.18.summary: To ensure Canada Day celebrations are accessible city-wide, Council asked city staff to propose a plan for fairly funding and distributing these events across all Toronto neighborhoods for 2025. This decision aims to make sure every part of the city has equitable access to community celebrations.

MM18.19.summary: City Council has asked city staff to prepare a report on how cultural events are currently funded and distributed across Toronto. This report will also include a plan to boost funding for cultural events in growing neighbourhoods outside the downtown core, aiming to ensure more residents have access to local cultural programming.

MM18.20.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to study whether Moss Park Arena should be run directly by the City instead of its current Board of Management. This review will look into the financial costs, operational changes, and staffing needs, which could affect how the arena serves the community and its users.

MM18.24.summary: Toronto City Council has formally asked the Ontario government to implement commercial rent control for small, locally-owned businesses. This move aims to protect main street shops, making them more affordable and stable by also requesting a dispute resolution tribunal and standardized leases for commercial tenants and landlords.

PH12.3.summary: City Council approved major changes to the city's Official Plan and zoning rules to allow more housing options along many main streets across Toronto. This decision aims to increase housing supply by permitting a wider range of building types, though some specific areas were excluded from these new rules due to unique local conditions.

TE13.8.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the local building rules for 1400 Gerrard Street East. This decision allows for a new development at this site, but the owner must first address city requirements for engineering, waste, and stormwater management. Council also requested a review of how the new development could impact local street parking for residents.

MM18.22.summary: To boost professional women's soccer and improve local sports facilities, Toronto City Council has decided to negotiate agreements for two key sites. The city will work with Association Football Club Toronto for the use of the future Centennial Park soccer field, and will also seek proposals for a new operator for Lamport Stadium. These steps aim to attract capital investment for stadium upgrades, support professional women's sports, and ensure ongoing community access to recreation.

IE13.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved major upgrades for Eglinton Avenue, creating a "complete street" from Bicknell Avenue to Mount Pleasant Road. This project aims to improve travel for drivers, transit riders, and pedestrians, and also includes new bike lanes on nearby streets to enhance cycling options.

MM18.5.summary: Toronto's City Council has requested a detailed plan to speed up ongoing rehabilitation work on the Gardiner Expressway. This plan, due by July, will explore options like 24/7 construction and off-site pre-fabrication to reduce traffic congestion and complete necessary repairs faster, while also considering impacts on nearby communities and other road projects.

TE13.6.summary: City Council has approved updated zoning rules for new construction at 1095 and 1111 Danforth Avenue. This decision includes a request to explore excluding the new development from permit parking, and directs any developer fees from the project towards creating affordable housing in Ward 14.

TE13.7.summary: New building rules for 1117 Danforth Avenue have been approved by City Council, permitting the demolition of 27 social housing units. This approval mandates that the developer build an equal number of replacement social housing units on the site, which must be kept affordable for decades, while also assisting current tenants.

CC18.12.summary: Toronto City Council has directed city staff to advocate for changes to planning rules for a segment of Danforth Avenue, specifically from the Don Valley to Coxwell Avenue. These changes would allow for taller buildings, reduced setbacks, and potentially more shadows, but only if new developments include affordable housing, exceed green building standards, and don't negatively impact public spaces or surrounding neighbourhoods. This approach will be proposed during a legal appeal related to the area's official plan, potentially shaping future development along this stretch of Danforth.

MM18.10.summary: City Council has approved changes to its operations policy, enhancing transparency for residents. For any expenses that require Council approval, the City Clerk will now publicly provide all supporting documentation, including receipts, as part of the agenda item.

MM18.16.summary: Toronto City Council has directed city staff to create a plan aimed at protecting residents' rights to practice their faith and gather freely around places of worship and community centres. This initiative seeks to ensure Torontonians can access these spaces without experiencing intimidation or harassment, and will involve relevant city departments and the police.

IA18.1.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to send the discussion about renaming Yonge-Dundas Square to its Executive Committee. This means the proposal will be reviewed by that committee first, postponing a final decision by the full Council on whether the square's name will change.

EX14.11.summary: Toronto City Council recently updated its Fair Wage Policy and the official schedule of wage rates for various trades working on city contracts. This update, effective July 1, 2024, ensures that workers performing services or construction for the City are paid fair and competitive wages. It aims to support local employment and the consistent delivery of quality city services.

PH12.8.summary: Toronto City Council has requested a review of rules for parking work vehicles outdoors on private property in residential areas. This review will consider the needs of vehicle operators to park near their homes and the impacts of COVID-19 on work arrangements, with a report expected by early 2025.

PH12.7.summary: Toronto City Council has provided feedback to the provincial government regarding a new bill and planning rules designed to speed up home construction. The City specifically urged the Province not to broadly limit the public's ability to appeal development decisions, unless the project is for social or affordable housing. This aims to protect community input on new buildings while still supporting housing initiatives.

EX14.6.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted its 2024 Corporate Asset Management Plan. This plan outlines how the city will strategically manage, maintain, and invest in its vast network of public infrastructure, ensuring city assets like roads, buildings, and parks continue to serve residents effectively.

PH12.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the Downsview Secondary Plan, guiding the future development of the Downsview area. This comprehensive plan includes requirements for new housing, with targets for affordable rental housing increasing by 1.5% each year, as well as a minimum of 3 hectares of new public parkland and new childcare centres, shaping a vibrant new community.

EC12.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the Downsview Community Development Plan and a related 5-year action plan. This means future growth and services in the Downsview area will be guided by this framework, with a focus on partnering with diverse community groups and residents for implementation.

EC12.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved nearly $1.9 million in grants for six local arts organizations across the city. This funding will support arts programs that reach residents in various communities, with a new focus on engaging low-income Torontonians, and future plans to ensure fair funding distribution for these groups.

NY13.1.summary: North York Community Council has denied a request to remove a healthy Colorado blue spruce tree at 2 Hawksdale Road. This decision upholds Toronto's Tree By-laws, which protect healthy trees even when they conflict with a planned driveway or alleged allergies, meaning the tree will remain.

PB18.7.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to reject a developer's plan to demolish and alter parts of the designated heritage property at 123 Wynford Drive. This decision protects the culturally and architecturally significant former Japanese Canadian Cultural Centre, designed by Raymond Moriyama, from a proposed residential development. The City will also explore nominating the site as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

CC17.1.summary: Toronto City Council has waived the penalty fee for residents who missed the 2023 Vacant Home Tax declaration deadline, and those who paid it will receive a refund. For properties that didn't declare their status, the city will use their 2022 occupancy status for 2023, sending new notices that still allow homeowners to correct the information. The Council also directed staff to report back on a completely redesigned Vacant Home Tax program for 2024, focusing on better communication and user experience to improve the system.

MM17.9.summary: Toronto City Council has asked the provincial Minister of Environment, Conservation and Parks to require a comprehensive environmental assessment for Atura Power's proposal. This request is for a thorough review of plans to expand the Portlands Energy Centre's capacity by 50 megawatts, ensuring potential impacts on the environment and local residents are carefully studied.

PH11.13.summary: City Council recently reviewed a proposal to protect 1111 Danforth Avenue as a heritage property, which would safeguard its historical or architectural features from significant changes. However, Council decided to take no further action on this proposal, meaning the property will not be designated for heritage protection by the city at this time.

PH11.8.summary: Toronto City Council has updated its RentSafeTO program to improve living conditions in apartment buildings and clarify responsibilities for landlords. The changes include new requirements for pest control logs, ensuring tenant notice boards are clear, and allowing the city to charge landlords for targeted visits and remedial inspections. These updates aim to better protect tenants and ensure landlords maintain their buildings.

EC11.4.summary: Following a pilot program, Toronto City Council has permanently approved allowing personal alcohol consumption in 45 specific parks across the city. This means residents can now legally drink alcohol in these public spaces, with certain health and safety restrictions in place.

EX13.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved moving forward with plans for a Somali Centre for Culture and Recreation, requesting financial support from the Provincial and Federal governments. City staff will negotiate an agreement and identify potential sites, also exploring opportunities to include affordable housing in the city's northwest.

EX13.8.summary: City Council has set the property tax rates that fund education in Toronto for 2024, as required by provincial regulations. These rates will generate over $2.15 billion for school purposes, with the City retaining a small portion of these funds. This decision directly impacts the education portion of property tax bills for Toronto homeowners and businesses.

MM17.8.summary: City Council has reinstated the northbound left-turn restrictions at the intersection of Old Forest Hill Road and Eglinton Avenue West during weekday morning and afternoon rush hours. This decision reverses an earlier one and aims to better manage traffic flow for residents in the area. The city will also study local neighbourhood traffic and install advance warning signs for drivers.

PH11.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the first chapter of "Our Plan Toronto," the city's updated master plan that guides future development and land use. This foundational chapter sets the vision and principles for how the city will grow, including new directives to prioritize reducing fossil fuel use and address anti-Black racism, gentrification, and displacement in future planning decisions.

EC11.3.summary: City Council has extended its pilot program for large multi-passenger pedalled vehicles, or 'pedicabs,' until 2032, allowing more of these vehicles to operate with updated rules. The city will now issue an unlimited number of annual licenses and will create a public list of streets where these pedicabs are not allowed to help manage traffic and ensure safety.

PH11.10.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted new rules to better protect tenants when rental buildings are demolished for redevelopment. The city will now use more up-to-date and indexed market rent data to calculate financial assistance for displaced tenants, ensuring they receive fair compensation. Council also reiterated its request for the Province of Ontario to reinstate rent control for all rental units and uphold the city's authority to mandate replacement rental housing.

MM17.25.summary: City Council has approved directing the city's legal team to represent its position before an independent tribunal regarding a property at 30 Lakeside Avenue. The lawyers will argue to uphold a local planning decision that rejected a development proposal for the site, and are also authorized to negotiate a resolution. This ensures the city actively defends local planning choices that can affect neighborhood development.

TE12.5.summary: City Council approved changes to planning rules for 171-175 Lowther Avenue, paving the way for a new development. This approval includes requirements for the owner to provide a tenant relocation plan with financial compensation for three existing rental units and to dedicate off-site parkland to the city.

EY12.16.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new parking rules for Longbourne Drive. Residents living on the south side of the street between Willowridge Road and Redgrave Drive will now need a permit to park overnight from 12:01 a.m. to 7:00 a.m., with some exceptions. Parking is now banned at all times on the north side of the street, changing how residents can park near their homes.

EY12.17.summary: Drivers on a section of Burnhamthorpe Road will need to slow down as the city has reduced the speed limit from 50 km/h to 40 km/h. This change, applying between Kipling Avenue and Dundas Street West, aims to make the road safer for all residents, including pedestrians and cyclists.

EX13.1.summary: City Council has decided to advocate for more funding for student nutrition programs across Toronto. This includes urging the provincial government to access the new $1 billion federal National School Food Program, increase their financial support to match the City's contribution, and cover rising food costs. City staff will also develop plans to expand nutritious meal programs in schools and summer camps if additional funding becomes available.

IE12.1.summary: City Council has approved changes to how Toronto handles blue box recycling and general waste collection as the Blue Box program transitions to a new province-wide model. This means producers are now responsible for recycling their packaging, and the City will continue to manage blue box materials at its facilities while also seeking new companies for future waste and blue box pickup services and selling its blue bins.

CC17.6.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the heritage-designated property at 4888 Dundas Street West. This allows a new mixed-use building to be constructed on the site, provided that the owner agrees to protect the historical elements through a conservation plan and a special agreement with the city.

CC17.7.summary: Toronto City Council has agreed to a settlement regarding a development proposal at 4884-4896 Dundas Street West, which was under appeal at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This means the city will support a compromise, allowing the project to move forward once the developer meets specific conditions related to infrastructure, stormwater management, and traffic plans.

RM17.1.summary: City Council officially opened its meeting on April 17th. The session began with O Canada, a moment of silence to honour several individuals who passed away, and a land acknowledgement recognizing Indigenous Peoples and treaties.

IE11.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved an increase to the fines for various parking offences across the city, with these changes taking effect on August 1, 2024. Additionally, new rules are now in place for off-street electric vehicle parking spaces, requiring vehicles to be actively charging to park there and preventing non-EVs from using these spots.

CA15.2.summary: City Council has appointed Liane Kim as a public member to the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) Board, serving until June 16, 2027. This appointment ensures ongoing oversight and decision-making for Toronto's public transit system.

EY12.7.summary: City Council has rejected an application to amend planning rules for a new home at 12 Hillside Road. This decision protects a designated natural area, ensuring that development aligns with the city's environmental and planning guidelines. If the developer appeals, the city will defend its refusal at the Ontario Land Tribunal.

PH11.9.summary: To protect long-term housing, Toronto City Council has strengthened rules for short-term rentals like Airbnb. These changes require operators to submit extra documents to prove their rental is their principal residence, clarify that secondary suites on the same property cannot be rented out short-term, and allow the city to request in-person interviews or inspections for compliance.

NY12.12.summary: North York Community Council approved the demolition of five detached houses located at 1837-1845 Bayview Avenue. This decision clears the way for new development in the area, potentially changing the local streetscape, with the approval including conditions for site cleanup after demolition.

NY12.19.summary: North York Community Council decided not to install all-way stop signs at the intersection of Broadlands Boulevard and Castlegrove Boulevard. City staff reviewed the location and determined that it did not meet the necessary criteria for all-way stop control, meaning traffic flow will remain unchanged for residents in the area.

NY12.26.summary: North York Community Council approved an exemption for the property owner at 31 Norden Crescent, allowing their existing pool fence and gate to remain as constructed. This means the fence can stay, despite not fully meeting current city safety rules regarding how close the gate is to climbable surfaces, but any future replacement must comply with those rules.

NY12.27.summary: The North York Community Council has approved a homeowner's request to remove a private black walnut tree at 173 Homewood Avenue. This decision requires the homeowner to plant five new trees or pay money to the city instead, addressing safety concerns despite the tree being healthy.

PB16.14.summary: The Toronto Preservation Board has approved changes to heritage properties at 650-664 Yonge Street, allowing for the construction of a 75-storey mixed-use tower. This decision permits some demolition of existing heritage elements while requiring the developer to protect others through a heritage agreement, balancing new development with historical preservation.

PB16.8.summary: The City's Preservation Board has adopted confidential recommendations regarding a proposed 29-storey development at 4888 Dundas Street West, which involves a designated heritage building. This decision addresses a settlement offer from the developer and will determine how the historic property is integrated into the new construction, impacting the character of the Etobicoke – Lakeshore neighbourhood.

PB16.6.summary: City Council intends to designate properties at 101 Mildenhall Road and 294, 306, 318 Lawrence Avenue East as heritage sites. This decision will protect buildings on the Toronto French School campus, including its original Moderne-style facility, preserving a piece of the city's architectural and cultural history.

PB16.16.summary: City Council has approved plans for a new 85-storey tower at 374-378 Yonge Street, allowing changes to existing heritage buildings on the site. This decision means a major new mixed-use development will proceed in the downtown core, while also putting in place protections for the historic elements of these properties.

PB16.9.summary: Toronto's Preservation Board is recommending that City Council approve plans to alter the heritage property at 171 Lowther Avenue, allowing an 11-storey residential building to be constructed. This decision would permit significant new development while requiring a Heritage Easement Agreement to protect the site's historical elements.

PB16.12.summary: The city has approved plans to build a 56-storey tower at 36-42 Maitland Street, which involves making changes and removing parts of two existing heritage buildings. This decision allows for new development while requiring the developer to protect important historical features through a special heritage agreement.

PB16.13.summary: The Toronto Preservation Board has recommended refusing proposed alterations to the William McBean Terrace at 68 Wellesley Street East. This decision aims to protect the designated 19th-century heritage property from changes linked to a new 28-storey development. It ensures the preservation of important historical architecture in the city.

PB16.11.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to maintain the heritage designation for the historic house at 64 Woodlawn Avenue West, built in 1906 by architect Eden Smith. This decision rejects the property owner's request to remove the designation and includes plans to amend the existing heritage protections, ensuring the continued preservation of this significant property.

NY11.2.summary: City Council approved changes to the Official Plan and Zoning By-laws for the property at 72 Church Avenue in Willowdale. This decision allows for a new development on the site, with a requirement to build a privacy fence for existing neighbours.

EX12.4.summary: City Council has approved a plan to review its properties, including parking lots and land around TTC stations, to identify opportunities for new affordable housing and community spaces. This initiative aims to make better use of public land, improve the city's financial health, and create more complete neighbourhoods across Toronto.

MM16.4.summary: City Council has sent a proposal called the "Night Economy Manifesto" to the Economic and Community Development Committee for further review. This means the committee will study ideas for improving Toronto's nightlife, cultural activities, and businesses that operate after hours, before any final decisions are made.

MM16.14.summary: A proposal to revisit City Council's Vision Zero road safety plans was not considered by Council. This means the idea of adding optional resident polling for traffic changes will not be debated or decided upon at this time.

MM16.15.summary: City Council is exploring clearer rules for posters in public spaces, like those for events or political messages. Instead of making an immediate decision, a proposal to clarify poster enforcement policies and bylaws was sent to the Economic and Community Development Committee for further review.

MM16.19.summary: Toronto City Council recently approved donating up to five surplus vehicles from its Fleet and Paramedic Services to the Ukrainian government for humanitarian purposes. The city also requested its agencies, like the TTC and Toronto Police, to consider donating their own end-of-life vehicles, and will provide three generators to Ukraine to support ongoing humanitarian efforts.

PH10.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new program aimed at preserving and improving affordable rental housing by supporting renovations in multi-tenant homes, commonly known as rooming houses. The program will offer grant funding to operators for upgrades and may waive certain permit fees for up to 250 rooms, helping to ensure safer and better living conditions for tenants.

EC10.2.summary: To enhance public safety and prevent serious dog attacks, City Council has approved creating a public, searchable online record of dogs that have received Dangerous Dog Orders. This new tool will inform residents about dangerous dog incidents in their area and will be supported by a proposed $500,000 public education campaign in the 2025 budget.

PH10.11.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a request to the provincial government to issue a special zoning order for a new mixed-use development at 4000 Leslie Street in North York. This decision helps fast-track the creation of 528 new long-term care beds, along with medical offices and retail space, to serve the community. The Council also ensures city staff will work with the developer and the province on project agreements and oversight.

PH10.13.summary: Toronto City Council is asking the Toronto Catholic District School Board to review its fees for schools and consider exempting secondary units, like basement apartments, from these charges. This move aims to make it easier to create more diverse housing options across the city.

MM16.24.summary: City Council has approved accepting an in-kind donation from Pride Toronto to upgrade Beach Road near Hanlan's Point Beach in Toronto Island Park. This decision allows the city to partner with Pride Toronto to refurbish the road, creating a "Rainbow Road" and improving a popular public space for residents and visitors.

MM16.25.summary: Toronto City Council approved funding for several improvements at Hanlan's Beach, including new bike parking, fencing to protect natural habitats, and better access trails. These projects, aimed at enhancing visitor experience and preserving the environment, will be fully paid for by developer contributions collected for parkland.

MM16.50.summary: City Council is taking steps to help Toronto's small businesses better protect themselves from break-ins. They have asked for a review of current city programs to see how they can better support businesses with security upgrades like cameras and improved locks, and will also ask the provincial and federal governments for funding for a new security grant program.

MM16.48.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to send an urgent motion about the auto theft crisis to its Economic and Community Development Committee for further review. This means the proposal, which calls for immediate action and stiffer penalties to address rising auto theft, will be discussed by the committee before potentially returning to Council for a decision.

IA16.2.summary: City Council decided not to take any action on renaming the Dundas Toronto Transit Commission stations. This means the stations will keep their current names for the time being.

NY11.6.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to officially oppose a developer's proposed plans for a new project at 1053 Don Mills Road and The Donway East before the Ontario Land Tribunal. The City will also continue discussions with the developer to resolve outstanding concerns about the project's impact on local infrastructure and services, ensuring any future development is appropriate for the area.

PH9.12.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to begin the process of officially recognizing the properties at 78, 80, and 86 Mimico Avenue as heritage sites. This designation will help protect their historical and architectural significance under the Ontario Heritage Act, affecting any future changes or development on these properties.

EX12.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved further steps to prepare for hosting the FIFA World Cup 2026. This decision allows city staff to secure funding from federal and provincial governments and finalize agreements with airports, moving Toronto closer to being ready for the international event. Council also approved a budget for legal services related to the World Cup planning and operations.

EX12.5.summary: City Council has outlined a new approach for planning major transit projects, confirming that keeping the existing system in good repair is the most important investment priority. The City will review its official plans for future rapid transit corridors and road widths by late 2025, which will shape where new subways or LRTs could be built and how surface transit is prioritized. This also includes advocating for the Sheppard Subway extension and planning a Finch West LRT extension towards Pearson Airport.

EX12.9.summary: City Council has extended the deadline for homeowners to declare their property's occupancy status for the 2023 Vacant Home Tax. The new due date for submitting declarations is March 15, 2024, providing residents more time to comply and avoid potential penalties.

PH10.12.summary: Toronto City Council approved minor technical corrections to the rules governing multi-tenant housing, also known as rooming houses. These updates clarify the city's zoning by-law for these types of homes, ensuring consistent regulations across the city and impacting housing options for residents.

TE11.29.summary: Toronto City Council has approved various organizations to host events at Nathan Phillips Square from May 1 to August 31, 2024. This means residents can look forward to a busy spring and summer at the square, with some events permitted to sell alcohol and others allowed to collect donations for their causes.

PB15.2.summary: City Council intends to designate the historic homes at 6 and 8 Cawthra Square as heritage properties, protecting these 1892 Queen Anne Revival-style houses from significant alterations or demolition. This action helps preserve Toronto's architectural legacy and character within the Church-Wellesley village area.

PB15.11.summary: A major development project was approved for 625 Yonge Street, allowing alterations to several heritage properties in the block to make way for a new 70-storey tower. This decision permits significant changes to the historic streetscape while ensuring a heritage easement agreement is put in place.

PB15.13.summary: Toronto's Preservation Board adopted confidential recommendations concerning a development proposal at 1779-1787 Bayview Avenue, which will now go to City Council for approval. These recommendations, prepared by the City Solicitor, relate to heritage concerns and ongoing appeals to the Ontario Land Tribunal, guiding the city's legal strategy against a developer's plans. This decision affects the future of a significant site that includes properties near the Leaside LRT station.

PB15.10.summary: The Toronto Preservation Board recently considered a plan to allow alterations and the demolition of interior features at 49 and 51 Yonge Street to make way for a 62-storey mixed-use building. The Board ultimately chose not to recommend approval of these changes to City Council. This means City Council will need to decide how to balance new high-rise development with the preservation of heritage properties at this downtown location.

CA14.2.summary: City Council has appointed Robin Pilkey as a public member to the PortsToronto Board of Directors for a three-year term. This ensures a public voice on the board that oversees key city assets like the Port of Toronto and Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport.

IE11.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved updates to its Congestion Management Plan, aiming to improve traffic flow and safety across the city. The plan includes developing strategies for better enforcement of blocked intersections and bike lanes, exploring new fees for road occupancy, and considering peak hour delivery restrictions. It also involves a pilot for accessible construction information and new transportation technology trials.

EY11.5.summary: Residents on Kingsway Crescent can expect a new speed hump to be installed between Bannon Avenue and Government Road. The Etobicoke York Community Council approved this measure to help slow traffic and improve safety in the area.

NY11.13.summary: North York Community Council has approved the demolition of four single-family homes at 26, 28, 36, and 38 Hounslow Avenue. The approval comes with conditions for the developers, including ensuring the site is maintained after demolition and hosting three community engagement events before new construction starts.

TE11.15.summary: Toronto's East York Community Council rejected a homeowner's appeal to create front yard parking at 63 Hammersmith Avenue. This decision means the homeowner cannot legally park in their front yard and must instead convert a paved area back into greenspace, upholding city rules that protect neighbourhood green spaces and manage parking.

SC11.1.summary: City Council has approved a zoning change for the properties located at 2157 to 2183 Lawrence Avenue East. This decision paves the way for new development on these lands, which will change the type and scale of buildings allowed in this part of Scarborough.

SC11.10.summary: City Council has approved extending the temporary closure of the east sidewalk on McCowan Road, between Sheppard Avenue East and Nugget Avenue, until December 31, 2024. This allows Metrolinx to continue tunnel construction for the Scarborough Subway Extension, with the City requiring the sidewalk to be fully restored to its original condition or better once the project is complete.

SC11.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the local building rules for properties at 4566 and 4568 Kingston Road in Scarborough. This decision paves the way for a developer to construct a new 12-storey building that combines homes and businesses, shaping the neighbourhood's future development.

MPB15.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved its 2024 Operating and Capital Budgets, setting out how the city will collect and spend money for the year. This decision funds essential services, with specific amendments made to support areas like arts and culture, economic development, parks, and community safety.

CC15.1.summary: City Council approved the implementation of the 2024 budget, establishing new property tax rates for the year. This decision means residential property owners will see an 8% increase, while an additional special levy will help fund priority transit and housing projects across the city.

IE10.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new process for handling tickets from red light cameras and automated speed enforcement, effective November 1, 2024. Instead of going through the provincial court system, these violations will now be managed by the city's own administrative penalty system, including appeals to the Administrative Penalty Tribunal. This change aims to make the ticket resolution process more efficient.

RM14.1.summary: This item formally opened a City Council meeting, beginning with a land acknowledgment and a moment of silence to honour community members who recently passed away. This is a standard procedural item that kicks off every Council meeting.

EC9.4.summary: To ensure stable and adequate spaces for residents experiencing homelessness, the City has approved a real estate strategy for its shelter and support services. This allows staff to extend leases for current facilities and acquire new properties, ensuring critical infrastructure for these vital programs.

PH9.1.summary: City Council has approved new zoning rules for specific apartment areas in Midtown, as part of the "Ready, Set, Midtown" review. These changes will guide how new apartment buildings can be developed in these neighbourhoods, affecting their future growth and housing options.

MM14.4.summary: City Council has referred a proposal to the Executive Committee that aims to establish a fairer way to calculate residential property tax rates for Toronto Island residents. This referral means the committee will now study the issue, which could lead to future changes in how these taxes are applied.

MM14.14.summary: Councillors proposed a motion to end city subsidies for new natural gas pipelines, aiming to prevent increases in residents' energy costs. Instead of making a decision, City Council referred this proposal to its Executive Committee for further consideration, meaning a decision on the subsidies is delayed.

IE10.7.summary: Toronto City Council has directed the City Manager to create new guidelines for managing and monitoring rallies and protests, specifically addressing safety concerns on highway overpasses. This policy will ensure respect for human rights while clarifying that police are responsible for enforcement and require adequate funding for this role.

EC9.5.summary: City Council has updated several definitions within Toronto's Noise By-law. These changes clarify what counts as noise, how it's measured, and how certain exemptions are handled, which helps ensure more consistent enforcement and understanding for residents and businesses across the city.

IE10.3.summary: Toronto's City Council has directed Transportation Services to ensure that designated bike lanes are safe and passable for cyclists during the winter season. This decision aims to improve winter cycling conditions, making it safer and more practical for residents to use bikes year-round.

IE10.2.summary: City Council has approved the installation of new bike lanes on several streets across Toronto as part of the 2024 Cycling Network Plan. This decision aims to expand the city's cycling infrastructure and improve safety for residents, with a priority on placing lanes off the main roadway where possible.

IE10.6.summary: To make transportation more accessible, City Council approved providing reduced-fare annual Bike Share Toronto memberships to eligible low-income residents. This decision helps people participating in the Fair Pass program access affordable bike rentals, expanding their transportation options.

CC14.10.summary: Toronto City Council approved a settlement for a major development in Scarborough, affecting properties near St. Clair Avenue East, Danforth Road, and Kennedy Road. This decision means the developer will contribute significantly to the community, including up to $2 million for public art, a new recreation centre, and potential affordable housing, shaping the future of these neighbourhoods.

MM14.9.summary: Toronto City Council approved a plan to bring back tobogganing on 45 hills across the city, replacing "no tobogganing" signs with warnings and re-installing hay bales for safety. This decision means future bans will require approval from local councillors and the Community Council, making it harder to prohibit the activity, and the City is also asking the Province to review liability laws.

MM14.17.summary: Toronto City Council is sending the City's legal team to the Ontario Land Tribunal to represent its interests regarding a development application at 717-733 Mount Pleasant Road. The City wants to ensure that its planning recommendations are upheld if the project is approved, or to oppose the application if the plans change in a way that staff do not support. This decision helps ensure local development follows city guidelines and protects the neighbourhood.

NY10.5.summary: Toronto City Council has approved plans for a new development at 3 Swift Drive, allowing the demolition of 61 existing rental units. This decision requires the developer to build 61 new rental homes on the site and dedicate over 500 square metres for a new public park, providing both housing and green space for the community in Don Valley East.

EY10.5.summary: City Council has decided to formally oppose a developer's request to change planning rules for a property at 4884-4896 Dundas Street West before the Ontario Land Tribunal. This means the city is fighting against the proposed development's current design and has set strict conditions for infrastructure, traffic, and stormwater management that must be met if the provincial tribunal ultimately approves it. This protects local services and ensures any future development fits community needs.

EX11.8.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted the RapidTO Surface Transit Network Plan, which aims to improve surface transit across the city. This decision endorses the plan in principle and directs city staff to start detailed studies and public consultations for potential rapid transit improvements on specific sections of Finch Avenue East, Dufferin Street, Lawrence Avenue East, and Steeles Avenue West. These efforts will help speed up bus and streetcar service, making daily commutes faster and more reliable for residents.

DM14.1.summary: City Council approved covering the travel costs for Councillor Nick Mantas and his Chief of Staff to attend a conference in Italy, using funds from the Councillor's existing office budget. This decision also directed the City Clerk to review current policies on travel expenses for Council members' staff, aiming for more clarity and accountability in future travel arrangements.

DM14.2.summary: City Council recently considered a proposal to explore or resume distributing paper copies of meeting materials to councillors. However, Council ultimately decided not to adopt this change. This means councillors will continue to primarily use digital formats for their meeting documents, avoiding potential new printing costs.

EX11.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new 10% Municipal Non-Resident Speculation Tax on residential properties bought by foreign buyers, effective January 1, 2025. This new tax aims to discourage foreign speculation in the housing market and could help make home ownership more accessible for Toronto residents.

EX11.7.summary: City Council has approved the final steps to set up the operating agreement for the new Finch West Light Rail Transit (LRT) line. This means the city will finalize an agreement with Metrolinx and the TTC to manage how the trains will run, ensuring the new transit service can begin once construction is complete.

PH9.14.summary: Toronto's Planning and Housing Committee rejected a proposal to make fraternities and sororities follow the new Multi-Tenant Housing By-law, which aims to ensure safe and accountable shared living conditions. This decision means these student residences will not be required to get a city licence or meet the same standards as other multi-tenant houses, potentially leaving students with fewer protections and neighbours without a clear way to address ongoing concerns.

SC10.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for the properties at 2031 and 2301 Kennedy Road in Scarborough. This decision allows for new types of development or altered uses for these lands, impacting the future shape of the Agincourt neighbourhood.

SC10.5.summary: City Council has approved the demolition of 68 rental homes on Glen Everest Road in Scarborough. This approval comes with conditions requiring the developer to build 68 new rental units on the same site, with many designated as affordable and mid-range for at least 10 years, and to provide support for existing tenants to relocate.

EY10.12.summary: To make a residential street safer, the Etobicoke York Community Council has approved the installation of speed humps on Kingsway Crescent. This decision aims to reduce vehicle speeds and improve safety for pedestrians and residents on this local street, which has many driveways and limited sidewalks.

MM13.11.summary: Toronto's City Council recently decided to rename the stadium at Centennial Park to the "Rob Ford Stadium." This decision bypasses the city's usual naming policy and will require new signage to be designed and installed at the public sports facility.

EC8.14.summary: Toronto City Council recently adopted a plan to support and grow the city's night economy, which includes businesses like bars, restaurants, and entertainment venues operating after hours. The plan focuses on improving communication and ensuring diverse voices, including residents, gig workers, unions, and businesses from all areas of the city, are included in ongoing discussions. This aims to create a more inclusive, safe, and well-planned night-time environment for everyone.

PH8.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to city planning rules for bars, restaurants, and entertainment venues, effective January 1, 2025. This includes allowing nightclubs to operate in building basements and removing some area-specific restrictions on parts of Kingston Road and Queen Street East, potentially increasing nightlife options across the city.

GG8.14.summary: Toronto City Council has approved directly hiring N. Barry Lyon Consultants to conduct a study on the financial impacts of allowing multi-tenant housing, such as rooming houses, across the city. This research will help the city understand how these housing options affect property values and the broader economy, informing future housing policies for residents.

MM13.33.summary: To support mental health and suicide prevention, City Council decided to display posters promoting the 988 national crisis line in all City-owned buildings. This initiative aims to make it easier for Toronto residents in crisis to access vital support, particularly during the holiday season when some may feel more alone.

MM13.32.summary: Toronto City Council has approved launching a planning study for the area surrounding Glencairn Subway Station. This study will identify needed community services and investigate traffic and transportation improvements in the Marlee and Lawrence Avenue West corridor, helping the neighbourhood manage future growth and reduce congestion.

EX10.12.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new policy that updates the rules for how city councillors manage their offices and budgets. This change provides clearer guidelines for how public funds are spent on constituent services and daily operations, affecting how councillors serve their communities.

PH8.16.summary: Toronto City Council strengthened rules regarding the demolition and conversion of rental properties, aiming to better protect tenants. The City also requested the Province of Ontario grant it more authority to regulate the conversion of dwelling rooms and consult on new regulations. Additionally, a report will explore options for giving displaced tenants priority access to new affordable rental units.

CC13.23.summary: Toronto City Council has settled a legal dispute regarding a new development at 4174 Dundas Street West, agreeing to support the proposal at a land use tribunal. This decision means future residents and visitors of this development will not be eligible for street parking permits, and the developer must ensure local infrastructure and school capacity can handle the project.

IE9.1.summary: City Council has approved major road closures for the Toronto Triathlon Festival on Sunday, July 14, 2024. The eastbound Gardiner Expressway and northbound Don Valley Parkway will be closed from early morning until noon, which will significantly impact traffic flow. To help manage congestion, other road closures for events will be prohibited that day, and the triathlon organizer must cover any city costs for securing work zones.

IE9.5.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted a new by-law requiring buildings across the city to report their greenhouse gas emissions performance starting January 1, 2024. This move aims to help Toronto reduce its carbon footprint and address climate change, with collected data eventually becoming publicly available. The city may also introduce or increase fines for buildings that don't comply.

PH8.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved significant updates to the city's zoning rules for low-rise residential areas across the city. These changes, part of a housing action plan, simplify and modernize the by-laws to make it easier to build diverse housing types like multiplexes and small apartment buildings, aiming to increase housing options for residents. The city will continue consultations for a second phase of these reforms, with future proposals expected next year.

MM13.13.summary: Toronto City Council adopted a plan to better support seniors who wish to age in their current homes and communities. This includes advocating for federal and provincial funding, a national strategy, and streamlined home care services. City staff will also develop a local plan by late 2024 to enhance services and infrastructure, such as adding benches and exploring zoning changes to support amenities for older adults in their neighbourhoods.

MM13.35.summary: To ensure efficient and inclusive representation, City Council approved changes to the City-School Boards Advisory Committee. This committee, which facilitates cooperation between the city and school boards, will now have two fewer City Council members and will include trustees from the French-language school boards.

IE9.3.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted new rules to reduce single-use and takeaway items like straws, lids, and cutlery, requiring businesses to offer them only upon request. Effective March 1, 2024, these changes also encourage businesses to accept reusable cups, with health guidelines to be provided. Importantly, the city decided not to require businesses to charge for reusable shopping bags at this time.

CC13.10.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to a development project at 250 Wincott Drive and 4620 Eglinton Avenue West, adjusting the timeline for the design and construction of related community infrastructure. A key change is that future residents of these buildings will not be eligible for on-street parking permits, and developers must inform all prospective buyers and renters about this restriction and the available on-site parking.

ST4.1.summary: Toronto City Council has appointed Councillors Parthi Kandavel and Jon Burnside to the City-School Boards Advisory Committee for terms ending December 31, 2024. Councillor Kandavel will also serve as the Committee's Chair, helping to strengthen cooperation between the city and local school boards on important issues affecting students and communities.

PH8.23.summary: City Council adopted a request to the province for a special zoning order at 65 Rexdale Boulevard to allow for new development. This order would pave the way for at least 220 new purpose-built rental homes, with 20-30% being affordable for 99 years, alongside retail, a place of worship, and a community centre in Etobicoke North. This decision aims to boost housing options, especially affordable rentals, for residents in the area.

MM13.14.summary: City Council rejected a request to send the City Solicitor to the Toronto Local Appeal Body regarding the property at 8-26 Jopling Avenue South. This decision means the city will not formally participate in any appeal proceedings for this site, potentially affecting future development or planning decisions in that area.

MM13.24.summary: City Council has approved an agreement requiring a developer to dedicate two new public parks, totalling at least 12,500 square metres, along Lake Shore Boulevard West in Etobicoke-Lakeshore. This ensures the community gains valuable green space as new residential towers are built.

BL13.1.summary: Toronto City Council officially finalized and documented all decisions made during its December meeting by passing a series of confirmatory bills. This common procedure ensures that all actions, including new by-laws and planning changes, are legally valid and transparent.

TE9.27.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to oppose a proposed development at 685 Lake Shore Boulevard East, directing its legal team to fight the zoning and site plan applications at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This means the City wants to ensure any development at this waterfront site meets strict conditions related to infrastructure, flood protection, and community impacts before moving forward.

EX10.17.summary: City Council has approved the route for the Eglinton East Light Rail Transit (LRT), moving forward with a crucial transit expansion for Scarborough. The decision includes allocating an additional $9.5 million to the project's capital budget to advance design, utility relocation, and ensure future connections with other transit lines. This ensures the project continues to progress and integrates effectively with Toronto's broader transit network.

TE9.42.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a temporary closure of the south sidewalk, parking, bicycle, and eastbound vehicle lanes on Davenport Road near Bedford Road from December 2023 to July 2024. This change allows for construction staging, and residents can expect a safe pedestrian walkway and enhanced safety measures around the site.

MM13.29.summary: Toronto City Council has approved renaming prominent locations like Yonge-Dundas Square (to Sankofa Square), the Jane/Dundas Library, and two subway stations to remove references to Henry Dundas, acknowledging his role in delaying slavery abolition. This move aims to address historical legacies and promote a more inclusive city, supported by a public education campaign about the transatlantic slave trade.

RM13.1.summary: This item formally marked the beginning of the City Council meeting, where the Speaker called members to order. The meeting opened with O Canada, a moment of silence for several individuals, and an acknowledgement of Indigenous Peoples and traditional territories. Council members were also reminded about procedural rules for speaking during debates.

MM13.27.summary: Toronto City Council has approved accepting funding from the federal Housing Accelerator Fund. This money will help the city fast-track initiatives aimed at building more housing and transforming Toronto's housing system to address the housing crisis.

PH8.11.summary: Toronto City Council is moving forward with implementing the federal Rapid Housing Initiative, which aims to create more affordable housing. This includes preparing to lease 65 Dundas Street East to a non-profit organization for housing and authorizing data sharing with University Health Network to help connect homeless individuals with housing support.

MM13.8.summary: City Council has postponed a decision about whether to reimburse councillors for expenses incurred during the Cities Forum 2023 conference in Torino, Italy. This item, which concerns the use of public funds for council travel, will now be discussed in February 2024.

MM13.31.summary: Aiming to reduce noise for residents, City Council has asked the City Manager to request Metrolinx immediately stop train whistles at the Manse Road rail crossing. If a quick fix isn't possible, the city will ask Metrolinx to post a safety worker to prevent the whistles, providing peace to nearby communities.

CC13.7.summary: Toronto City Council has reversed its recent decision to limit the total number of ride-share driver licenses, like those for Uber. This means the city will resume issuing new licenses under the previous system while city staff prepare a report by March 2024 on new options for managing driver numbers, considering traffic congestion and environmental goals.

NY9.4.summary: Toronto City Council has added the property at 55 Yeomans Road to the City's Heritage Register.
