This decision protects the historical character of the Beth David Synagogue building and will guide any future changes or development, with city staff working with the synagogue on its future use and potential preservation support.

CC13.17.summary: Toronto City Council has agreed to a settlement regarding the proposed development at 975 Danforth Avenue. This means the City will support the development at the provincial planning tribunal, but only after the developer meets specific conditions for zoning, engineering, tree protection, and establishes a construction management plan that includes a committee for local residents and businesses.

MM13.15.summary: Toronto's City Council is directing its legal team to challenge an appeal concerning a development at 413 The Kingsway before a local appeals tribunal. This action aims to defend the city's planning decisions for the property and could influence what gets built in this neighbourhood.

EC8.8.summary: Drawing from the 2022 Toronto Fire Services Annual Report, City Council approved several initiatives to enhance public safety and equity. They requested Toronto Fire Services to address 2SLGBTQ+ recruitment and report on fire safety for people living outdoors, while also exploring firefighting career paths for Toronto Community Housing tenants. This aims to foster a more inclusive fire service and improve safety for vulnerable residents.

EX10.2.summary: Toronto City Council has updated the mandates for its housing agencies, Toronto Community Housing Corporation (TCHC) and CreateTO, to boost the supply of new affordable and market-rent housing across the city. The agencies are now directed to build more diverse homes on city-owned properties and ensure these lands stay publicly owned. This aims to create more complete, mixed-income communities and accelerate housing development to meet Toronto's housing goals.

EX10.9.summary: Toronto City Council has approved internal adjustments to the 2023 operating budget, reallocating funds based on actual spending over the first nine months of the year. These changes help manage the city's finances by aligning departmental budgets with their spending, without altering the overall net budget for the City.

EX10.16.summary: Toronto City Council has urged the Province of Ontario to fund and include Cummer Station as part of the Yonge North Subway Extension project. This decision aims to secure a new subway stop for residents, improving transit access for thousands living and working in the area.

NY9.2.summary: City Council is exploring options to make pedestrian crossings safer at the intersection of Wilson Avenue and Maniza Road. City staff will investigate various improvements, including traffic control signals, and report back on their findings in February 2024.

RM13.7.summary: City Council finalized its agenda, known as the Order Paper, for its meetings on December 13, 14, and 15, 2023. This action formally approved the list of topics and proposals that councillors would discuss and vote on during those sessions.

IA13.1.summary: City Council recently reviewed an inquiry about renaming Dundas Street and the City Manager's reply. They decided to simply acknowledge this information without taking any further steps, effectively halting any immediate action on the street's renaming from this specific item.

SC9.9.summary: Toronto City Council has approved installing new traffic lights at Birchmount Road and Chaldean Street/Glendower Circuit. This decision aims to improve pedestrian safety for residents in the area, even though city staff had initially suggested it wasn't strictly necessary based on technical guidelines.

GG8.20.summary: City officials are reviewing Toronto's fair wage policy, which sets pay standards for workers on city contracts. A city committee sent a proposal back to staff, asking for more detailed financial analysis and options to ensure small businesses can continue to compete for city projects. This review affects how workers are paid on city-funded jobs and could influence which companies win these contracts.

EX10.20.summary: Following complaints that the Etobicoke Coat of Arms displayed in the Civic Centre is offensive or racist, a city committee reviewed information on the matter, including a report suggesting its removal aligns with the city's reconciliation plan. However, the committee simply received the item for information, deciding to take no further action on the complaints or the potential removal of the Coat of Arms. This means the display will remain in place for now.

TA4.8.summary: The Toronto Atmospheric Fund has approved an investment of up to $3.5 million to help drivers of vehicles for hire, such as taxis, purchase electric vehicles. This initiative aims to accelerate the adoption of electric vehicles in Toronto, supporting the city's climate goals and making it easier for these drivers to transition to greener transportation.

TE9.12.summary: City Council has approved changes to the Official Plan and Zoning By-law for properties located at 822-838 Richmond Street West. This decision paves the way for a new development project at this site, altering what can be built and how the land can be used in this part of the city.

NY9.30.summary: North York Community Council has asked for a report on creating a branding strategy for the North York area. This initiative aims to highlight the neighbourhood's diverse culture, restaurants, and local businesses, encouraging more Toronto residents to explore the area and fostering community spirit, with plans to survey residents for a new slogan.

NY9.3.summary: North York Community Council approved a request from the owner of 23 Laurentia Crescent, allowing them to keep an existing fence that doesn't meet city rules. This means the fence can stay as it is for now, but any future replacement must follow current by-laws.

NY9.18.summary: Toronto's North York Community Council has denied a request to remove a healthy Norway spruce tree from a private property at 7 Marwill Street. This decision upholds the city's tree protection by-laws, indicating a commitment to preserving the urban canopy even on private land.

EX9.10.summary: Toronto City Council has endorsed the city-wide expansion of the Toronto Community Crisis Service, recognizing it as the city's fourth emergency service. This means residents across Toronto will soon have access to non-police, community-led mental health and crisis support, improving how the city responds to emergency calls. The city will now proceed with negotiating funding agreements to implement this critical service expansion.

PH7.13.summary: City Council has approved a change to the sign by-law for the property at 195 Galaxy Boulevard, allowing for a new large electronic sign at this specific location. This decision also reclassifies a portion of the property's sign district from Open Space to Employment, which permits different types of signs.

EX9.14.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the detailed design and planning for the Waterfront East Light Rail Transit (LRT) project, including its full route from Queens Quay to Villiers Loop. This decision moves the project closer to construction by securing environmental approvals and coordinating with other major waterfront developments, with an increased capital budget allocated for this design work.

MM12.6.summary: A proposal to display the Etobicoke Coat of Arms in the new Etobicoke Civic Centre was sent to the Executive Committee for further review. This means the decision on whether to showcase this symbol of Etobicoke's heritage in a prominent civic building has been postponed for now.

MM12.12.summary: Toronto has officially joined a global effort to combat hate, extremism, and polarization by endorsing the New York City Mayoral Declaration. This commitment means the city will work to build stronger, more inclusive communities, address misinformation, and collaborate with other cities worldwide to foster safety and belonging for all residents.

MM12.14.summary: To promote community understanding and celebrate diverse faiths, Toronto City Council has decided to support Interfaith Harmony Week, running from February 1 to 7, 2024. The City will develop a guide for councillors, helping them organize events and engage residents across the city during this important week.

MM12.17.summary: City Council decided to send its legal team to an independent appeals body to defend the city's original decision to reject a property change application at 169 Parkmount Road. This action helps ensure that local planning rules are upheld and allows the legal team to negotiate a resolution on the city's behalf.

IE7.4.summary: To make Toronto streets safer, the city has approved updates to its Vision Zero road safety initiatives. This includes a new policy for how traffic calming measures like speed cushions are put in place and an expanded framework for creating Community Safety Zones, especially around schools, where traffic fines are higher. The city also renewed its call for provincial action on motorcycle blood alcohol limits and area-based speed limits.

CC12.14.summary: Toronto City Council is taking steps to protect the city's interests regarding tree removal at Ontario Place. Council has directed the City Solicitor to explore intervening in the Province's federal environmental assessment process and to prepare for potential legal action related to the site.

EX9.3.summary: City Council has approved a comprehensive plan to transform Toronto's housing system, aiming to urgently build more affordable homes and protect existing non-profit and co-operative housing. This initiative involves reviewing current housing programs, developing new strategies for community housing, and engaging with various partners to identify lands for new home creation.

EX9.19.summary: Toronto City Council has formally asked the Federal government for urgent financial and operational support to address the ongoing refugee and humanitarian crisis. The city is specifically seeking federal funding for emergency housing and reception centres, full reimbursement for costs incurred supporting refugees, and an increase in housing benefits, including $6.67 million for 2023.

EC7.7.summary: City Council has approved a new long-term strategy and a specific 2024 plan to address homelessness and expand shelter services. This initiative aims to improve and develop new facilities, secure existing temporary sites, and create a comprehensive infrastructure for supporting people experiencing homelessness across Toronto.

EC7.8.summary: Toronto City Council is urging the federal government to provide immediate funding and open emergency shelter sites on federal lands for people seeking asylum, and to create a new reception centre near Pearson Airport. The city is also asking both federal and provincial governments for financial support to expand Toronto's winter shelter services, including warming centres and extra spaces, to help address the city's overwhelmed shelter system.

DM12.1.summary: City Council has decided to settle a dispute regarding a development application for the property at 25 Old York Mills Road. This means the city will support a new agreement for the project at the Ontario Land Tribunal, rather than continue to oppose it. This decision helps resolve a contested building proposal, which impacts future development in the area.

MM11.39.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to ensure that proper procedures are followed before any trees are removed on City-owned land at Ontario Place. Council directed staff to report on all requirements for tree removal, including heritage and environmental assessments, and authorized the City Solicitor to take legal steps to protect the City's interests if trees are removed without permits. This aims to safeguard the area's natural heritage amidst planned development.

MM11.37.summary: To combat rising hate, City Council has adopted a plan to ensure Toronto is a safe and welcoming city for everyone. This includes directing staff to create an online 'anti-hate package' with information on reporting hate crimes and available support resources. Council also asked the Toronto Police to establish community safety zones around vulnerable locations like places of worship and provide public updates on their hate crime efforts.

CC11.18.summary: Toronto City Council has largely completed its review of city land use, confirming the zoning for many employment areas. However, decisions on converting several key employment lands, including sites in East Harbour and along Finch Avenue West, have been put on hold and will require further consideration before their future use (for jobs or housing) is decided.

PH6.2.summary: Toronto City Council has updated its planning rules and processes to comply with the provincial Bill 109, which aims to accelerate housing development approvals. These changes affect how the city reviews new building projects and conducts early consultations with developers, and the city will now track how quickly these applications are processed. This shift could impact the pace of new housing construction across the city.

CC11.9.summary: The City has accepted a settlement offer regarding proposed changes to the official plan for a property at 18 Champlain Boulevard, part of the Tippett Road Regeneration Area. This agreement will be presented to the provincial land planning body, avoiding a potentially lengthy dispute and providing clear direction for development in that neighborhood.

GG6.25.summary: Toronto City Council has increased the penalty for parking without consent on municipal or private property. Starting December 1, 2023, the fine for these violations will rise from $30 to $75, aiming to deter unauthorized parking across the city.

IE6.2.summary: City Council has approved a one-year pilot project to create "Mobile Only Zones" at some on-street paid parking spots across the city. In these specific locations, drivers will only be able to pay for parking using the Green P App, text-to-pay, or by scanning a QR code, moving away from traditional parking meters. New parking offenses related to these zones will be introduced, carrying a fine to help ensure compliance.

MM11.40.summary: Toronto City Council approved a plan to help improve the tennis courts at 15 San Romanoway, used by Doug Philpott Inner City Children's Tennis. This means a bubble can be built over the courts, allowing more free or low-cost tennis programs for local youth and public use. The City is supporting this by exempting the project from certain fees.

EX8.8.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted an update to the ConnectTO program, which aims to expand public Wi-Fi access across the city. This initiative focuses on closing the digital divide for residents in low-income housing and other underserved communities by leveraging existing city network infrastructure. The plan directs city staff to collaborate with community groups and other governments to improve affordable internet access and promote economic development.

MM11.19.summary: Toronto City Council has officially asked the Province of Ontario to fully fund and construct the proposed Cummer Station for the Yonge North Subway Extension. This decision aims to secure a crucial transit stop for 15,000 current and future North York residents, with the city also preparing a business case to strengthen its appeal.

BL11.1.summary: City Council formally enacted a series of by-laws, including those that confirm past meeting decisions and others that authorize special charges on specific properties. These special charges are typically levied to recover costs for local improvements that directly benefit those properties.

EY7.3.summary: City Council has approved a zoning change for the properties at 5, 7, and 9 Jopling Avenue South, paving the way for new development in this area. This approval comes with conditions, including a requirement for the developer to provide a relocation and financial assistance plan for the tenant of the existing rental unit slated for demolition, and to ensure necessary upgrades to local storm and sewer infrastructure.

EY7.23.summary: To help residents dealing with limited parking, City Council approved a step towards implementing permit parking on Longbourne Drive. This decision temporarily exempts the area from certain rules, allowing the local community council to consider formal requests for permit parking there.

EX8.7.summary: Toronto City Council updated its Vacant Home Tax, clarifying rules and making it more expensive to keep homes empty. This aims to encourage owners to rent or sell their properties, rather than leave them vacant, while also adjusting some exemptions for new builds and inherited properties.

EC6.6.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new rules requiring all taxis, limousines, and ride-sharing vehicles to be zero-emission by 2031. This initiative aims to reduce air pollution in the city, and a $10 million grant program will be established to help vehicle operators transition their fleets.

PB10.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to two designated heritage properties at 220 Cowan Avenue and 1313 Queen Street West. This decision paves the way for the new Parkdale Hub, ensuring that the historical character of these buildings is preserved through specific conservation plans.

TE7.26.summary: Toronto's East York Community Council has denied a resident's request to move a healthy city-owned tree at 104 Scarboro Beach Boulevard. The resident sought to injure the red maple to build a new parking pad, but city bylaws protect healthy, maintainable trees, reinforcing efforts to preserve Toronto's urban forest.

EY7.16.summary: Etobicoke York Community Council has approved the installation of nine speed humps on Government Road, between Thorndale Crescent/Mill Cove and The Kingsway. This decision to slow traffic and improve safety for residents was made despite city staff advising against it because the street did not fully meet the City's technical criteria for traffic calming.

RM10.4.summary: Toronto City Council recently acknowledged a petition submitted by residents concerned with saving the Marian Shrine of Gratitude. The Council received the petition for information, meaning they formally noted residents' concerns but did not take further action on it through this specific agenda item.

MM10.5.summary: Toronto's City Council has asked its planning department to evaluate the property at 3100 Weston Road for official heritage designation. This step could lead to the building being legally protected, helping to preserve its historical character and importance for the neighbourhood.

CC10.2.summary: City Council has approved an urgent request to the Federal Government for significant financial and logistical support for refugee claimants in Toronto. The city is asking for full reimbursement of an estimated $200 million spent in 2023 and ongoing funding for projected annual costs of $250 million in 2024 and beyond, to alleviate the strain on Toronto's shelter system.

EX7.2.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted an ambitious plan to address the city's housing crisis by committing to build 25,000 new rent-controlled homes. This target includes increasing the supply of affordable housing by 7,500 units, with 2,500 designated as rent-geared-to-income. City staff will now develop a detailed strategy, timeline, and cost analysis, working with various partners to make more housing options available for residents.

DM10.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a settlement offer for a development proposal at 20 Broadoaks Drive and 11 Catford Road. This agreement resolves a dispute at the provincial land planning tribunal, allowing the project to proceed once specific planning and technical requirements are met.

EX7.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved an updated long-term financial plan to address a substantial $46.5 billion financial challenge facing the city. Beginning January 1, 2024, higher Municipal Land Transfer Tax rates will apply to residential properties sold for over $3 million, with the highest rate reaching 7.5% for properties over $20 million. These new graduated taxes aim to generate more revenue and primarily affect buyers and sellers of high-value homes.

BL10.1.summary: At its September 6th meeting, City Council formally passed a series of new by-laws, making previous decisions official. These by-laws cover a range of topics, including designating several properties as culturally significant, amending city financial reserves, and adjusting land use rules for specific areas.

CC8.2.summary: Toronto City Council has formally declared gender-based violence and intimate partner violence an epidemic within the city. This decision aims to draw attention to the crisis, urging provincial and federal governments to also declare an epidemic and implement key recommendations to prevent violence and femicide. The City will also explore ways to make meaningful investments in programs and services that support victims and prevent future violence.

TE6.74.summary: City Council has approved the installation of new traffic lights at the intersection of Carlton Street and Homewood Avenue. This decision aims to improve pedestrian safety and better connect the Garden District, creating a continuous and safer path for residents.

TE6.75.summary: City Council has approved new traffic lights for the intersection of Yonge Street and Grenville Street/Wood Street. This decision came after a community review revealed a strong resident desire for better traffic control, aiming to improve safety and traffic flow in the area.

TE6.76.summary: City Council has approved the installation of new traffic lights at the intersection of Yonge Street and Maitland Street/Breadalbane Street. This decision responds to community input, aiming to improve safety and traffic flow for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers in this busy downtown area.

TE6.21.summary: City Council has approved updated zoning rules for the next phases of redevelopment at 325 Gerrard Street East, part of Regent Park. This decision allows for new buildings and requires the developer to create over 3,700 square metres of community space for local residents, ensuring public benefits are included in the project.

MM8.4.summary: Toronto City Council has requested a detailed report on the future of the Ontario Science Centre. This report will explore the provincial lease terms for the current site and investigate if the City could operate the Science Centre at its Don Mills location, including potential revenue from events, which is important for the surrounding communities of Thorncliffe and Flemingdon Park.

MM8.6.summary: City Council has instructed its legal team to defend a city planning committee's decision to reject a proposed development at 64 Mildenhall Road. This means the City will argue against the project at an independent appeal body, and can hire outside experts to support its case.

EY6.2.summary: City Council has approved a zoning change for 2801 Jane Street to allow for a new development, with specific conditions to protect current residents. The developer must maintain the existing 164 rental units for at least 20 years, provide tenants with access to new amenities, and fund repairs to the current building's parking and concrete at no cost to them.

TM2.1.summary: City Council adopted recommendations for Metrolinx's Ontario Line construction in Toronto. These recommendations aim to ensure local residents, particularly Indigenous, Black, and equity-deserving groups, benefit from job opportunities and local procurement during the project. The recommendations also seek to support businesses impacted by construction through better communication, signage, and traffic management.

MM8.33.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to traffic and parking rules in Wards 3 and 4 to implement safety improvements on local streets. These updates will help create "Complete Streets" designed for safer use by drivers, cyclists, and pedestrians, with installation work scheduled to begin in August.

MM8.28.summary: Toronto City Council is looking into the risks of deadly tanker truck crashes on Highway 401. City emergency services officials will prepare a report on how to keep residents living near the highway safe and suggest actions for the city and recommendations for provincial and federal governments.

MM8.22.summary: Toronto City Council recently decided against reviewing the current requirements for mayoral candidates. This means that rules like nomination fees and the number of signatures needed to run for mayor will remain unchanged, keeping the existing framework for who can qualify to run in future elections.

PH5.3.summary: Toronto City Council has changed the planning rules for several properties, converting land previously reserved for employment uses into areas where homes, businesses, and community spaces can be built. This decision, part of "Our Plan Toronto," guides future development across the city and helps shape how various neighborhoods will grow.

BL8.1.summary: At its July 19-20 meeting, City Council formally approved a range of decisions from previous meetings by passing numerous by-laws. This standard procedural step makes previously adopted items, such as new zoning rules or special charges, legally binding.

SC6.6.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the zoning rules for the property at 750 Huntingwood Drive. This decision allows for specific types of development on this site, which will impact what can be built and how the land can be used in the local neighbourhood.

SC6.8.summary: City Council approved changes to Toronto's long-term development plan and local zoning rules for 875 Morningside Avenue (840 Military Trail). This decision allows for new construction on the property, which will change the character and use of the area.

RM8.7.summary: City Council formally approved its meeting agenda for July 19 and 20, including adjustments to the order of business and which items would be discussed. This ensures that important city matters, such as the refugee crisis and gender-based violence, are prioritized for debate by councillors.

MM8.29.summary: Toronto City Council is taking steps to support refugees by immediately providing temporary shelter for hundreds of individuals and committing funds to help 1,350 households find permanent housing. The city approved a $6.67 million contribution from its reserve fund to match provincial funding for the Canada-Ontario Housing Benefit. The city will also work with the federal government to establish a welcome centre at Pearson Airport for new arrivals, while urging higher levels of government for more funding.

GG5.4.summary: City Council has increased funding and extended a contract with the Canadian Red Cross to continue providing emergency lodging and related support services. This additional $10 million ensures vital assistance for vulnerable residents, likely refugees and asylum claimants, through the end of 2023.

MM8.26.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to appeal a local planning board's decision regarding a property at 2 Cora Crescent. The city's legal team will challenge a request for a minor change to building rules for this address, aiming to ensure development aligns with city planning guidelines and protects the neighbourhood.

EC5.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a temporary pilot program allowing adults to drink alcohol in 27 designated city parks from August 2 to October 9, 2023. This change to park rules means residents can enjoy alcoholic beverages responsibly in these select public spaces for a limited time this summer and fall.

IE5.1.summary: Toronto City Council has expressed support for a potential ban on two-stroke small engine equipment, like leaf blowers and lawnmowers, to protect human health and the environment. City staff will now identify the resources needed to develop and implement this ban, with a goal to introduce a by-law if funding is secured in the 2024 budget. The city will also engage with residents and businesses to explore support programs for transitioning to cleaner equipment.

IE5.5.summary: Toronto City Council is exploring how e-scooters and other micromobility devices could safely operate in the city. Council has directed staff to develop a comprehensive Micromobility Strategy by early 2024, including recommendations on launching potential pilot projects. This strategy will focus on pedestrian safety, accessibility, climate benefits, liability insurance, and the mandatory use of helmets, aiming to ensure safety and order on city streets.

HL5.5.summary: Toronto's Board of Health has approved the 2022 performance review for the city's Medical Officer of Health. While the details of the appraisal remain confidential to protect the individual's privacy, this action ensures accountability for a key public health official.

SC6.22.summary: The Scarborough Community Council approved an exemption for the owners of 31 Ellington Drive, allowing them to keep a backyard wood fence that is currently taller than the city's 2-meter height limit. This decision means the existing fence can remain as long as it is maintained, but any future replacement must follow city bylaws.

NY6.22.summary: The North York Community Council has approved a resident's request to remove a large, healthy Norway spruce tree from their property at 146 Three Valleys Drive, overturning a recommendation from City staff to deny the permit. To offset the loss of the tree, the resident is required to provide five replacement trees, either by planting them on-site or by making a payment to the City for tree planting elsewhere. This decision impacts the local urban tree canopy and how private tree removals are handled in the community.

NY6.20.summary: The North York Community Council refused a request from the owner of 23 Laurentia Crescent to keep a fence that does not meet city standards. This means the property owner must change the fence to comply with Toronto's fence by-laws.

NY6.36.summary: North York Community Council has approved the installation of an all-way stop sign at Seneca Hill Drive and Kingslake Road. This decision aims to improve pedestrian safety for local residents and children walking to Seneca Hill Public School, addressing long-standing concerns about speeding vehicles and poor visibility on the curved road.

EX5.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved accepting up to $226 million in provincial funding for the SmartTrack Stations Program. This decision helps move forward the construction of new transit stations across the city, aiming to improve connectivity and public transit options for residents.

EC4.6.summary: Toronto City Council has updated its rules for temporary signs like A-frames and portable signs displayed on public property. The new regulations require signs to be placed closer to the curb, maintain specific distances from street infrastructure, and ensure a wider 2.5-metre clear path for pedestrians on many busy streets. These changes aim to improve sidewalk accessibility, reduce clutter, and ensure public safety for residents.

PH4.1.summary: City Council has created a new "Woodbine Special Sign District" for the area around 555 Rexdale Boulevard, home to Woodbine Racetrack and Casino. This change allows for and regulates various types of electronic and static advertising signs within this designated district, which may alter the visual landscape for local businesses.

CC7.9.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a settlement for a proposed development at 4, 6, 8, and 10 Beamish Drive. This means the city will support revised building plans at a provincial tribunal, aiming to resolve a dispute over the property's zoning rules and ensure a planned outcome for the neighbourhood.

MM7.5.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a review of how Yonge-Dundas Square is managed and operated, exploring options like public operation similar to Nathan Phillips Square. This decision aims to find the best future model for the prominent public space, with recommendations expected early next year. During this review, the current management board is asked to pause new external funding agreements that might impact its future structure.

MM7.8.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to explore new rules for rental housing temperatures. Staff will investigate creating a by-law that would require landlords to maintain specific heating and cooling temperatures in rental units, and to offer cooling options for tenants during heatwaves, with a report due early next year. This initiative aims to improve tenant health and comfort in extreme weather conditions.

MM7.15.summary: City Council has approved plans to expand the clothing-optional beach at Hanlan's Point and will review changes to local rules to support this. The city will also start planning urgent projects to restore an eroded section of the beach, create a new paved trail, and install fencing to protect the environment and commemorate its historical significance to 2SLGBTQI+ communities.

MM7.23.summary: Toronto City Council decided to explore becoming a "Charter City," a move that would give the city more direct control over its own affairs, such as planning, taxation, and elections. City staff will now investigate the legal process and how to engage residents on potentially enshrining these powers within the Canadian Constitution.

MM7.25.summary: Toronto City Council has asked the federal government to issue Clean Electricity Regulations that prevent any increase in gas-fired power generation at the Portlands Energy Centre. This move supports the city's climate goals by aiming to reduce carbon emissions and promote cleaner energy sources for Toronto residents.

MM7.17.summary: City Council decided to send a motion regarding the delivery of graphic images, like flyers, to the Executive Committee for further review. This means the committee will discuss potential rules or changes related to how these materials are distributed in the city before Council makes a final decision.

PH4.8.summary: City Council formally submitted its concerns and requested changes to the Province's proposed planning statement, which will significantly impact how Toronto develops. The city is advocating for policies that maintain strong affordable housing requirements, promote dense growth around transit, and prioritize building within existing areas over urban sprawl.

CC7.7.summary: Toronto City Council has appointed Nicole Martin to the Toronto Hydro Board of Directors. This ensures qualified oversight for Toronto's electricity distribution company, which delivers power to homes and businesses across the city.

MM7.1.summary: Toronto City Council has decided that the money from selling a city-owned property at 276 Lauder Avenue will be set aside for a specific purpose. These funds will be used to purchase new parkland in Ward 9 (Davenport), ensuring more green spaces for residents in that area.

MM7.20.summary: Council decided not to approve a proposal to move planned modular housing from 175 Cummer Avenue and formally protect the green space there as parkland. This means the modular housing project at this location is likely to continue as planned, and the green space will not gain official park status through this motion.

MM7.43.summary: Toronto City Council did not approve a motion that proposed improvements for the modular housing site at 175 Cummer Avenue. This means the suggested upgrades will not proceed, which could affect residents and impact the ongoing zoning bylaw appeal for this site at the Ontario Land Tribunal.

MM7.19.summary: City Council has approved a plan to improve coordination between the Toronto Transit Commission and Toronto Water. They will work together to proactively inspect sewers along streetcar lines, aiming to prevent sinkholes and ensure safer roads and transit.

MM7.16.summary: City Council has requested a comprehensive review of the popular CaféTO program, which allows restaurants to expand their patios onto public spaces. The review, due by the end of 2023, aims to improve permit processing, communication with businesses, and the program's overall operations for 2024, including considering financial assistance for patio setup costs and potential changes to program fees.

EY5.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new zoning rules for a property at 4500 Jane Street, ensuring the existing 164 rental units remain protected for at least 20 years. This decision prevents their conversion to condos or demolition, providing stability for current residents. The property owner must also undertake significant building improvements at no cost to tenants and mitigate construction impacts.

TE5.40.summary: Toronto City Council has approved reducing the speed limit on many major arterial roads in central Toronto wards, including Parkdale-High Park, Davenport, University-Rosedale, Toronto-St. Paul’s, Toronto Centre, and Toronto-Danforth. This change will set the maximum speed to 40 kilometres per hour, aiming to improve safety for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers in these areas.

CC7.11.summary: The City Council has approved a settlement for a development proposal at Concorde Gate and Concorde Place, which was under appeal. This decision allows the development to proceed, but only after the developer meets specific conditions, including fully covering the cost of any necessary infrastructure upgrades in the area.

RM7.1.summary: City Council formally began its meeting by observing a moment of silence for community members who passed away, and acknowledging the traditional Indigenous territories where Toronto is located. This procedural item officially opened the Council meeting, setting the stage for subsequent decisions and discussions.

CC7.2.summary: Toronto City Council has appointed Jag Sharma as the new Deputy City Manager for Development and Growth, effective August 14, 2023. This crucial role oversees how the city plans for new housing, infrastructure, and overall urban development, impacting how Toronto grows and evolves.

CC7.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the appointment of Will Johnston as the new Deputy City Manager for Infrastructure Services. In this key role, he will oversee the city's vital infrastructure projects and services, impacting everything from roads to water systems across Toronto.

PH4.5.summary: City Council adopted a new Official Plan Amendment, which makes decisions about the future use of 45 specific employment areas across Toronto. This amendment determines which lands will continue to be protected for jobs and businesses, and which can be considered for conversion to other uses like housing, influencing the city's future development.

TE5.9.summary: New development is moving forward at Eastern Avenue and Knox Avenue after City Council approved a zoning change. The approval requires the developer to include six affordable rental housing units in the project, which will be rented at prices targeting 80% of average market rent for 25 years. This decision will create more affordable housing options in the community.

CA6.5.summary: City Council has appointed two new public members, Fenton Jagdeo and Julie Osborne, to the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) Board for a four-year term. These individuals will help oversee and guide decisions for Toronto's public transit system, influencing service and operations for riders across the city.

EC4.3.summary: Toronto City Council is urging the provincial and federal governments to improve the Canada-Wide Early Learning and Child Care program in the city. Council wants more funded spaces to meet demand, updated income thresholds for fee subsidies to help low-income families, and increased funding for staff wages and benefits. They are also requesting capital funding to build more non-profit child care facilities.

IE4.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the installation of several new bikeway projects, including cycle tracks and bike lanes, on various streets across the city. This decision expands Toronto's cycling network, aiming to provide more safe and accessible routes for residents to cycle.

PB7.9.summary: Toronto has added the property at 94 Cortleigh Boulevard to the City's Heritage Register. This decision recognizes the cultural heritage value of the 1914 Arts and Crafts style home and provides it with interim protection from demolition or significant alteration.

PB7.1.summary: Toronto is moving to protect the Walmer Road Baptist Church at 38 Walmer Road by declaring its intention to designate it as a heritage property. This decision aims to preserve the building's significant historical and architectural value, ensuring this over 130-year-old community institution remains a key part of the city's heritage.

PH4.7.summary: A city committee has approved public consultations on potential changes to design rules for mid-rise buildings, specifically how they are built next to other properties. These updates aim to provide more flexibility in design, which could help increase housing supply and offer more housing options across Toronto. This work is part of the city's plan to streamline housing development.

GG4.19.summary: Toronto City Council has approved contracts to secure school crossing guard services across the city. This decision ensures children's safety around schools for an initial period of two years, with options to extend these services for three additional one-year terms.

PB6.6.summary: City Council is allowing alterations to the heritage-protected building at 64 Wellesley Street East. This decision requires the owner to enter a legal agreement to preserve the building's historic elements, ensuring its character is maintained while allowing for new development.

PB6.5.summary: The Toronto Preservation Board has recommended approving significant changes to heritage properties on Yonge Street, allowing for the construction of a 50-storey building at 1420 Yonge Street. This decision also includes the demolition of some heritage features at 1404 and 1406 Yonge Street, with conditions requiring a conservation plan and a Heritage Easement Agreement. These changes will impact the character and future development of this important Yonge Street corridor.

CT4.5.summary: TO Live's Board has approved hiring a consultant to find sponsors and secure naming rights for the redeveloped St. Lawrence Centre. This move aims to generate revenue through corporate partnerships, helping to fund the major renovation of this important city cultural venue.

NY5.28.summary: North York Community Council has decided to deny a resident's appeal to remove a privately owned black walnut tree at 136 Craighurst Avenue. Despite reasons given by the homeowner like structural issues and allergies, the City's Urban Forestry deemed the tree healthy and maintainable, meaning it will remain on the property.

MM6.8.summary: Toronto City Council has extended the application deadline for property tax, waste collection, and water bill relief programs for low-income seniors and disabled persons. The new deadline is October 31, 2023, giving eligible residents more time to apply for financial assistance with these household expenses.

MM6.13.summary: Toronto City Council has taken a stand against new fossil fuel power plants, specifically opposing any new proposals to burn natural gas for electricity within city limits. This decision pushes for a cleaner energy future for residents by urging the Province of Ontario to invest in energy efficiency and conservation programs instead of expanding fossil fuel production.

EC3.4.summary: Toronto has decided to end its UrbanHensTO backyard hens pilot program, meaning residents can no longer apply to keep hens. Existing participants are allowed to keep their current hens under the original program rules, and the city will study backyard hen programs in other cities for a potential relaunch in 2025, pending Avian Flu risks.

EC3.5.summary: Toronto City Council has expressed its willingness to potentially manage local employment services for the Province, but only if the Province updates its performance targets and improves financial and training support for job seekers. This aims to better coordinate workforce development programs and provide more effective job support for low-income residents and other vulnerable communities across the city.

MM6.1.summary: Toronto City Council has urged the Federal government to help small businesses struggling to repay their Canada Emergency Business Account (CEBA) loans. The Council is requesting a multi-year, no-interest repayment plan and for the government to forgive $20,000 of the loan for businesses that agree to this plan. This initiative aims to support local businesses, helping them recover from the pandemic and continue contributing to Toronto's economy.

DM6.3.summary: Toronto City Council is addressing public safety concerns related to the growing use of micro-mobility vehicles like e-bikes and scooters, particularly by delivery couriers. To make sidewalks safer for pedestrians and ensure compliance with road rules, the city will explore options for requiring commercial delivery vehicles to have unique identifiers, ask courier companies to educate their staff, and research ways to deter dangerous sidewalk riding.

TE4.37.summary: In a recent decision, City Council adopted a report on the Gerrard-Carlaw North development, urging the provincial government and developers to commit to 20% affordable housing for 50 years. Council also requested an assessment of how 1098 new housing units would impact the Matty Eckler Community Centre by the end of 2023.

EC3.13.summary: Responding to a declared homelessness emergency, Toronto City Council moved to improve winter services and secure critical funding. The city will develop a long-term strategy for warming centres, prioritize 24-hour respite sites, and requested $5 million from other levels of government for additional warming centres and $20 million for housing benefits to help 1,600 to 2,000 households leave shelters. This decision aims to enhance support for Toronto residents experiencing homelessness.

MM6.17.summary: To improve transit reliability, Toronto City Council has directed a review of traffic light adjustments aimed at reducing delays for streetcars and RapidTO buses. This means staff will investigate adding or extending left-turn restrictions or dedicated left-turn signal phases at intersections to help public transit move more efficiently.

DM6.5.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to move forward with a pilot program that will allow responsible personal alcohol consumption in some city parks. City staff will consult with Councillors to identify suitable parks in interested wards and develop a plan for the pilot to run from August 5 to October 9, 2023. This initiative aims to explore if residents can safely enjoy alcohol in designated park areas, while also considering public health, safety, and operational needs.

EX4.3.summary: Toronto City Council has set the 2023 property tax rates that will fund education, collecting over $2.14 billion from local property owners. While most of these funds go to the provincial government for schools, the City keeps a small portion to cover administrative costs. Council also adopted rules to adjust tax decreases for some commercial and industrial properties, and asked the province to let Toronto keep all property taxes it collects.

EC3.16.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a partnership with Unity Health Toronto and Inner City Health Associates to explore developing a shared platform aimed at improving health and housing outcomes for people experiencing homelessness. This decision also requires city staff to provide more frequent and aligned reports on the deaths of people experiencing homelessness, increasing transparency and accountability for this vulnerable population.

TE4.66.summary: City Council has adopted several measures to improve pedestrian safety on Avenue Road, reducing the speed limit between Bloor Street and St. Clair Avenue West to 40 km/h. The city will also install physical barriers near a church and has asked police to increase speed enforcement, with further studies planned for additional safety upgrades.

MM6.26.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a request for the City Manager to investigate how the city can earn more money through sponsorships, naming rights, and advertising. This review aims to find new revenue opportunities from public assets and materials, helping the city diversify its income and fund services.

CC6.13.summary: Toronto City Council has settled a land dispute concerning properties on Toryork Drive, avoiding a longer hearing at the Ontario Land Tribunal. As part of the agreement, a developer will receive a credit on their parkland fees if they construct new park improvements, potentially benefiting local residents with new green space.

GG3.18.summary: After reviewing the Customer Experience Division's 2022 report, City Council approved plans to improve how residents interact with city services like 311. These plans include studying new ways to engage residents, especially vulnerable communities, and ensuring their feedback is better incorporated into service delivery.

IE3.7.summary: City Council has decided to permanently close High Park to private visitor vehicles on Saturdays, Sundays, and statutory holidays, making the park more accessible for pedestrians and cyclists. This strategy will include developing a recreational cycling pilot program for 2023 and requesting the TTC to explore improved transit service within and around the park.

PH3.17.summary: City Council has approved a plan to guide the future growth and development of the Downsview area, using a draft secondary plan and community development priorities as a starting point. This decision means city staff will now use these drafts for more public consultations, discussions with stakeholders, and to assess new development proposals. Staff will also develop a full Community Development Plan and identify its budget needs for consideration in 2025, ensuring a focus on accessible, seniors-friendly, and diverse housing options.

EC3.17.summary: City officials are developing new rules for when stores can open on holidays, especially in designated "tourist areas." This means places like Yorkdale Shopping Centre could eventually be allowed to open on more holidays, changing shopping options for residents.

MM6.5.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the relocation of the Delta Bingo & Gaming charitable bingo hall. This moves the "Class A" facility from 1799 St. Clair Avenue West to a new address at 360 Evans Avenue, affecting where residents can participate in charitable gaming that supports local community groups.

MM6.9.summary: Toronto City Council has taken the first step to honour Canadian music icon Gordon Lightfoot by asking for a plan to create a statue. This plan will explore possible locations, such as Massey Hall, and suggest funding sources like developer contributions or private donations, aiming to fund it without directly using general city funds.

MM6.10.summary: Toronto City Council has requested the incoming Mayor to establish a "Mayor's Roundtable to End Homelessness." This group will bring together people with lived experience, advocates, and experts to develop a city-wide plan to reduce and ultimately end homelessness in Toronto. This initiative aims to create a coordinated and informed strategy to tackle a critical social issue facing the city.

MM6.12.summary: City Council has formally asked the Ontario government to amend provincial law to make City Councillors ineligible for jury duty while they are serving in office. This request aims to ensure that elected officials can fully dedicate their time and attention to city matters without interruption.

MM6.22.summary: City Council has officially recognized the long and important queer history and community presence at Hanlan's Point Beach. To ensure this legacy continues, the city will involve 2SLGBTQ+ community groups in planning for Toronto Island and establish an advisory group to help safeguard the area from discrimination and violence. This decision helps preserve the historical significance of Hanlan's Point and makes the space more inclusive and safe for everyone.

MM6.25.summary: Toronto City Council has officially proclaimed November 17 as "Gordon Lightfoot Day" in the city. This decision honours the late legendary Canadian singer-songwriter, recognizing his profound musical legacy and connection to Toronto.

MM6.30.summary: A proposal to redirect money from the sale of City-owned land at 276 Lauder Avenue was not discussed by City Council. Councillors voted against introducing the motion, meaning the decision on how to use these funds will be delayed or require a new proposal.

MM6.33.summary: City Council sent a proposal to the Executive Committee for further review, which aims to increase the diversity of non-councillor members appointed to the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) board. This initiative seeks to bring a wider range of perspectives to the leadership overseeing Toronto's public transit system.

MM6.35.summary: Toronto City Council has approved allocating $50,000 from developer contributions to rebuild the community garden at 133 Broadway Avenue. These funds, collected from a nearby development as a community benefit, will be given to Toronto Community Housing to create a better green space for residents.

MM6.41.summary: City Council has directed the City Solicitor to challenge a decision made by the Committee of Adjustment regarding development proposals for 73-83 Brooklawn Avenue. This action means the city will oppose certain plans for property changes, such as minor variances or subdivisions, to uphold local planning standards for residents in the Scarborough neighbourhood. The City Solicitor can also negotiate a settlement on the city's behalf.

DM6.4.summary: Toronto City Council turned down a proposal to study new options for the Gardiner Expressway East. Instead, councillors voted to request the provincial government take over all future construction and maintenance costs for both the Gardiner and the Don Valley Parkway, aiming to alleviate a significant financial burden on city taxpayers.

EX4.10.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new priority bus lanes and traffic signal changes on several major roads in Scarborough. These measures will help the new bus service run more smoothly and quickly as it replaces the Scarborough RT, aiming to improve transit reliability for residents in the area.

PH3.6.summary: City Council has approved confidential plans for new affordable housing projects at several sites, including locations on Dundas Street West, Wilson Heights Boulevard, and Merton Street, as part of its Housing Now initiative. The Council also adopted instructions conditional on the Province of Ontario fully reimbursing the city for financial losses caused by the Bill 23, "More Homes Built Faster Act." This decision aims to advance housing goals while protecting city finances from provincial policy changes.

RM6.1.summary: City Council opened its meeting by acknowledging the traditional territories of Indigenous Peoples and observing moments of silence for community members who passed away, including former Deputy Mayor Michael Feldman, and for the discovery of unmarked graves at a former residential school. This formal beginning sets the stage for Council's work, reflecting on important community and historical contexts.

DM6.2.summary: Toronto City Council has directed the City Manager to ask the TTC to consider reducing the fines for not paying transit fares. The goal is to make these tickets more in line with other city transportation fines, promoting fairness for all riders.

RM6.7.summary: City Council finalized its daily agendas for the May 10-12 meetings, determining the order in which items would be debated and decided. This procedural step ensured that important issues like housing options and climate action reports were scheduled for discussion, setting the stage for major decisions affecting Toronto residents.

EY4.9.summary: City Council has approved a new pedestrian crossing on Rockcliffe Boulevard, near Rockcliffe Court. This will create a safer way for pedestrians, especially students from the nearby middle school, to cross the street.

GG3.15.summary: City Council has approved an agreement allowing Conseil scolaire Viamonde, a French school board, to use a section of Felstead Park. This permits the school to use part of the park for its operations, while also requiring the school board to pay for improvements like new fencing and updated pathways for the entire park.

PH3.13.summary: Toronto City Council has moved to protect the property at 4888 Dundas Street West by initiating its designation under the Ontario Heritage Act. This action means the building is recognized for its cultural heritage value, ensuring its preservation and preventing significant changes or demolition without city approval.

IE3.3.summary: Toronto City Council has endorsed its TransformTO 2022 annual report, outlining progress towards achieving net-zero emissions. The city will advocate for federal and provincial funding to support its climate initiatives and update building standards to discourage the use of natural gas in new developments. These actions aim to reduce Toronto's carbon footprint and secure necessary resources for climate action.

EX4.2.summary: To boost accountability on climate goals, Toronto's City Council decided that Toronto Hydro's climate actions will be included in the city's annual climate reporting. This means residents will soon have access to a new dashboard showing how Toronto Hydro is contributing to the city's overall climate strategy.

PH3.16.summary: City Council has approved changes to city rules that now allow multiplexes (buildings with 2-4 units) to be built in all residential areas across Toronto. This decision aims to create more housing options and diverse living arrangements within existing neighbourhoods, helping address the city's housing shortage.

PH3.18.summary: Toronto's Planning and Housing Committee recently received an update on "Our Plan Toronto," an ongoing review of requests to change how certain employment lands across the city can be used. This process considers proposals to convert areas currently designated for businesses and jobs into other uses, like housing, impacting the city's future development. A previous set of over 30 conversion requests is still awaiting approval from the provincial government.

PA3.11.summary: The Toronto Parking Authority (TPA) has approved a payment to a company's receiver to resolve a complex issue regarding property tax refunds for a municipal car park. This payment untangles a situation where property taxes were previously refunded to the TPA, and ensures correct financial accounting for the city's assets.

PA3.5.summary: The Toronto Parking Authority Board recently reviewed a confidential report outlining its ongoing legal disputes for the first quarter of 2023. This report provides updates on various legal challenges related to parking operations, Bike Share Toronto, and property management, ensuring the Authority continues to manage its risks with suitable insurance and legal counsel.

GG3.16.summary: City Council has decided to restore cleaning hours for employees who clean city facilities, while also introducing new standardized cleaning methods that fit within the 2023 budget. This aims to ensure city buildings remain clean, address staffing impacts in collaboration with the union, and adapt to new office arrangements like shared desks as employees return to work.

GG3.1.summary: City officials have approved an additional $2 million for repairs to the William Inglis passenger vessel. This funding is needed due to unexpected severe damage found during drydocking, requiring extensive repairs to its hull, roof, and other critical components to ensure it remains safe and certified for operation by Transport Canada.

GG3.20.summary: A City committee has postponed a decision to declare city-owned land and water at Ontario Place as 'surplus,' a necessary step for a potential land exchange with the Province of Ontario. This means the City will not move forward with this part of the Ontario Place redevelopment until Council approves official plan changes and receives more information from the Province and Federal government.

HL3.3.summary: Toronto's Board of Health adopted recommendations from the Black Scientists’ Task Force on Vaccine Equity to address health inequities in Black communities. These actions include directing the Medical Officer of Health to integrate community-led research into health reporting and to explore establishing a permanent Black Health Task Force and ongoing mental health supports. The Board also requested the Province of Ontario and Ontario Health to collect race-based health data and tackle long-term COVID-19 effects impacting Black Torontonians.

HL3.1.summary: Toronto's public health board is developing a comprehensive strategy to address mental health and addictions across the city. This plan will include specific actions for substance use, integrate Indigenous perspectives, and consider the impact of social isolation. The board also calls on federal and provincial governments to increase support for mental health and harm reduction services, and to decriminalize simple drug possession.

TE4.13.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the necessary planning and zoning changes for a new development at 25 St. Mary Street. As part of this approval, the developer will provide a non-profit child care facility designed to accommodate at least 62 children, contributing a valuable service to the community.

TE4.36.summary: Toronto's local councillors have endorsed a set of guiding principles to shape the future redevelopment of Ontario Place. These principles will guide city planners in reviewing development applications, ensuring public access to the waterfront, enhancing green spaces, improving connections with Exhibition Place, and incorporating Indigenous placemaking.

EP3.5.summary: Exhibition Place has approved a five-year lease agreement, allowing Metrolinx to rent office space starting May 2023, with an option to extend. This lease helps Metrolinx align its operations with the Ontario Line project's completion and will generate revenue from City-owned land.

NY4.31.summary: Residents at 59 Palm Drive were denied permission to remove a healthy white spruce tree from their private property. The North York Community Council upheld the city's tree protection bylaws, which do not allow the removal of healthy and maintainable trees, prioritizing urban forest preservation.

NY4.35.summary: North York Community Council made a decision regarding an application to remove three private trees from a property. The Council denied the removal of two healthy spruce trees, but approved the removal of one pine tree. To protect the urban tree canopy, the homeowner is required to plant five new trees or pay money to the city instead.

CC5.4.summary: Toronto City Council has found Councillor Josh Matlow in violation of its Code of Conduct for criticizing city staff on social media. As a result, Council has reprimanded him and decided to suspend his pay for 10 days, starting May 1. This action holds elected officials accountable for their conduct when interacting with and speaking about City employees.

IE2.6.summary: Toronto City Council has updated the fees for Bike Share Toronto, affecting annual memberships, casual rides, and the cost of using e-bikes. These changes, effective April 3, 2023, also introduce new low-income options and allow installment payments to make the service more accessible to residents.

IE2.7.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan to study and design new bike lanes and pedestrian safety improvements along College Street, specifically between Lansdowne Avenue and Manning Avenue. This decision directs city staff to conduct public consultations with local residents and businesses, aiming to include the project in the city's 2025-2027 cycling network plan.

NY3.31.summary: Toronto City Council has formally objected to a federal plan that would reduce the number of voting areas in the city. Council believes this change would further reduce Toronto residents' representation in government, where they are already underrepresented. They also requested that the federal commission provide more public notice and opportunities for residents to give input on any proposed boundary changes.

DM5.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the creation of a new unit within Ombudsman Toronto, led by a Deputy Ombudsman for Housing. This unit will investigate systemic issues like housing discrimination and problems with the city's housing services, aiming to improve fairness and accessibility for residents.

CC5.6.summary: City Council has established a new Housing Rights Advisory Committee to provide advice on housing policy and independently monitor the city's housing programs. This committee will help ensure Toronto's housing initiatives are effective and advance residents' right to housing.

EX3.7.summary: Toronto City Council has made permanent the option for councillors and the public to participate electronically in all its meetings and those of its local boards. This decision aims to increase accessibility for both elected officials and residents, making it easier to engage in city governance. Additionally, the time allowed to achieve a quorum before meetings begin has been extended from 15 to 30 minutes.

CC5.2.summary: City Council updated rules on how Council Members can use city resources when they campaign for Mayor. Now, limits on using services like office staff or budgets will begin earlier once a Council Member announces their intent to run for Mayor, aiming to prevent taxpayer-funded resources from being used for election campaigns.

CC5.24.summary: Toronto City Council chose not to accept a request for directions regarding a settlement offer for a major development at 1-3 Concorde Gate and 10-12 Concorde Place. This means the city will continue to fight the developer's appeal at the Ontario Land Tribunal, affecting the future of this project and the surrounding community.

MM5.12.summary: City Council has formally objected to the provincial government's plan to close local courthouses in North York and Scarborough. Councillors are asking the Attorney General of Ontario to pause these closures until the new downtown courthouse's capacity is fully assessed, as consolidating services could make it harder for residents to access justice.

MM5.44.summary: Toronto City Council has approved an audit to examine the financial impacts of the provincial government's Bill 23. This audit will help the city understand how the new provincial housing law affects Toronto's finances and its ability to pay for new services and infrastructure related to growth.

CC5.3.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted a report from Ombudsman Toronto, directing city officials to implement its recommendations for improving how the city handles encampments. This decision aims to ensure residents in encampments receive proper social and health supports, improve communication, and prioritize their needs during clearing processes.

GG2.27.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to immediately pause any planned reductions in service hours at City Hall, Metro Hall, and all Civic Centres. This pause allows for a thorough review of how service changes would affect essential services, public access, safety, and wait times for residents before any final decisions are made.

AU1.7.summary: Toronto City Council is looking into ways to improve the city's tree maintenance services, including whether the City should provide these services directly instead of using contractors. This aims to ensure better care for urban trees across the city, with a report on options expected later this year.

CC5.11.summary: Toronto City Council has approved legal advice from its City Solicitor regarding a provincial order that demands a more extensive environmental assessment for a new multi-use trail. This decision will guide the city's response to the provincial requirement, which could impact the trail project's timeline and expenses for residents.

MM5.31.summary: Toronto City Council has urged the incoming Mayor to reject new powers granted by provincial Bill 3, which allow the Mayor to veto Council decisions. This move aims to protect Council's collective decision-making authority and ensure that hiring and firing of senior city staff remain with the non-political City Manager.

MM5.32.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a motion calling on the city's future mayor to publicly commit to protecting local decision-making. This means the mayor would not support major changes to how City Council operates or governs if they come from the provincial government, without first getting full support from City Council and Toronto residents.

BL5.1.summary: Toronto City Council passed a series of new city laws (by-laws) during its extended meeting. This included a significant decision to require a by-election for the Mayor's office, directly affecting the city's leadership. Council also formally approved other decisions made during the meeting, ensuring their legal validity.

MM5.5.summary: Toronto City Council has officially supported Montreal's call for a high-speed train system to connect major cities like Toronto and Quebec City. This initiative, advocated over a high-frequency train, aims to significantly cut travel times, offer more reliable service, and provide environmental and economic benefits for the entire region.

MM5.6.summary: City Council has postponed a decision on a motion seeking to compare and balance fines for Toronto Transit Commission fare evasion with parking fines. This discussion, focused on 'Fine Equity,' aims to ensure penalties are fair and consistent for residents across different types of infractions, and will be revisited at the May 2023 City Council meeting.

MM5.25.summary: To make Toronto's roads safer, the city will significantly expand its automated speed enforcement program. City staff are now tasked with exploring options to acquire 75 new speed cameras and will report back on the costs needed for the 2024 budget. This initiative aims to deter speeding and reduce traffic incidents across the city.

MM5.29.summary: City Council has decided to explore the creation of an Iranian Cultural District along Yonge Street in Willowdale, specifically between Steeles Avenue and Drewry Avenue. This initiative aims to recognize the area's vibrant Iranian culture and could lead to increased support for local businesses and cultural events once the city's cultural district criteria are established.

MM5.33.summary: A proposal to study new options for the Gardiner East expressway was put on hold by City Council. Council decided to postpone its discussion and vote on this motion until its May 2023 meeting, delaying any immediate decisions on potential changes or studies for the Gardiner.

MM5.45.summary: Toronto City Council has urged the provincial government to reverse its decision to end the Physician and Hospital Services for Uninsured Persons Program. This program provides essential healthcare to vulnerable residents who do not have health insurance, and its cancellation would impact many in the city.

EX3.13.summary: Toronto City Council has approved allocating up to $15 million from a reserve fund to improve safety on the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC). This funding will support a new strategy that focuses on community services, including mental health crisis response and expanding outreach teams, to address the root causes of violence and enhance rider safety. The Council also requested investigations into transit security in other cities and a plan to educate riders on reporting hate incidents.

RM5.1.summary: Toronto City Council formally opened its multi-day meeting, starting with O Canada and observing moments of silence to honour individuals and the Tseshaht First Nation. Council also acknowledged the traditional Indigenous territories on which the meeting was held, setting a respectful tone for its proceedings.

CC5.5.summary: Toronto City Council is calling on the federal and provincial governments to provide more support for refugee claimants arriving in the city. Council is asking for extended funding for shelter services, a coordinated plan for housing new arrivals across Ontario, and faster processing of refugee protection claims, which helps ensure Toronto can continue to offer necessary assistance and receive fair compensation.

RM5.7.summary: At its meetings in late March, City Council approved the official agenda, known as the Order Paper, for the days, with some changes to the order of business. This decision set the priorities and sequence for important discussions, including financial updates and major infrastructure projects.

EY3.8.summary: To make crossing safer, Toronto City Council has approved the installation of new traffic lights at the intersection of Islington Avenue and Prince George Drive/Ridgevalley Crescent in Etobicoke Centre. This decision aims to improve pedestrian protection for local residents.

SC3.20.summary: City Council has approved installing new traffic control signals at Morningside Avenue and Warnsworth Street. This decision aims to improve safety and manage traffic flow more effectively for everyone using this intersection in Scarborough.

CC5.22.summary: Toronto City Council has taken a stance in an upcoming land dispute, deciding to support a revised development plan for properties on Yonge Street near Gerrard. This plan allows for a tall building at 409-415 Yonge Street and, importantly, requires that any future tall building at 399-405 Yonge Street include a social housing program. This decision influences future building heights in the area and promotes the provision of affordable housing.

EX3.2.summary: Toronto City Council has endorsed a plan to strongly advocate to the Federal and Provincial governments for a new financial framework and additional revenue tools. The city highlighted a significant strain on its budget, with $1.1 billion in property tax revenue currently used to fund services that are the responsibility of other government levels. This push aims to secure Toronto's long-term financial health and ensure the city can adequately fund essential services for residents.

MM5.46.summary: Toronto City Council has postponed its discussion on an update to the 'Alcohol in Parks Pilot' until its meeting in May 2023. This delay means that any potential new rules allowing residents to drink alcohol in city parks will also be pushed back, impacting what's allowed for the upcoming summer season.

EX3.9.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to negotiate with the provincial government to cover an estimated $234 million in increased costs for the SmartTrack Stations Program. This aims to ensure that Toronto residents are not solely responsible for these cost overruns for new transit stations. The City also put a condition on a new station's construction, stating it won't proceed until the Province commits to paying its share.

PB4.3.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to designate the properties at 153 and 185 Eastern Avenue as heritage sites. These historic industrial buildings are valued for their role in Toronto's rail history, reflecting the West Don Lands' past as an important industrial centre and ensuring their preservation.

PB4.10.summary: The city approved adding 11 properties in the Charles Street West, Irwin Avenue, Isabella Street, St. Luke Lane, and Yonge Street areas to its Heritage Register. This decision means these buildings are recognized for their historical importance and will have protections against demolition or significant changes, helping preserve Toronto's heritage for everyone.

CA4.3.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the appointment of Nadine Spencer as a public member to the Toronto Police Services Board until November 2026. This decision helps ensure public oversight and community representation in the governance of the city's police services.

GG2.11.summary: To protect homes from flooding, City Council has authorized agreements with engineering firms CH2M Hill Canada Limited and Stantec Consulting Limited. This move secures professional engineering services for Phase 5 of the Basement Flooding Protection Program.

TE3.43.summary: Councillors from areas affected by the Ontario Line construction have created a new committee. This committee will gather public feedback on Metrolinx's plans and develop recommendations to ensure the project best serves residents and addresses concerns about transparency and community impact.

CC4.1.summary: Toronto City Council has set the property tax rates for 2023, including a 5.50% increase for residential properties. This decision outlines how property tax increases will apply to different property types and determines the total amount of property tax revenue the city will collect to fund essential services.

MPB4.1.summary: City Council has finalized Toronto's 2023 annual budget, outlining how the city will spend funds on services and infrastructure for the year. This adopted budget includes additional funding to expand programs for youth culture, rent assistance, homeless shelters, and various community initiatives.

BL4.1.summary: City Council formally approved several by-laws, including the annual property tax levy for 2023. These decisions also adjust the Municipal Accommodation Tax rate, update eligibility for property tax relief programs for low-income seniors and disabled persons, and modify administrative dates for the Vacant Home Tax. This ensures city operations are funded and revises various tax and service rules for residents.

HL1.6.summary: Toronto City Council has approved several measures to help people experiencing homelessness during cold weather. This includes calling on provincial and federal governments for more shelter space and refugee funding, and exploring new 24/7 drop-in and warming centres, potentially partnering with faith-based organizations. These actions aim to improve support and safety for vulnerable residents in the city.

IE1.5.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the annual closure of the F.G. Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway for the 'Bike for Brain Health' event on specific Sundays in June for the next three years (2023-2025). This will mean significant traffic impacts on those days, and the city has asked the TTC to avoid subway closures to help manage congestion. Event organizers will cover any city costs related to securing construction zones during the closures.

GG1.12.summary: Toronto City Council has approved updated Fair Wage Schedules, which define the minimum pay rates for workers on City-funded contracts across various industries. Effective March 1, 2023, these updates ensure fair compensation for contractors' employees and establish clear rules for how the new rates apply to current and upcoming city projects.
