IE1.4.summary: Toronto City Council has made the temporary ActiveTO cycling routes on Yonge Street and Bayview Avenue permanent. This decision means the bike lanes and multi-use trail installed in 2021 will remain in place, and the city will also study and implement improvements to address traffic and safety concerns on nearby residential streets.

CC3.1.summary: City Council decided to postpone a discussion about creating a new Housing Commissioner role within Ombudsman Toronto. Council requested more details from the Ombudsman on how this new function would operate and align with the city's ongoing efforts to ensure a right to housing for residents. This delay allows for further review of how best to address housing-related concerns.

EX2.5.summary: To boost transparency in future Toronto elections, City Council has approved a new rule for candidates. Candidates for Mayor and City Councillor will now be required to submit copies of all their campaign invoices along with their financial statements. This change provides residents with more detailed insight into how election campaigns are funded and where money is spent, promoting greater accountability.

PH1.9.summary: To address homelessness, Toronto City Council adopted a plan to create new affordable and supportive homes. This involves asking the provincial and federal governments to dedicate surplus land for housing, directing Toronto Community Housing to lease sites to the City, and developing a plan to evaluate and improve the city's supportive housing program. The city will also report on funding allocations from the federal Rapid Housing Initiative to advance new projects.

CA2.2.summary: City Council has appointed six new public members to the Board of Health, which oversees Toronto's public health services. These appointments also include school board trustees, ensuring the Board has the necessary leadership to guide important health initiatives for residents until November 2026.

BL3.1.summary: City Council formally approved a series of new by-laws, including those that confirm the decisions made at its recent meetings. This legal step ensures that all resolutions passed by Council are officially recognized and binding.

IA3.1.summary: City Council has sent an inquiry about the Gardiner East Project to the Infrastructure and Environment Committee for review. This means the committee will now discuss the project in more detail, ensuring ongoing consideration of this major infrastructure before any final decisions are made.

EC1.7.summary: Toronto City Council has extended the UrbanHensTO pilot project. This means residents currently registered in the program can continue to keep up to four hens for personal egg production on their property until May 31, 2023.

CC3.6.summary: City Council has approved a settlement regarding a proposed development at 5238 Dundas Street West and 28 Jopling Avenue South. This means the city is agreeing to a revised plan for new construction in this Etobicoke-Lakeshore neighbourhood, pending review by an independent tribunal and ensuring local infrastructure can handle the changes. This decision helps shape the type of development permitted in the community.

IE1.3.summary: The City Council has approved an extension for a construction staging area on Broadway Avenue, allowing the continued closure of a sidewalk and a portion of the curb lane until June 30, 2025. This means local residents, pedestrians, and drivers in the area will experience ongoing disruptions for an extended period, though the developer is directed to maintain safety, cleanliness, and clear communication.

MM3.5.summary: Toronto City Council recently decided to explore new ways to raise money for the city, asking for a detailed report by late 2023 on various revenue-generating options. This includes a comprehensive study on potentially implementing a new parking levy on commercial, paid, and unpaid spaces across the city. The goal is to find new funding sources for city services and transit, which could impact residents and businesses.

EC1.5.summary: To ensure continued support for residents experiencing homelessness, City Council has approved extending contracts for COVID-19 related shelter services until April 2024. This decision also involves increasing the value of some existing contracts to cover the ongoing costs of these vital services.

EX2.4.summary: City Council has updated its rules for the popular CaféTO program, making it easier for restaurants and cafés to set up outdoor patios in curb lanes. These changes remove a requirement for direct consent from neighbouring properties when patios want to extend their outdoor space in Business Improvement Areas, potentially leading to more flexible and expanded dining options.

SC2.1.summary: A developer has received approval from City Council to demolish 33 rental homes near Kingston Road and Birchcliff Avenue. This decision is conditional on the developer replacing all 33 units with new rental homes, with 32 offered at affordable rents and one at mid-range rent for at least 10 years. Current tenants will also receive a relocation plan, including financial assistance and the option to return to the new building.

TE2.65.summary: Toronto and East York Community Council has asked city staff to study allowing free one-hour parking on Fridays between 12 p.m. and 3 p.m. along parts of Parliament Street. This change aims to make parking easier for residents attending Friday prayers at local mosques, promoting equity by offering similar parking flexibility to what's provided for Sunday church services.

NY2.15.summary: North York Community Council denied a resident's request to remove a large, healthy bur oak tree at 20 Pinemore Crescent. This decision protects the city's urban forest by upholding bylaws that prevent the removal of maintainable trees on private property.

NY2.14.summary: North York Community Council has approved the removal of a large, healthy black walnut tree from a private property at 31 Norden Crescent. This decision goes against city staff recommendations, who argued the tree should be protected as it was only being removed to make space for a new swimming pool. This action impacts the local tree canopy and the city's guidelines for private tree protection.

BU3.1.summary: Toronto's Budget Committee recommended a 2023 budget for the Mayor to propose to City Council, which includes a 5.5% property tax increase for residents. This plan also dedicates over $615 million to various housing-related programs and extends the City Building Levy for 10 years to fund important infrastructure projects across the city.

HL1.4.summary: Toronto's Board of Health approved an update to the 2023 spending plan and services provided by Toronto Public Health. This decision makes public previously confidential information about proposed budget efficiencies and asks for a review of existing public health services, ensuring they reflect lessons learned from the COVID-19 pandemic.

HL1.1.summary: The Board of Health has elected Chris Moise to serve as its Chair for the year 2023. This decision is important because the Chair leads the Board in making key decisions about public health services and initiatives for Toronto residents.

BU2.1.summary: The Budget Committee has postponed its decision on the city's 2023 Capital and Operating Budgets, moving the discussion to a later date. This delay means the final plans for property taxes, city services, and major investments for the year are still pending. The committee also requested more information on various topics, including childcare centres, housing grants, and the financial impact of delaying climate initiatives.

EX1.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to implement the province's Bill 109, "More Homes for Everyone Act," which aims to speed up housing development approvals. The city will streamline its application processes, hire 150 new staff to handle faster timelines, and asked the province to delay mandatory fee refunds for missed deadlines. These increased staffing and operational costs will be covered by existing development application fees, not property taxes.

EX1.7.summary: Toronto City Council has formally requested that the federal government provide funding to help cover the city's 2022 budget shortfall. Council also decided to use 2023 property tax notices to inform residents across the city about whether this federal support is received, highlighting the financial impact of inter-governmental funding decisions.

CC2.3.summary: Toronto City Council responded to the Province's new "Strong Mayor" powers by requesting the Mayor to not use certain new authorities that would allow motions to pass with less than a majority vote. Council also asked the Province to give Toronto more local decision-making power on issues like housing and revenue, and to consult with the city on future governance changes. This item is about preserving Council's role in local democracy.

MM2.1.summary: Toronto City Council is demanding accountability and financial compensation from the Province and Metrolinx for the long-standing delays and disruptions caused by the Eglinton Crosstown LRT construction. They've requested an independent review and an explanation from Metrolinx officials regarding the project's issues and new completion timeline. The Council also called for Metrolinx and the Province to fund property tax rebates for affected residents, provide free TTC bus fares along Eglinton, and deploy traffic wardens to help alleviate the burden on communities.

MM2.5.summary: City Council has decided to drop its challenge against the approval of a bingo hall at 360 Evans Avenue. This means the city will no longer oppose the decision by the Committee of Adjustment to allow the bingo hall, making it more likely to proceed.

MM2.14.summary: Toronto City Council has directed city staff to investigate and propose solutions for improving safety on Bloor Street bike lanes during construction. This means staff will look into ways for cyclists and people using motorized wheelchairs to safely navigate construction zones without having to move into car traffic or onto pedestrian sidewalks, aiming to make bike lanes safer for everyone.

MM2.15.summary: City Council sent a proposal to the Executive Committee that aims to increase transparency in the 2022 election. The proposal suggests changes to the Contribution Rebate Program, which helps candidates get back some of their campaign donation costs, to make the process more open to the public.

EX1.1.summary: Toronto City Council approved an interim spending plan of $7.488 billion to keep essential city services running smoothly at the start of 2023. This temporary funding ensures city programs and agencies can continue operating without disruption until the full annual budget is finalized.

CC2.1.summary: Toronto City Council has directed city staff to create a "2023 Housing Action Plan" with the ambitious goal of building 285,000 new homes over the next decade. This plan will tackle housing shortages by updating zoning rules, reviewing city policies, and partnering with various organizations to increase housing options across all neighbourhoods.

PA1.6.summary: Toronto Parking Authority has approved a plan to secure a title sponsor for Bike Share Toronto. This partnership aims to generate new revenue and in-kind contributions, helping to offset operating and capital costs and improve the overall Bike Share service for residents.

PB1.4.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to protect the property at 51 Panorama Court under the Ontario Heritage Act. This action, which takes effect when the provincial government transfers the property to a new owner, ensures the historic former Hospital for Sick Children site is preserved for its cultural heritage value.

SC1.9.summary: Scarborough Community Council has approved a homeowner's request at 2 Amberjack Boulevard to keep their fence and hedges taller and denser than city bylaws typically allow. This decision was made due to safety concerns raised by the resident, highlighting how the city can make exceptions to property rules for specific circumstances.

TE1.6.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to oppose a proposed development at 415 Broadview Avenue, instructing city lawyers to argue against it at the provincial planning tribunal. This action ensures that any future development on the site adheres to city planning rules, addresses potential impacts on local services like water and traffic, and protects local heritage.

NY1.10.summary: North York Community Council approved a special request for a homeowner at 25 Ames Circle. This allows them to continue using their house wall as part of their pool enclosure, even though it doesn't fully meet city fence rules, provided the existing fence is kept in good repair. This decision addresses a specific property issue and indicates how similar exemption requests might be handled by the council in the future.

CC1.5.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to begin the process of officially designating the property at 415 Broadview Avenue as a heritage site. This action aims to protect the building's historical or architectural significance, ensuring its preservation for future generations. If there are no objections, the property will receive formal heritage status, limiting future alterations or demolition.

CC1.2.summary: Toronto City Council has formally asked the Province of Ontario to reverse parts of Bill 23, the More Homes Built Faster Act, 2022. The city is particularly concerned about proposed provincial changes that would reduce the funds collected from developers, which are used to pay for new infrastructure, services, and parks. Council also requested the province amend the Bill to preserve the city's green building standards and its ability to require affordable housing.

FM1.8.summary: Toronto City Council has elected its new leadership team for the term ending in November 2026. Councillor Frances Nunziata will serve as Speaker and Councillor Stephen Holyday as Deputy Speaker. This decision means they will preside over Council meetings, guiding discussions and ensuring proper procedure for important city matters.

EX29.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new strategy to tackle potholes across the city, aiming to make roads safer and smoother for residents. This plan includes an ongoing "pothole blitz" informed by data, exploring smart technology to detect road damage, and improving how residents can report potholes.

AU11.3.summary: City Council is moving to increase accountability and oversight for the SmartTrack transit program and future major city projects. They have asked Metrolinx to provide information for an audit by Toronto's Auditor General and requested the Auditor General of Ontario also audit the SmartTrack program. These measures aim to ensure taxpayer money is well-managed by including audit rights in future capital agreements where the city provides significant funding.

MM39.45.summary: Toronto is rethinking public space recycling by directing staff to consider replacing public recycling bins with litter-only bins, encouraging residents to take recyclables home. This change aims to reduce litter and improve recycling effectiveness, with a new strategy for public spaces due in 2027 that could include targeted high-tech bins and incentive programs. Additionally, the Council is urging the provincial government to reinstate public space recycling obligations and require retailers to manage packaging waste.

MM39.51.summary: City Council decided to seek permission to get involved in a Supreme Court of Canada appeal concerning Ontario Place. This action allows the city to join a legal challenge to protect the public's interest in the future of the iconic waterfront site.

MM39.53.summary: Toronto City Council officially recognized West Chinatown and the remaining strip of First Chinatown as a Cultural District, emphasizing its cultural importance. The City requested the provincial government to introduce commercial rent control and give local businesses and current tenants a first right to rent or purchase properties. These measures aim to protect the area's unique character and support its small businesses and residents.

MM39.54.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a plan to develop a process for naming future Toronto Island ferries. This means a report will be prepared by late 2027 outlining how new ferries will get their names, ensuring public input and consistency with existing city naming policies.

MM39.57.summary: Toronto City Council has approved $10,000 to fund a new heritage plaque commemorating the historic Bathhouse raids. This plaque, paid for by community contributions from a local development, will be installed at 231 Mutual Street to recognize an important event in the city's LGBTQ+ history.

MM39.60.summary: Toronto City Council has officially opposed any involvement of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) in the city, especially during the 2026 FIFA World Cup. This decision directs city staff to not assist ICE and asks federal and provincial governments to also reject the agency's presence in Toronto.

GG27.14.summary: Toronto City Council has approved new leases for various boat clubs situated along the city's waterfront. These renewed agreements will require the clubs to offer more public benefits, including greater community access to programs, affordable participation rates, or economical storage options for non-members. This initiative aims to make the waterfront more accessible for all Toronto residents.

IE27.7.summary: Toronto City Council has adopted an update to its Ravine Strategy, expanding efforts to protect and enhance the city's natural spaces. This includes permanently establishing a program for community-led restoration projects and allowing the city to accept donations for capital improvements to the Loop Trail. Council also approved joining 'Cities With Nature,' an international initiative focused on urban nature.

IE27.8.summary: City Council has endorsed a future reduction in the minimum size of trees protected on private property, changing from 30 cm to 20 cm in 2027, which could make it easier for residents to remove smaller trees. Alongside this, a pilot program will offer financial incentives to property owners for maintaining "distinctive" trees. These decisions also include updates to Toronto's tree and ravine protection by-laws.

MM39.87.summary: Toronto City Council officially declared March 2026 as Persian Heritage Month, recognizing the cultural contributions of Iranian-Canadians and expressing solidarity with the community. To further acknowledge their pursuit of freedom and human dignity, the Toronto sign in Nathan Phillips Square will be lit in green, white, and red during April.

CC39.10.summary: Toronto City Council has agreed to a settlement offer for a development proposal at 895 Lawrence Avenue East, directing the City Solicitor to support it at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This decision sets out the city's position on the project and requires the developer to cover all costs for necessary engineering reports before the Tribunal issues its final order.

TE29.12.summary: Toronto City Council has approved changes to the Official Plan and Zoning By-law for properties at 1684, 1698, 1700, and 1702 Queen Street East. This decision allows a new development to proceed, with the requirement that the owner include three affordable rental housing units as part of the project.

MM39.86.summary: City Council has directed city staff to review existing rules for loading spaces, which are used for deliveries and waste collection, specifically for housing projects that include a mix of different living arrangements. This initiative aims to ensure these rules are appropriate for diverse housing types, potentially supporting the development of more varied housing options across Toronto.

MM39.36.summary: Toronto City Council is addressing an urgent parking shortage at three seniors' living facilities on Cummer Avenue. Council has directed city staff to conduct a detailed study of current and future parking needs, including public transit options, and to explore solutions like better enforcement and adding more parking capacity. This move aims to improve accessibility for residents, visitors, and staff at Cummer Lodge, Willowdale Manor, and Seniors Supportive Housing.

MM39.50.summary: To boost safety and confidence for TTC subway riders, City Council has approved a plan to develop a strategy for deploying uniformed police officers in all stations. The plan also commits to installing platform edge doors, with a multi-station pilot project beginning by late 2027, and calls for improved cleanliness standards, updated lighting, and safety design audits across the subway system.

MM39.59.summary: Toronto City Council has formally requested the provincial and federal governments to include local residents and their elected officials in all major decisions about the city's waterfront. This decision opposes any unilateral government land seizures and directs city lawyers to explore legal options to protect Toronto's ownership of critical waterfront properties, including the Billy Bishop Airport lands.

CC39.1.summary: Toronto City Council recently reviewed a report from the Integrity Commissioner concerning comments made by Councillor Chris Moise to a constituent. To promote professional conduct and responsible communication, Council also requested that the Integrity Commissioner provide guidance to all councillors on how to properly respond to hateful, discriminatory, or harassing speech.

EX29.14.summary: Toronto City Council is stepping up efforts to hold negligent landlords accountable. They've approved creating a public database to track rental property complaints and will coordinate city divisions to proactively investigate all properties owned by repeat offenders. This initiative aims to improve living conditions and ensure proper maintenance for renters across the city.

IE12.4.summary: Toronto City Council has approved the installation of new bike lanes and cycling infrastructure on several streets across the city. This expansion of the cycling network aims to provide safer and more convenient travel options for residents in multiple neighbourhoods. The General Manager of Transportation Services was also given authority to manage these projects as they are implemented.

MM13.17.summary: City Council has postponed its discussion and decision on how legislative meeting materials are distributed. Residents interested in changes to how city documents are shared will need to wait until the February 2024 Council meeting for this topic to be addressed.

CC13.2.summary: Toronto City Council has endorsed a "New Deal" agreement with the Province of Ontario, designed to strengthen the city's finances and improve services. This partnership outlines significant provincial funding for shelters and new Line 2 subway trains, contingent on the federal government providing matching funds. It matters to residents as it could bring substantial investments in critical city services and transit.

NY8.10.summary: City Council has approved zoning changes for a new development at 48 Grenoble Drive, allowing for the demolition of 109 existing rental units. This decision requires the developer to build at least 110 replacement rental units on the site for 20 years and create a new publicly accessible outdoor space for the community.

CC11.1.summary: Toronto City Council is advocating for a Canada-wide student nutrition program. They are urging the federal government to allocate funds in the 2024 budget to establish a national program that would help more children in Toronto and across Canada receive healthy food at school.

IE6.3.summary: Toronto City Council has decided that the city will stop collecting blue bin recycling materials from homes and facilities starting January 1, 2026, as the province takes over this service through a new program. The City plans to sell its existing recycling carts, and the funds will help keep solid waste utility rates stable for residents.

MM11.22.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to extend the Alcohol in Parks pilot program, allowing residents to continue legally drinking in designated parks until at least March 31, 2024. This extension gives staff more time to evaluate the program and potentially adjust the list of participating parks or add new safety rules for the winter season.

MM39.27.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a pilot project to open four city-run grocery stores, with one in each Community Council district. These stores will operate as not-for-profits to provide affordable food options, especially in neighborhoods with limited access to full-service grocery stores and lower incomes.

MM39.35.summary: Toronto City Council has decided to stop flying flags of foreign nations, or flags depicting them, on courtesy poles at City Hall and civic centres, effective December 1, 2026. This change alters how the city officially recognizes diverse communities through flag displays and will lead to a new review for a broader community recognition program.

MM39.42.summary: Responding to community concerns about local wildlife, City Council will assess changes to the mute swan population management program in Etobicoke-Lakeshore, including studying the feasibility of leaving some viable eggs in nests. Council also requested the federal government to review the scientific basis for mute swan population control, which could impact future wildlife management policies in Toronto.

EX29.13.summary: Toronto City Council has approved creating a new paid program for sidewalk snow shovelling. This program will bring in extra help during heavy winter snowfalls, drawing on successful models from cities like New York and Montreal, to ensure sidewalks are cleared for residents by the 2026-2027 winter season.

IE22.4.summary: Toronto City Council adopted a series of updates to its Vision Zero road safety plan, introducing new policies for deciding where to install pedestrian crossings and traffic lights. The changes also include adding traffic signals at 15 intersections across the city, updating road classifications, and lowering speed limits on specific streets like parts of Forest Manor Road and Avenue Road. These initiatives are designed to improve safety for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers, helping to reduce collisions and serious injuries on Toronto's streets.

SC23.2.summary: City Council has updated the Official Plan and Zoning By-law for the properties located at 672-676 Birchmount Road in Scarborough Southwest. This decision permits new development at these sites, which could lead to changes in the type or density of buildings in the area.

EX23.2.summary: Toronto City Council has approved transferring ownership of City-owned lands along the Eglinton Crosstown West Extension to Metrolinx or another provincial agency. This supports the creation of Transit-Oriented Communities, which are new developments built near transit to encourage ridership and urban growth. Council has also requested the Province hold public consultations to get community input on their plans for these lands.

IE7.3.summary: Toronto City Council has doubled the maximum number of permits for free-floating car-share companies, raising it from 500 to 1000 per company. This decision aims to expand access to car-sharing services for residents across the city, with a focus on encouraging more electric and non-gas powered vehicles.

MM11.36.summary: A proposal to develop a mechanical vacuum leaf collection program for 2024 was not approved by Toronto City Council. This means the city will not be moving forward with plans to use specialized equipment for leaf pickup, impacting how leaves are managed in neighborhoods next year.

EX29.1.summary: Toronto City Council has approved accepting a grant of over $1.6 million from Bloomberg Philanthropies. This funding will be used to develop and implement a universal school food program, aiming to provide nutritious meals for students across the city.

CC39.9.summary: City Council has agreed to a settlement offer regarding a proposed development at 444-468 Eglinton Avenue West, directing the City Solicitor to support this plan at the Ontario Land Tribunal. This decision will shape the future of this site, allowing for new construction once the developer meets specific conditions related to design, engineering, and transportation.

PH29.4.summary: To improve living conditions in Toronto apartment buildings, City Council has updated fees related to its RentSafeTO program. Building owners will now pay a new, small fee to re-issue a colour-coded sign and face higher charges if the City needs to fix problems on their property, encouraging better maintenance and accountability.

PH29.6.summary: City Council is preparing to challenge a developer's proposal to change how land can be used at 105-109 Vanderhoof Avenue and 10 Brentcliffe Road. The developer wants to allow mixed-use development instead of current employment-only zoning, and the City Solicitor will present the city's opposition at the provincial land tribunal while also trying to reach a compromise.

EC27.2.summary: Toronto City Council has updated its rules for street vendors and artists, allowing mobile food vendors to operate for up to 12 hours and lifting a ban on new food permits in Wards 10, 11, and 13. This move modernizes street vending regulations across the city, though older curb lane vending permits will be phased out by January 2029.

EC27.6.summary: Toronto City Council has approved a confidential plan to acquire a new property for the Applegrove Community Complex. This decision will allow the complex to relocate and continue providing important community services in the Toronto-Danforth area. The City will also seek external funding, likely from the provincial government, to help cover future renovation and setup costs.
